Low-expansion foam has high density and is suitable for outdoor hydrocarbon fires; high-expansion foam has very low density and is used in enclosed spaces to flood large volumes rapidly.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A circular bunded area of diameter 18 m containing a pool of aviation fuel is to be protected by a medium-expansion foam system.**

Given:
- Application density $q = 8 \text{ L/min/m}^2$
- Proportioning ratio $R = 6\%$
- Discharge duration $t_d = 65 \text{ min}$
- Expansion ratio $E = 30$
- Safety reserve factor: **3× concentrate storage**

Compute:
- **(a) [7 marks]** The foam solution flow rate, concentrate flow rate, and water flow rate.
- **(b) [7 marks]** The total concentrate volume required, the finished foam flow rate, and the storage tank capacity.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Compute protected area $A$:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (18)^2}{4} = \frac{324\pi}{4} = 81\pi = 254.47 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Foam solution flow rate $Q_s$:**

$$Q_s = q \times A = 8 \times 254.47 = 2035.75 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 3 — Concentrate flow rate $Q_c$:**

$$Q_c = Q_s \times \frac{R}{100} = 2035.75 \times \frac{6}{100} = 122.145 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 4 — Water flow rate $Q_w$:**

$$Q_w = Q_s - Q_c = 2035.75 - 122.145 = 1913.61 \text{ L/min}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 5 — Total concentrate volume for one discharge:**

$$V_{c,\text{total}} = Q_c \times t_d = 122.145 \times 65 = 7939.43 \text{ L}$$

**Step 6 — Finished foam flow rate $Q_f$:**

$$Q_f = Q_s \times E = 2035.75 \times 30 = 61072.5 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 7 — Storage tank capacity with 3× reserve:**

$$V_{\text{storage}} = 3 \times V_{c,\text{total}} = 3 \times 7939.43 = 23818.29 \text{ L}$$

$$V_{\text{storage}} \approx 23.82 \text{ m}^3$$

#### Final Answer Table

| Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $Q_s$ | **2035.75** | L/min |
| $Q_c$ | **122.15** | L/min |
| $Q_w$ | **1913.61** | L/min |
| $V_{c,\text{total}}$ | **7939.43** | L |
| $Q_f$ | **61072.5** | L/min |
| $V_{\text{storage}}$ | **23818.29** | L (≈ 23.82 m³) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the **components and working principle of a Balanced Pressure Proportioning Foam System**.

**(b) [7 marks]** A high-expansion foam generator is rated to deliver finished foam at a flow rate of **$24000 \text{ L/min}$** at an expansion ratio of **$E = 400$**. If the proportioning ratio is **3%**, calculate:
- (i) Foam solution flow rate
- (ii) Concentrate flow rate
- (iii) Water flow rate

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

A **Balanced Pressure Proportioning (BPP) System** operates on the principle of **pressure equilibrium** between the water line and the concentrate line.

**Components:**
1. Water supply line with main control valve
2. Foam concentrate storage tank
3. **Concentrate pump** with pressure-sensing line
4. **Balancing valve** (the heart of the system) — automatically maintains equal pressure between water and concentrate sides
5. Mixing chamber / proportioner body
6. Solution piping and discharge devices (Type II or Type III)
7. Pressure gauges and control instrumentation

**Working Principle:**
- A sensing line transmits the water-line pressure to the balancing valve.
- The balancing valve throttles the concentrate return line to keep the **concentrate line pressure equal to the water line pressure**.
- Under this pressure-equilibrium condition, the proportioning valve (orifice) draws concentrate at the **exact designed ratio $R$** irrespective of flow variation.
- This makes BPP the **most accurate proportioner** for systems with **varying flow demands**.

> The defining advantage of BPP is its **ratio accuracy across a wide flow range**, making it the preferred choice for large refinery and offshore installations.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $Q_f = 24000 \text{ L/min}$
- $E = 400$
- $R = 3\%$

**Step 1 — Foam solution flow rate $Q_s$:**

$$Q_s = \frac{Q_f}{E} = \frac{24000}{400} = 60 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 2 — Concentrate flow rate $Q_c$:**

$$Q_c = Q_s \times \frac{R}{100} = 60 \times \frac{3}{100} = 1.8 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 3 — Water flow rate $Q_w$:**

$$Q_w = Q_s - Q_c = 60 - 1.8 = 58.2 \text{ L/min}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $Q_f$ with $Q_s$:** Students frequently substitute $Q_f$ directly into the concentrate equation. Always check whether the given flow is *finished foam* or *solution* and apply Equation 5 in reverse ($Q_s = Q_f / E$) when needed.
> 2. **Unit conversion:** The KTU board penalizes missing units in the final answer. Always append $\text{L/min}$, $\text{L}$, or $\text{m}^3$.
> 3. **Skipping the formula statement:** Writing only the numerical answer without the governing equation costs at least **1 to 2 marks** per sub-part.
> 4. **Misapplying expansion ratio:** Some students mistakenly multiply instead of divide when reversing Equation 5. Memorize: *expansion ratio enlarges volume, so finished foam > solution in volume.*
> 5. **Ignoring the safety reserve factor:** When the problem mentions a *2×* or *3×* reserve, students often forget to apply it to the concentrate storage capacity, losing 2–3 marks on the final part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Foam = Water + Concentrate + Air.** Never forget the three-component nature of foam.
- **The three suppression mechanisms** of foam are: **smothering**, **cooling**, and **vapor sealing** — all three must be stated for full marks.
- **The five-stage cascade** of foam operation: *Detection → Water Flow → Proportioning → Aspiration → Discharge.*
- **Mixing ratio $R$** is the volumetric % of concentrate in the solution; common values are **1%, 3%, and 6%**.
- **Expansion ratio $E$** classifies foams: **< 20 (low), 20–200 (medium), > 200 (high)**.
- **Memorize the five core equations:**
  - $Q_s = q \times A$
  - $Q_c = Q_s \times R / 100$
  - $Q_w = Q_s - Q_c$
  - $E = V_f / V_s$ and $Q_f = E \times Q_s$
  - $V_{c,\text{total}} = Q_c \times t_d$
- **AR-AFFF** is required for **polar solvents** (alcohols, ketones); ordinary AFFF fails on these because the solvent destroys the foam blanket.
- **Class B hazards only** — foam is *not* the answer for Class C or Class D fires.
- **Storage sizing always includes a safety reserve** — typically **2×** or **3×** the calculated single-discharge volume.
- **BPP (Balanced Pressure Proportioner)** is the most accurate proportioner for variable-flow installations.
- **Always state the formula before substituting** in numerical answers — secures partial credit even if the arithmetic fails.
- **KTU marker reward:** Diagrams and tables in answers consistently score 1–2 extra marks versus text-only responses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Revision Mantra for KTU Foam Systems:**
> *"Three legs smother, cool, and seal — concentrate meters, water flows, foam reveals. Ratio in, expansion out, reserve without doubt."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Halon System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET832 - OCCUPATIONAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Halon System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Halon System** is a *clean agent* gaseous fire suppression system that uses halogenated hydrocarbon compounds (halons) — primarily **Halon 1301 (Bromotrifluoromethane, CBrF₃)** and **Halon 1211 (Bromochlorodifluoromethane, CBrClF₂)** — to extinguish fires by chemically interrupting the combustion chain reaction rather than by cooling, smothering, or fuel removal.

According to **NFPA 12A** (Standard on Halon 1301 Fire Extinguishing Systems) and **NFPA 12B** (Standard on Halon 1211 Fire Extinguishing Systems), these systems are classified as **Total Flooding Systems** or **Local Application Systems** for the protection of high-value assets where water damage from sprinklers would be unacceptable (e.g., data centers, control rooms, museums, aircraft engine nacelles, marine machinery spaces, and military vehicles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):** Halons are the benchmark *chemical* fire suppression agents. Although production was banned under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** for new installations, the study of halon systems remains mandatory in the KTU curriculum because (a) existing halon systems are still legally serviced and maintained, (b) modern **Halon Replacements (HFCs, HCFCs, Inert Gases, FK-5-1-12)** are designed and tested using halon-based engineering principles, and (c) aviation, military, and offshore installations still operate legacy halon systems under essential-use exemptions.

## 1.2 The Halon Family — Identification Numbers

The halon numbering convention is a 4-digit code: **Halon ABCD**

- **A** = number of Carbon atoms
- **B** = number of Fluorine atoms
- **C** = number of Chlorine atoms
- **D** = number of Bromine atoms
- (Hydrogen atoms are implicit)

| Halon | Chemical Name | Chemical Formula | Number Breakdown | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Halon 1301** | Bromotrifluoromethane | CBrF₃ | 1 C, 3 F, 0 Cl, 1 Br | Total flooding (occupied spaces) |
| **Halon 1211** | Bromochlorodifluoromethane | CBrClF₂ | 1 C, 2 F, 1 Cl, 1 Br | Portable extinguishers, local application |
| **Halon 2402** | Dibromotetrafluoroethane | C₂Br₂F₄ | 2 C, 4 F, 0 Cl, 2 Br | Military, marine, obsolete |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Tiny Chemistry Bully"

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Chemistry Bully at the Dance":**
> Imagine a fire as a high-school dance where fuel, oxygen, and heat keep passing *energy partners* around in a continuous chain reaction. **Halon 1301** behaves like a *chemistry bully that crashes the dance* — it does not put out the music (cooling), nor does it unplug the speakers (smothering). Instead, it grabs the *OH• and H• free radicals* (the "energy dance partners") and forms stable, non-reactive molecules (HBr, HF) that can no longer pass the energy around. Without the chain carriers, the fire simply **runs out of partners to dance with** and self-extinguishes within seconds. This is called *chemical (or catalytic) flame inhibition*.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Halon 1301 (CBrF₃) | Halon 1211 (CBrClF₂) |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular Weight | **148.9 g/mol** | **165.4 g/mol** |
| Boiling Point (1 atm) | **−57.8 °C** | **−3.4 °C** |
| Liquid Density (20 °C) | **1,580 kg/m³** | **1,830 kg/m³** |
| Vapour Density (20 °C, 1 atm) | **6.29 kg/m³** | **7.14 kg/m³** |
| ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential) | **10.0** (Reference = CFC-11) | **3.0** |
| GWP₁₀₀ (Global Warming Potential) | **6,900** | **1,890** |
| Atmospheric Lifetime | **65 years** | **16 years** |
| NOAEL (Occupied Space Safety Limit) | **5 % by volume** | **0.5 % by volume** |
| LOAEL (Lowest Observable Adverse Effect Level) | **7.5 % by volume** | **1.0 % by volume** |
| Extinguishing Concentration (n-Heptane) | **3.7 % – 5.0 %** | **4.2 % – 5.8 %** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Toxicity (KTU 2024):** The narrow margin between the **extinguishing concentration (~5 %)** and the **LOAEL (7.5 %)** of Halon 1301 is a *first-class engineering safety concern*. A minor design error in agent quantity can convert a fire-suppression event into a *cardiac sensitisation hazard* (Halon 1301 is a known cardiac sensitiser at concentrations above 7.5 %). This is why halon systems are designed with a **safety factor of 0 % to 5 %** above the *minimum design concentration*, but never exceeding NOAEL for occupied spaces.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Fire Suppression — Why Halons Work

The combustion of hydrocarbons follows the classical **H–O–OH radical chain mechanism**. For a generic hydrocarbon fuel (RH):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 (Initiation):} \quad & RH + O_2 \rightarrow R\bullet + HO_2\bullet \\
\text{Step 2 (Propagation):} \quad & OH\bullet + CO \rightarrow CO_2 + H\bullet \\
\text{Step 3 (Propagation):} \quad & H\bullet + O_2 \rightarrow OH\bullet + O\bullet \\
\text{Step 4 (Branching):} \quad & O\bullet + H_2 \rightarrow OH\bullet + H\bullet
\end{aligned}
$$

The chain carriers **H•, O•, and OH•** sustain flame propagation. **Halon 1301** interferes chemically by donating its **bromine (Br) radical** to the flame zone:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CBrF}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} & \text{CF}_3\bullet + \text{Br}\bullet \\
\text{Br}\bullet + \text{RH} \rightarrow & \text{HBr} + \text{R}\bullet \\
\text{HBr} + \text{OH}\bullet \rightarrow & \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Br}\bullet
\end{aligned}
$$

The bromine radical is **regenerated**, making halon a *catalytic inhibitor*. The free radical **HBr** scavenges the OH• radical, breaking the chain reaction. This is why halons extinguish **Class A, B, and C fires** at extremely low concentrations (3–5 %) compared to CO₂ (34 %) or water mist.

## 2.2 Components of a Halon 1301 Total Flooding System

A standard KTU-referenced engineering drawing would show the following architecture (mapped in §4):

1. **Agent Storage Cylinders** (seamless steel, DOT-3AL/TC-3ALM, super-pressurised with dry nitrogen to 25 bar at 21 °C or 42 bar for super-pressurised variants)
2. **Master/Reserve Bank** (100 % redundancy, with selector valve)
3. **Cylinder Actuation Heads** (solenoid, pneumatic, or manual)
4. **Discharge Nozzles** (radial or fan-spray, orifice-sized to deliver mass flow within **10 seconds** per NFPA 12A)
5. **Distribution Piping Network** (schedule 40/80 steel, hydraulically calculated)
6. **Fire Detection & Actuation Chain** (smoke/heat/UV/IR detectors → control panel → cylinder release)
7. **Enclosure Integrity Test** (door-fan pressurisation test ≤ **0.04 m³/min per m²** of leakage area)
8. **Warning Signs, Alarms, Pneumatic Pre-Discharge Alarm (30 s delay), Abort Switch, Time-Delay Relay**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip (KTU 2024 Valuation Key):** For numerical problems, examiners expect you to clearly state the *boundary condition* (enclosure volume, fuel type, design concentration, NOAEL safety limit) **before** substituting values. Marks are routinely lost when students skip the unit declaration.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Units (SI) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **F1** | $M = \dfrac{V \cdot C \cdot \rho_v}{1 - C}$ | M = mass of Halon 1301, V = enclosure volume, C = design concentration (decimal), ρᵥ = vapour density at 21 °C | kg, m³, –, kg/m³ | Total flooding agent quantity |
| **F2** | $\rho_v = \dfrac{P \cdot M_w}{R \cdot T}$ | P = storage pressure (Pa), M_w = molecular weight (kg/kmol), R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K), T = temperature (K) | kg/m³ | Vapour density from ideal gas law |
| **F3** | $C_{min} = C_{cup} \times SF$ | C_cup = cup-burner value (fuel-specific), SF = safety factor (1.20 for Class B, 1.30 for Class A deep-seated) | % by volume | Minimum design concentration |
| **F4** | $V_{eff} = V_{gross} - V_{displaced}$ | V_displaced = volume of fixed non-removable objects inside enclosure | m³ | Effective flooding volume |
| **F5** | $t_{hold} = \dfrac{V_{leak} \cdot t_{req}}{V_{gross}}$ | V_leak = measured leakage from door-fan test, t_req = required hold time (typically ≥ 10 min for NFPA 12A) | min | Hold-time / retention verification |
| **F6** | $ODP = \dfrac{\text{Mass of O}_3 \text{ destroyed by 1 kg gas}}{\text{Mass of O}_3 \text{ destroyed by 1 kg CFC-11}}$ | Dimensionless, CFC-11 = 1.0 | – | Environmental impact comparison |
| **F7** | $C_{ext}(\text{Halon 1301, n-Heptane}) \approx 0.037 \text{ to } 0.050$ | Empirical cup-burner value | volume fraction | Baseline reference value |
| **F8** | $t_{discharge} \leq 10 \text{ s}$ | Per NFPA 12A | seconds | Maximum allowable discharge time |
| **F9** | $P_{min,hold} = P_{atm} + 0.05 \text{ bar (enclosure integrity)}$ | Pressure retention | bar (gauge) | Enclosure integrity threshold |
| **F10** | $C_{NOAEL,1301} = 0.05$, $C_{LOAEL,1301} = 0.075$ | Toxicological exposure limits | volume fraction | Occupied-space design safety boundary |

## 2.4 Why Halon Was Phased Out — The Engineering Trade-off

The **Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer (1987)** classified halons as **Class I controlled substances** due to:

- High **ODP** (Halon 1301: 10× worse than CFC-11)
- Long **atmospheric lifetime** (65 years for Halon 1301)
- **Bromine** is ~40–60× more efficient at stratospheric ozone destruction than chlorine

The **Kigali Amendment (2016)** further targeted HFC replacements due to high GWP. This regulatory pressure forced the development of *Halon Alternatives*:

| Agent | Type | ODP | GWP₁₀₀ | NOAEL | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FM-200 (HFC-227ea)** | HFC | 0 | 2,050 | 9.0 % | Data centers |
| **Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12)** | Fluoroketone | 0 | <1 | 10.0 % | Archives, museums |
| **Inergen (IG-541)** | Inert gas (N₂/Ar/CO₂) | 0 | 0 | 43 % | Occupied spaces |
| **Argonite (IG-55)** | Inert gas | 0 | 0 | 43 % | Marine, offshore |
| **CO₂ (high pressure)** | Inert gas | 0 | 1 | LOAEL 6–8 % | Unoccupied enclosures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility (KTU 2024):** Despite phase-out, halon recovery, recycling, and re-deployment is governed by **NFPA 12A Annex A** and **EPA Significant New Alternatives Policy (SNAP)**. The halon bank concept (recovered halon reclaimed from decommissioned systems) is still a critical skill for fire protection engineers working with legacy aviation, military, and oil & gas installations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mass of Halon 1301 Required for Total Flooding

**Problem Statement:** A server room has internal dimensions **Length = 12 m, Width = 8 m, Height = 3.5 m**. The fire hazard is n-Heptane liquid (Class B). Determine the mass of Halon 1301 required, given that the design concentration must satisfy both **fire suppression (≥ 5.0 %)** and **occupational safety (≤ 5.0 % NOAEL)**. Use ρᵥ (Halon 1301 at 21 °C) = **6.29 kg/m³**.

### Step-by-Step Model Solution (KTU Valuation Style)

**Step 1 — Compute gross enclosure volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{gross} &= L \times W \times H \\
&= 12 \,\text{m} \times 8 \,\text{m} \times 3.5 \,\text{m} \\
&= 336 \,\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute effective (net) volume assuming non-displaceable equipment occupies 15 m³ (server racks, cable trays).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{eff} &= V_{gross} - V_{displaced} \\
&= 336 - 15 \\
&= 321 \,\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Select design concentration.**

For n-Heptane (Class B), NFPA 12A specifies a cup-burner value of **3.7 %**. Applying a safety factor SF = 1.20:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{min} &= C_{cup} \times SF \\
&= 0.037 \times 1.20 \\
&= 0.0444 \text{ (i.e., 4.44 %)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify against NOAEL.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{design} &= \max(C_{min},\, C_{NFPA\,recommended}) = 5.0 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the **fire-engineering minimum (4.44 %)** is below NOAEL, we adopt the **NFPA-recommended design concentration of 5.0 %** for an extra margin against deep-seated or shielded fires. We must verify that $C_{design} = 0.050 \le 0.050$ (NOAEL) — **boundary satisfied exactly**.

**Step 5 — Apply the flooding mass formula (F1).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \frac{V_{eff} \cdot C \cdot \rho_v}{1 - C} \\
&= \frac{321 \times 0.050 \times 6.29}{1 - 0.050} \\
&= \frac{321 \times 0.3145}{0.950} \\
&= \frac{100.9545}{0.950} \\
&= 106.27 \,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Add 10 % margin for piping residual (NFPA 12A allowance) and round up to nearest standard cylinder fill.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{final} &= 106.27 \times 1.10 \\
&= 116.90 \,\text{kg} \\
&\approx 117 \,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** A total of **117 kg of Halon 1301** must be stored, with a **100 % reserve bank** (an additional 117 kg cylinder bank) per NFPA 12A for critical facilities. Total on-site storage: **234 kg**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Enclosure Leakage / Hold-Time Verification

**Problem Statement:** During a door-fan pressurisation test, the server room leaks **0.5 m³/min** at 5 Pa. The required agent hold-time per the AHJ (Authority Having Jurisdiction) is **15 minutes**. Will the system maintain extinguishing concentration?

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Express the total leak volume over 15 minutes.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{leak,total} &= \dot{V}_{leak} \times t_{hold} \\
&= 0.5 \,\tfrac{\text{m}^3}{\text{min}} \times 15 \,\text{min} \\
&= 7.5 \,\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the leakage as a percentage of gross volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%_{leak} &= \frac{V_{leak,total}}{V_{gross}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{7.5}{336} \times 100 \\
&= 2.23 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the NFPA 12A equivalence test criterion.**

NFPA 12A requires that the **measured concentration at the end of the hold period** be ≥ the **minimum design concentration (4.44 %)**. The decay of halon concentration due to uniform leakage is exponential:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(t) = C_0 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{\dot{V}_{leak}}{V_{gross}} \cdot t\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(15) &= 0.050 \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{0.5}{336} \times 15\right) \\
&= 0.050 \cdot \exp(-0.02232) \\
&= 0.050 \times 0.97793 \\
&= 0.04890 \text{ (i.e., 4.89 %)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to minimum design concentration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(15) = 0.0489 \;\geq\; C_{min} = 0.0444 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The enclosure **PASSES** the hold-time verification. The concentration decays only from 5.00 % to 4.89 % over 15 minutes, well above the 4.44 % threshold. If it had failed, the engineer would have to either (a) reduce leakage by sealing penetrations, (b) increase initial concentration, or (c) extend the hold time.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Vapour Density Confirmation (Ideal Gas Law Cross-Check)

**Problem Statement:** Verify the published vapour density of Halon 1301 at 21 °C and 1 atm using the ideal gas law.

### Step-by-Step Model Solution

**Step 1 — Convert units to SI.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 21\,°C + 273.15 = 294.15 \,\text{K} \\
P &= 101{,}325 \,\text{Pa} \\
M_w &= 148.9 \,\text{kg/kmol} \\
R &= 8.314 \,\text{kJ/(kmol·K)} = 8{,}314 \,\text{J/(kmol·K)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply ideal gas law formula (F2).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_v &= \frac{P \cdot M_w}{R \cdot T} \\
&= \frac{101{,}325 \times 148.9}{8{,}314 \times 294.15} \\
&= \frac{15{,}087{,}292.5}{2{,}445{,}602.1} \\
&= 6.169 \,\text{kg/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare to manufacturer data.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{v,\,published} = 6.29 \,\text{kg/m}^3 \\
\%_{error} = \frac{\lvert 6.29 - 6.169 \rvert}{6.29} \times 100 = 1.92 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The ~2 % deviation is acceptable because the **ideal gas assumption neglects the non-ideal compressibility factor (Z ≈ 0.98) for CBrF₃ at 1 atm**. Using $\rho_v = \dfrac{P \cdot M_w}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$ with $Z = 0.98$ would yield **6.295 kg/m³** — an exact match.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Halon vs. Halon Alternatives Decision Grid

| Engineering Parameter | Halon 1301 | FM-200 | Novec 1230 | Inergen | CO₂ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Design Concentration (n-Heptane) | 5.0 % | 6.7 % | 4.5 % | 38 % | 34 % |
| NOAEL (% v/v) | 5.0 % | 9.0 % | 10.0 % | 43 % | 6 % |
| Margin (NOAEL − Design) | **0 % (boundary)** | 2.3 % | 5.5 % | 5.0 % | −28 % (UNSAFE) |
| ODP | 10.0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| GWP₁₀₀ | 6,900 | 2,050 | <1 | 0 | 1 |
| Storage Pressure (typical) | 25 / 42 bar | 25 / 42 bar | 25 / 42 bar | 200 / 300 bar | 60 bar |
| Discharge Time | ≤ 10 s | ≤ 10 s | ≤ 10 s | ≤ 60 s | ≤ 60 s |
| Use in Occupied Space | Yes (5 % limit) | Yes | Yes (preferred) | Yes (preferred) | **No (asphyxiation)** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Halon 1301 Total Flooding System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the *signal flow* and *agent flow* through a typical NFPA 12A-compliant halon system, including detection, decision logic, alarm sequencing, and agent release.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Smoke Detector ASD] --> D[Fire Alarm Control Panel FACP]
    B[Heat Detector Fixed Temp 57C] --> D
    C[UV or IR Flame Detector] --> D
    D --> E{Cross Zoning Logic Double Knock}
    E -- Confirmed Fire --> F[Stage 1 Pre Discharge Alarm 30s]
    F --> G{Manual Abort Switch Operator}
    G -- Hold --> F
    G -- Continue --> H[Stage 2 Discharge Alarm Evacuation Siren]
    H --> I[Time Delay Relay TDR 0 to 30s]
    I --> J[Activate Master Bank Solenoid Valve]
    J --> K[Open Reserve Bank Selector Valve]
    J --> L[Release Pilot Cylinder Nitrogen 17 bar]
    L --> M[Drive Pneumatic Actuator on Main Bank]
    M --> N[Halon 1301 Cylinders 25 or 42 bar Nitrogen Pad]
    N --> O[Discharge Manifold and Check Valves]
    O --> P[Distribution Piping Network Schedule 40 Steel]
    P --> Q1[Nozzle 1 Radial Spray Hazard Zone A]
    P --> Q2[Nozzle 2 Radial Spray Hazard Zone B]
    P --> Q3[Nozzle 3 Fan Spray Hazard Zone C]
    Q1 --> R[Enclosure Volume V eff cubic m]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    R --> S[Maintained at 5 percent by volume for 10 to 15 min]
    S --> T[Door Fan Enclosure Integrity Test less than 0.04 m3 per min per sqm]
    D --> U[System Status Annunciator and Event Log]
    J --> U
    N --> U
    U --> V[Remote Monitoring BMS or SCADA]
```

## 4.2 Process Logic Sequence — Halon System Activation Timeline

```mermaid
gantt
    title Halon 1301 Total Flooding Activation Sequence (NFPA 12A)
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S s

    section Detection
    Detector Activation         :a1, 0, 2s
    FACP Cross Zoning Decision  :a2, after a1, 2s

    section Alarm Phase
    Pre-Discharge Alarm 30 s    :b1, after a2, 30s
    Manual Abort Window         :b2, after b1, 0s

    section Discharge Phase
    TDR 0 to 30 s Delay         :c1, after b1, 30s
    Solenoid Activation         :c2, after c1, 1s
    Pilot Cylinder Release      :c3, after c2, 2s
    Main Bank Discharge         :c4, after c3, 8s

    section Hold Phase
    Concentration Hold 10 min   :d1, after c4, 600s
    Enclosure Pressure Monitor  :d2, after d1, 600s
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Halon vs. Halon Alternative Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Fire Suppression System Design] --> Q1{Is Space Continuously Occupied}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Are People Sensitive to Cardiotoxic Agents}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is Ozone Depletion a Hard Constraint}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4{Is Asset Value very High per m3}
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Use Halon 1301 Legacy or FM200 at 6.7 percent]
    Q3 -- Yes Aviation and Military --> Q6[Use Halon 1301 or HFC Blend under Essential Use Exemption]
    Q3 -- Yes Civilian --> Q7[Use Novec 1230 FK51112 at 4.5 percent]
    Q3 -- No --> Q8[Use Inert Gas IG541 Inergen at 38 percent]
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q9[Use Novec 1230 minimum design 4.5 percent max safety]
    Q4 -- No --> Q10[Use Inert Gas lowest cost option]
    Q5 --> End[Final Design]
    Q6 --> End
    Q7 --> End
    Q8 --> End
    Q9 --> End
    Q10 --> End
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flow follows a top-down decision hierarchy. The first branch identifies the occupancy nature (occupied vs. unoccupied), which determines whether *cardiac sensitisation* from halon by-products (HBr, HF) is acceptable. Unoccupied spaces (transformer vaults, switchgear rooms) can use **CO₂ at 34 %** despite NOAEL violation, because no personnel are present at discharge.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OEPET832]**

**Q:** Define *Halon 1301*. Why is it classified as a "clean agent"? State its **NOAEL** and **ODP** values.

**Model Answer:**

Halon 1301 is **bromotrifluoromethane (CBrF₃)**, a halogenated hydrocarbon used as a gaseous fire extinguishing agent. It is classified as a *clean agent* because it:
- Leaves **no residue** upon discharge (does not contaminate electronics, paper, or precision equipment)
- Is **electrically non-conductive** (suitable for Class C fires)
- **Evaporates completely** after discharge, allowing rapid re-occupancy

The key values are:
- **NOAEL (No Observable Adverse Effect Level): 5.0 % by volume**
- **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential): 10.0** (relative to CFC-11 = 1.0)

The very high ODP is the reason halon production was banned under the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** for new civil installations. **(3 Marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, OEPET832]**

**Q:** Explain the **chemical flame inhibition mechanism** by which halons extinguish fires. Write the key radical-scavenging reaction.

**Model Answer:**

Halon 1301 extinguishes fire through *chemical chain-breaking interference*, not by cooling or smothering. On contact with the flame, the C–Br bond breaks (bond dissociation energy ≈ 234 kJ/mol) at ~500 °C, releasing a highly reactive **bromine radical (Br•)**.

The key reaction is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HBr} + \text{OH}\bullet \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Br}\bullet
\end{aligned}
$$

The **Br• radical is regenerated** after each cycle, making halon a *catalytic inhibitor*. By removing the **H• and OH• chain carriers** essential for combustion propagation, the chain reaction is broken and the flame extinguishes at very low concentrations (3.7–5.0 % for n-Heptane). **(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module 2 of OEPET832 carries Part B questions of **14 marks** with internal choice between two question sets. Each question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The model solutions below are framed exactly to this pattern.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, OEPET832 Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **components and working of a Halon 1301 total flooding fire suppression system** as per NFPA 12A. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A computer server room of internal dimensions **10 m × 6 m × 3.2 m** contains immovable equipment occupying **12 m³**. The design fuel is **n-Heptane**. Compute the **mass of Halon 1301** required for total flooding. Use ρᵥ = **6.29 kg/m³**, cup-burner value = **3.7 %**, safety factor = **1.20**, NFPA-recommended design concentration = **5.0 %**, and piping residual allowance = **10 %**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a) — 7 Marks

A Halon 1301 total flooding system consists of the following components arranged per the diagram in §4.1:

| # | Component | Function | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Agent storage cylinders** (DOT-3AL/3ALM seamless steel) super-pressurised with dry N₂ to 25 or 42 bar at 21 °C | Stores liquid Halon 1301 and provides propellant energy for discharge | 1 |
| 2 | **Master and reserve banks** with selector valve | 100 % redundancy per NFPA 12A for critical facilities | 1 |
| 3 | **Actuation hardware** — solenoid valves, manual pulls, pneumatic actuators | Initiates discharge via electrical signal, manual pull, or pilot gas | 1 |
| 4 | **Discharge nozzles** (radial 180°/360°, fan-spray) | Atomise and distribute agent to achieve concentration uniformity in ≤ 10 s | 1 |
| 5 | **Distribution piping** (Schedule 40 steel) | Transport agent from cylinders to nozzles; hydraulically calculated | 1 |
| 6 | **Detection & control** — smoke (ASD), heat (57 °C fixed), UV/IR flame detectors → FACP with cross-zoning | Confirms fire and initiates pre-discharge sequence | 1 |
| 7 | **Enclosure integrity** — door-fan pressurisation, pneumatic pre-discharge alarm (30 s), abort switch, evacuation siren, warning signs | Maintains concentration hold, allows safe egress, prevents accidental discharge | 1 |

**Working sequence:** Detection → FACP cross-zoning confirmation → 30 s pre-discharge alarm → manual abort window → TDR delay → solenoid activation → pilot cylinder release → main bank discharge (≤ 10 s) → hold period (10–15 min) at 5.0 % concentration. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Gross volume.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{gross} &= 10 \times 6 \times 3.2 = 192 \,\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective volume (subtract displaced equipment).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{eff} &= 192 - 12 = 180 \,\text{m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum design concentration (C_cup × SF).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{min} &= 0.037 \times 1.20 = 0.0444 \;(4.44 \,\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Adopt NFPA-recommended design concentration $C = 0.050$** (since 4.44 % < 5.0 % and 5.0 % = NOAEL boundary).

**Step 5 — Apply the flooding mass formula (F1).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &= \frac{V_{eff} \cdot C \cdot \rho_v}{1 - C} \\
&= \frac{180 \times 0.050 \times 6.29}{1 - 0.050} \\
&= \frac{180 \times 0.3145}{0.950} \\
&= \frac{56.61}{0.950} \\
&= 59.59 \,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Add 10 % piping residual.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{final} &= 59.59 \times 1.10 = 65.55 \,\text{kg} \approx 66 \,\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** **66 kg of Halon 1301** for the master bank, with a **66 kg reserve bank** per NFPA 12A. Total on-site storage: **132 kg**. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, OEPET832 Module 2]**

**(a)** Discuss the **Montreal Protocol phase-out of halons** and the **environmental parameters** (ODP, GWP, atmospheric lifetime) that necessitated the ban. Compare **Halon 1301, FM-200, Novec 1230, and Inergen** on these parameters in a tabular format. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A halon 1301 system discharges into a room of **240 m³ gross volume**. The measured leakage rate from a door-fan test is **0.4 m³/min**. The required hold time is **10 minutes**. The initial discharge concentration is **5.0 %**. Verify whether the system will maintain the **minimum design concentration of 4.5 %** for the entire hold period. Use the exponential decay equation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a) — 7 Marks

The **Montreal Protocol (signed 1987, entered into force 1989)** classified all halons as **Class I controlled substances** because of their destructive impact on the stratospheric ozone layer. The key environmental parameters are:

- **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential):** Halon 1301 has ODP = **10**, meaning 1 kg of Halon 1301 destroys 10× the ozone that 1 kg of CFC-11 destroys. Bromine is ~40–60× more efficient than chlorine in catalytic ozone destruction cycles.
- **GWP₁₀₀ (Global Warming Potential over 100 years):** Halon 1301 has GWP = **6,900** (CO₂ = 1), making it a potent greenhouse gas.
- **Atmospheric lifetime:** Halon 1301 persists for **65 years**, allowing it to diffuse into the stratosphere long after release.

**Comparative Table (full marks for tabular + 2-line interpretation):**

| Agent | ODP | GWP₁₀₀ | Atmospheric Lifetime (yr) | NOAEL (% v/v) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Halon 1301** | 10.0 | 6,900 | 65 | 5.0 |
| **FM-200 (HFC-227ea)** | 0 | 2,050 | 36 | 9.0 |
| **Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12)** | 0 | <1 | 0.014 (days) | 10.0 |
| **Inergen (IG-541)** | 0 | 0 | N/A (atmospheric gases) | 43.0 |

**Interpretation:** Although Novec 1230 has a GWP <1, it is now the preferred replacement. Inergen has zero environmental impact but requires very high storage pressures (200/300 bar). The Kigali Amendment (2016) further restricted HFCs like FM-200 due to GWP. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute the leakage fraction rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
k = \frac{\dot{V}_{leak}}{V_{gross}} = \frac{0.4}{240} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{min}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the exponential decay equation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(t) = C_0 \cdot \exp(-k \cdot t)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values at t = 10 min.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(10) &= 0.050 \cdot \exp(-1.667 \times 10^{-3} \times 10) \\
&= 0.050 \cdot \exp(-0.01667) \\
&= 0.050 \times 0.98347 \\
&= 0.04917 \text{ (i.e., 4.92 %)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare to minimum design concentration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C(10) = 0.0492 \;\geq\; C_{min} = 0.045 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The system **PASSES** the hold-time verification. Concentration decays from 5.00 % to 4.92 % over 10 min, maintaining a **9.3 % safety margin** above the minimum. If the result had been below 4.5 %, the engineer would have to seal penetrations or increase initial agent mass. **(7 Marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Cost 2–3 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Forgetting the (1 − C) denominator** in Formula F1. Students often write $M = V \cdot C \cdot \rho_v$ and lose 2–3 marks. The denominator corrects for the volume of halon vapour that *replaces* a portion of the air.
> 2. **Ignoring displaced volume** (V_displaced) of equipment in the enclosure. NFPA 12A explicitly requires *effective volume*.
> 3. **Not verifying the NOAEL boundary.** Designing at 6 % for an occupied room with Halon 1301 is a *toxicological violation*; full marks are not awarded.
> 4. **Mixing up Halon 1301 and 1211 properties.** Halon 1211 is for *local application/portable extinguishers*, not total flooding of occupied spaces (NOAEL 0.5 %).
> 5. **Skipping the 10 s discharge time** check. NFPA 12A mandates that the agent reach 95 % of design concentration within 10 s for Class B hazards.
> 6. **Omitting units** in the final answer. Always write $\text{kg}$ or $\text{m}^3$ explicitly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-Second Revision (KTU Exam Eve Strategy):**

- **Halon 1301** = CBrF₃, MW = 148.9 g/mol, boiling point = −57.8 °C, NOAEL = **5 %**, LOAEL = **7.5 %**, ODP = **10**, GWP = **6,900**
- **Halon 1211** = CBrClF₂, MW = 165.4 g/mol, NOAEL = **0.5 %** → *local application / portable only*
- **Halon numbering:** 4 digits = (C)(F)(Cl)(Br) count
- **Extinguishing mechanism:** *Catalytic chemical flame inhibition* via Br• radical scavenging of OH• and H• chain carriers
- **Standard design concentration (n-Heptane):** 5.0 % (NFPA 12A)
- **Maximum discharge time:** ≤ **10 seconds**
- **Enclosure leakage limit:** ≤ **0.04 m³/min/m²** (door-fan test)
- **Hold time:** **10–15 minutes** typical; verify via $C(t) = C_0 \cdot \exp(-kt)$
- **Master + Reserve bank:** **100 % redundancy** for critical facilities
- **Flooding mass formula:** $M = \dfrac{V \cdot C \cdot \rho_v}{1 - C}$ (always add 10 % piping residual)
- **Montreal Protocol (1987):** banned new production; Kigali Amendment (2016) further restricts HFC replacements
- **Halon alternatives:** FM-200 (HFC-227ea), Novec 1230 (FK-5-1-12), Inergen (IG-541) — all have ODP = 0
- **NOAEL safety check** is **mandatory** for every occupied-space design — examiners award 0 marks if omitted
- **Key standards:** NFPA 12A (Halon 1301), NFPA 12B (Halon 1211), IS 6385 (BIS Indian Standard equivalent)
- **Hazards:** *Cardiac sensitisation* above 7.5 %; *thermal decomposition products* (HBr, HF, COF₂) toxic in confined fires
- **Recovery/recycling** of existing halon stock is governed by **EPA SNAP Program** and **Halon Essential Use Exemptions** (aviation, military, oil & gas)
- **Design rule of thumb:** $C_{min} = C_{cup} \times 1.20$ for Class B; $C_{min} = C_{cup} \times 1.30$ for deep-seated Class A
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Portable Extinguisher
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET832 - OCCUPATIONAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Portable Extinguisher

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Portable Fire Extinguisher (KTU OEPET832 Definition):**
> A *portable fire extinguisher* is a hand-held, pressurized, self-contained fire protection device containing an extinguishing agent that can be discharged by manual operation to suppress or extinguish an incipient-stage fire. It is engineered to be carried and operated by a single operator, typically weighing between **0.5 kg to 23 kg** (excluding wheeled units), and is governed under the **Indian Standard IS 15683:2018** (harmonized with **ISO 7165**) and the **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016, Part 4)**.

In KTU's 2024 Scheme parlance, a portable extinguisher is treated as the **first-line defence** in the hierarchy of fire control measures — that is, it is designed to fight a fire **at the incipient (inborn) stage**, *before* the fire brigade arrives or fixed firefighting systems (sprinklers, hydrants) activate.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: "The Firefighter's First Aid Kit"**
> Imagine your home has a medical first-aid box. You do not call a hospital for every small cut — you use the first-aid kit. A portable fire extinguisher works exactly the same way. It is the **fire's "first-aid kit"**. When a candle tips over and a small flame ignites a paper, you do not evacuate the entire building and call the fire brigade; you grab the extinguisher, aim it at the paper, and stop the fire in **under 30 seconds**. But the analogy has a critical lesson: a first-aid kit cannot perform heart surgery. Likewise, a portable extinguisher is **NOT** meant for large, fully developed fires. It is designed **only for the first 60 seconds** of a fire's life.

## 1.3 Fundamental Purpose & Engineering Significance

The four engineering purposes of a portable extinguisher are:

1. **Suppression** – To reduce the heat of the combustion reaction below its ignition temperature.
2. **Smothering** – To remove the oxygen supply by blanketing the fuel surface.
3. **Chain-breaking** – To interrupt the chemical chain reaction in the flame zone.
4. **Cooling** – To extract heat from the fuel and surrounding surfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** A portable extinguisher is designed to fight a fire at the **CLASS A INCEPTION STAGE** (i.e., a fire that has not spread beyond its point of origin and involves less than **0.5 m²** of combustible material). The internationally accepted maximum time-to-control is **30 seconds**, with a minimum discharge duration of **8 seconds** for Class A fires.

## 1.4 Terminology Snapshot (KTU Glossary Lock)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Incipient Stage** | The early, controllable stage of a fire |
| **Discharge Time** | The total time for which the agent is released |
| **Throw Range** | The horizontal distance the agent stream travels |
| **Recharge Pressure** | The N₂ pressure at **+20 °C** typically **15 bar** for powder units |
| **Hose Assembly** | The flexible tube conducting the agent to the nozzle |
| **Tamper Seal** | A safety pin preventing accidental discharge |
| **Pressure Indicator** | A gauge showing internal pressure status |
| **Class Rating** | A letter (A, B, C, D, K) indicating the fire class the unit fights |
| **Numerical Rating** | A number indicating extinguishing capacity (e.g., 2A, 10B, 21B) |
| **Boiling Point** | The temperature at which a liquefied gas (e.g., CO₂) vaporizes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tetrahedron of Fire & How Extinguishers Break It

Fire is a self-sustaining chemical chain reaction that requires **four elements** to exist simultaneously — this is called the **Fire Tetrahedron**. Each portable extinguisher is engineered to remove **at least one** of these four elements:

- **Fuel** (solid, liquid, gas)
- **Heat** (energy above ignition temperature)
- **Oxygen** (typically ≥ 16% in air)
- **Uninhibited Chemical Chain Reaction** (free radicals H•, O•, OH•)

| Fire Tetrahedron Element | Extinguisher Type That Removes It | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Heat | Water, Water-Mist | Latent heat absorption (cooling) |
| Oxygen | Foam, CO₂, Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) | Blanketing / displacement |
| Fuel | Foam (AFFF), Wet Chemical | Vapour sealing, saponification |
| Chain Reaction | Dry Chemical Powder (ABC), Clean Agent (FK-5-1-12) | Free-radical scavenging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Intuition — "Why is ABC Powder the most versatile?"**
> Monoammonium phosphate (the active agent in ABC powder) is the *only* commonly used extinguishing medium that simultaneously attacks **three of the four** tetrahedron sides. It decomposes endothermically (absorbs heat), coats the fuel (smothers), and releases free-radical scavengers (interrupts chain reaction). Hence, ABC powder is the universal choice for offices, schools, and commercial buildings.

## 2.2 Classification of Fires (IS 15683 & NFPA 10 Aligned)

The **KTU 2024 syllabus** demands knowledge of fire classification as the foundation for extinguisher selection:

- **Class A** – Ordinary combustibles (wood, paper, cloth, rubber, plastics).
  *Numerical Rating Test:* A wood crib/pile fire of a specific size.
- **Class B** – Flammable liquids & gases (petrol, diesel, LPG, paints, solvents).
  *Numerical Rating Test:* A square pan fire of a known area.
- **Class C** – Flammable gases under pressure (LNG, methane, propane).
- **Class D** – Combustible metals (magnesium, sodium, potassium, titanium).
- **Class E (India) / Class K (US)** – Cooking oils & fats (vegetable oils, animal fats).
- **Electrical (F)** – Energized electrical equipment (NOT a "class" in NFPA, but treated as one in India).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Rule:**
> A **Class A** fire extinguisher is rated with both a *letter* (A) and a *number* (e.g., 4A). The number indicates the *equivalency to water* — **1A rating = 1.25 gallons (≈ 4.75 litres) of water**. So a **4A extinguisher** has the cooling capacity of **5 gallons of water (≈ 18.93 litres)**, but stored in a 4–6 kg pressurized cylinder.

## 2.3 Types of Portable Extinguishers & Their Working Principles

### 2.3.1 Water-Type Extinguisher (Stored Pressure)

- **Agent:** Ordinary water + N₂ propellant (≈ 1.0 MPa stored pressure).
- **Use:** Class A fires only.
- **Discharge Time:** 30 – 60 seconds.
- **Throw Range:** 6 – 10 m.
- **Working Principle:** Water absorbs **2,257 kJ/kg** of latent heat of vaporization, cooling the fuel below its ignition temperature.

### 2.3.2 Foam-Type Extinguisher (AFFF — Aqueous Film-Forming Foam)

- **Agent:** 3% AFFF concentrate + 97% water + N₂.
- **Use:** Class A & B fires.
- **Working Principle:** The foam bubble blanket **smothers** the fuel and the aqueous film **seals** the vapour.

### 2.3.3 Dry Chemical Powder (DCP) Extinguisher

- **Variants:** ABC (Monoammonium Phosphate) and BC (Sodium Bicarbonate).
- **Use:** ABC = A, B, C, Electrical; BC = B, C, Electrical.
- **Working Principle:** Powder particles **radiate outward**, blocking H•, O•, OH• radicals — interrupting the **chain reaction**.
- **Pressure:** 15 bar N₂; discharge time 9 – 13 seconds; throw 4 – 7 m.

### 2.3.4 CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide) Extinguisher

- **Agent:** Liquefied CO₂ at **57 – 65 bar** at 21 °C.
- **Use:** Class B & Electrical fires.
- **Working Principle:** CO₂ displaces oxygen (smothering) and provides mild cooling (-78 °C dry-ice temperature at nozzle).
- **Key Fact:** **No residue** — preferred for IT rooms, server racks, laboratories.

### 2.3.5 Wet Chemical Extinguisher

- **Agent:** Potassium acetate / citrate solution.
- **Use:** Class K / F (cooking oil fires).
- **Working Principle:** **Saponification** — the agent reacts with hot oil to form a soapy foam layer (a *cool, non-ignitable soap*).

### 2.3.6 Clean Agent Extinguisher (FK-5-1-12 / Novec 1230 / FM-200)

- **Use:** Class B, C & Electrical — protects data centres, museums, archives.
- **ODP** (Ozone Depletion Potential) = **0**; **GWP** (Global Warming Potential) = **1**.

## 2.4 The PASS Technique (Operator Use)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PASS (the universal KTU-marked operating mnemonic):**
> - **P**ull the pin.
> - **A**im at the base of the fire.
> - **S**queeze the handle.
> - **S**weep side to side.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula / Standard | Description | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1A = 1.25 \text{ gallons of water}$ | Class A numerical rating | gallons / L | Sizing for Class A fires |
| 2 | $1B = 1 \text{ ft}^2$ of pan area | Class B numerical rating | ft² / m² | Sizing for liquid fires |
| 3 | $E_{\text{latent}} = m \times 2{,}257$ | Heat absorbed by water vaporization | kJ | Cooling capacity of water extinguishers |
| 4 | $\rho_{\text{CO}_2} = 1.98$ at 0 °C | CO₂ gas density | kg/m³ | Discharge volume calculation |
| 5 | $t_{\text{discharge}} \geq 8 \text{ s}$ | NFPA minimum discharge time | s | Acceptance criterion |
| 6 | $D_{\text{travel, A}} \leq 23 \text{ m (75 ft)}$ | Maximum travel distance for Class A | m | Placement of extinguishers |
| 7 | $D_{\text{travel, B}} \leq 15 \text{ m (50 ft)}$ | Maximum travel distance for Class B | m | Placement of extinguishers |
| 8 | $P_{\text{N}_2} = 15 \text{ bar}$ at 20 °C | Standard propellant pressure | bar | Charging specification |
| 9 | $P_{\text{CO}_2} = 57$ to $65$ bar | CO₂ cylinder pressure | bar | High-pressure vessels |
| 10 | $T_{\text{test,hydro}} = 5$ yrs (DCP), $12$ yrs (CO₂) | Hydrostatic test interval | years | Maintenance schedule |
| 11 | $V_{\text{exp}} = V_{\text{liq}} \times \rho_{\text{liq}} / \rho_{\text{gas}}$ | CO₂ gas expansion ratio | dimensionless | Discharge physics |
| 12 | $\text{Height}_{\text{mount}} = 1.5 \text{ m max}$ | Mounting height for $<\ 18$ kg units | m | Ergonomic placement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility in KTU Engineering Domains:**
> - **Mechanical Engineering:** Designing extinguisher bracket assemblies, pressure-vessel certification.
> - **Civil Engineering:** Sizing extinguishers per floor area as per **NBC 2016, Part 4, Table 3** — typically **1 extinguisher per 200 m²** for Class A hazards.
> - **Electrical Engineering:** Selecting **CO₂ or Clean Agent** for switchgear rooms (water + electricity = electrocution hazard).
> - **Safety Engineering:** Hazard analysis, hot-work permit systems, PPE compatibility.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation 1 — Class A Rating Equivalency

**Problem:** A KTU exam question typically states: *"What is the effective water capacity of a 6A-rated portable extinguisher?"*

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

The NFPA 10 / IS 15683 defines the **Class A numerical rating (N)** such that:

$$N = \frac{V_{\text{water}}}{1.25 \text{ gallons}}$$

where $V_{\text{water}}$ is the effective volume of water in gallons that has equivalent firefighting capacity.

**Step 1 — Write the relationship:**

$$V_{\text{water}} = N \times 1.25 \text{ gallons}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute N = 6:**

$$V_{\text{water}} = 6 \times 1.25 = 7.5 \text{ gallons}$$

**Step 3 — Convert gallons to litres (1 US gallon = 3.785 L):**

$$V_{\text{water}} = 7.5 \times 3.785 = 28.39 \text{ litres}$$

**Result:** A **6A** extinguisher has the same firefighting capacity as **28.39 litres of water**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation 2 — CO₂ Discharge Volume Expansion

**Problem:** *"A 4.5 kg CO₂ extinguisher discharges its full charge. Calculate the volume of CO₂ gas released at standard conditions, given liquid CO₂ density = 1,030 kg/m³ and gas CO₂ density at 1 atm, 0 °C = 1.98 kg/m³."*

**Step 1 — Calculate the volume of liquid CO₂ in the cylinder:**

$$V_{\text{liq}} = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{\rho_{\text{liq}}}$$

Substituting $m = 4.5$ kg and $\rho_{\text{liq}} = 1{,}030$ kg/m³:

$$V_{\text{liq}} = \frac{4.5}{1{,}030} = 4.368 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of mass (no CO₂ is lost — only phase changes):**

$$m_{\text{liq}} = m_{\text{gas}} = 4.5 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the volume occupied by 4.5 kg of gaseous CO₂ at NTP:**

$$V_{\text{gas}} = \frac{m_{\text{CO}_2}}{\rho_{\text{gas}}}$$

$$V_{\text{gas}} = \frac{4.5}{1.98} = 2.273 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Express the expansion ratio as a dimensionless quantity:**

$$\text{Expansion Ratio} = \frac{V_{\text{gas}}}{V_{\text{liq}}} = \frac{2.273}{4.368 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 520$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 4.5 kg liquid CO₂ cylinder, when fully discharged, produces approximately **2,273 litres of CO₂ gas** — an expansion ratio of about **520:1**. This huge volume is what allows it to *displace* oxygen from the fire zone.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation 3 — Discharge Time Verification (IS 15683 / NFPA 10)

**Problem:** *"A 6 kg ABC dry powder extinguisher contains 6 kg of monoammonium phosphate. The discharge flow rate is 0.6 kg/s. Does it meet the NFPA 10 minimum discharge time?"*

**Step 1 — Recall the NFPA 10 minimum discharge time criterion:**

$$t_{\text{discharge, min}} \geq 8 \text{ seconds (for Class A)}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the actual discharge time:**

$$t_{\text{discharge}} = \frac{m_{\text{agent}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{agent}}}$$

$$t_{\text{discharge}} = \frac{6}{0.6} = 10 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the minimum criterion:**

$$t_{\text{discharge}} = 10 \text{ s} \geq 8 \text{ s} = t_{\text{min}}$$

**Result:** The extinguisher **PASSES** the NFPA 10 acceptance test. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation 4 — Travel Distance & Extinguisher Count for a Hall

**Problem:** *"A workshop of dimensions 20 m × 30 m contains Class A and Class B hazards. Determine the number of extinguishers required as per IS 15683 placement rules."*

**Step 1 — Floor area:**

$$A = 20 \times 30 = 600 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Travel distance for Class A (NFPA 10 / IS 15683):**

$$D_{\text{travel, A}} \leq 23 \text{ m (75 ft)}$$

**Step 3 — Travel distance for Class B (NFPA 10 / IS 15683):**

$$D_{\text{travel, B}} \leq 15 \text{ m (50 ft)}$$

**Step 4 — Number of extinguishers required for Class A coverage (rule of thumb: 1 per 200 m²):**

$$N_A = \left\lceil \frac{600}{200} \right\rceil = \lceil 3 \rceil = 3 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5 — Number of extinguishers required for Class B coverage (denser placement due to shorter travel distance):**

$$N_B = \left\lceil \frac{600}{150} \right\rceil = \lceil 4 \rceil = 4 \text{ units}$$

**Step 6 — Total extinguisher count (assuming separate ABC and CO₂ units):**

$$N_{\text{total}} = N_A + N_B = 3 + 4 = 7 \text{ units}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The hall requires a minimum of **3 ABC dry powder units** (for Class A) and **4 CO₂ or BC powder units** (for Class B/electrical hazards). The shorter travel distance for Class B is the *binding* constraint.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection Quick-Reference

| Hazard | First-Choice Extinguisher | Forbidden Extinguisher | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Office (paper, wood) | ABC Dry Powder (4A/21B) | None (ABC is universal) | — |
| Server Room | CO₂ or Clean Agent | Water, Foam, DCP | Residue damages electronics |
| Petrol Pump | Foam (AFFF) or DCP (BC) | Water | Water spreads flammable liquid |
| Kitchen (oil fryer) | Wet Chemical (K-Class) | Water, CO₂, DCP | Water causes **flash fire / boilover** |
| Electrical Panel | CO₂ | Water, Foam | Electrocution hazard |
| Magnesium Foundry | Special Class D Powder (NaCl, Cu) | Water, CO₂, ABC Powder | Water reacts with Mg → H₂ explosion |
| Library / Archive | Clean Agent (FM-200, Novec) | DCP | DCP ruins books; Clean Agent is residue-free |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Internal Anatomy of a Stored-Pressure DCP Extinguisher

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Cylinder Body MS-IS:2062] --> A2[ABC Monoammonium Phosphate 6 kg]
    A2 --> A3[Nitrogen Propellant 15 bar]
    A1 --> A4[Carrying Handle SS-304]
    A1 --> A5[Pressure Gauge 0 to 25 bar]
    A1 --> A6[Discharge Valve Assembly]
    A6 --> A7[Tamper Seal Pin]
    A6 --> A8[CO2 Cartridge Quick-Release]
    A6 --> A9[Siphon Tube PVC 12 mm]
    A6 --> A10[High-Pressure Hose EPDM 600 mm]
    A10 --> A11[Nozzle with Suction Strainer]
    A1 --> A12[Wall Mounting Bracket]
    A1 --> A13[Inspection Tag with QR Code]
    A1 --> A14[Hydrostatic Test Stamping]

    B1[Annual Visual Inspection] --> B2[Check Pressure Gauge]
    B1 --> B3[Verify Tamper Seal]
    B1 --> B4[Weigh for Agent Loss 10 percent]
    B5[5-Year Hydrostatic Test] --> B6[Pressure Test at 35 bar]
    B5 --> B7[ISI Marking Verification]
    B8[Recharge Cycle] --> B9[Depressurize and Empty]
    B8 --> B10[Refill Agent per IS 14609]
    B8 --> B11[Repressurize to 15 bar N2]
    B8 --> B12[Leak Test at 20 bar 60 s]

    A11 -.-> B1
    A13 -.-> B1
    A14 -.-> B5
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Flow of a Portable Extinguisher During Fire Suppression

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Fire Inception Stage 0 to 60 s] --> P2[Operator Detects Flame and Smoke]
    P2 --> P3[Locate Nearest Extinguisher within 23 m]
    P3 --> P4[Pull Pin P in PASS]
    P4 --> P5[Aim at Fire Base A in PASS]
    P5 --> P6[Squeeze Handle S in PASS]
    P6 --> P7[Sweep Side to Side S in PASS]
    P7 --> P8{Agent Hits Fuel Surface}
    P8 --> P9[Heat Absorption Cooling]
    P8 --> P10[Oxygen Displacement Smothering]
    P8 --> P11[Chain Reaction Interruption]
    P8 --> P12[Cooling of Fuel and Surroundings]
    P9 --> P13[Fire Extinguished within 30 s]
    P10 --> P13
    P11 --> P13
    P12 --> P13
    P13 --> P14[Watch for Re-ignition for 30 min]
    P14 --> P15[Report and Refill Unit]
    P15 --> P16[Re-certification by ISI-authorized agency]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Selection Logic Flowchart for KTU Hazard Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Identify Fire Hazard at Workplace] --> S2{Type of Combustible?}
    S2 -->|Wood, Paper, Cloth, Plastic| S3[Class A Hazard]
    S2 -->|Petrol, Diesel, Paint, Solvent| S4[Class B Hazard]
    S2 -->|LPG, Methane, Acetylene| S5[Class C Hazard]
    S2 -->|Mg, Na, K, Ti, Zr| S6[Class D Hazard]
    S2 -->|Vegetable Oil, Ghee, Animal Fat| S7[Class K or F Hazard]
    S2 -->|Energized Electrical Equipment| S8[Electrical Class F Hazard]

    S3 --> R1[ABC Dry Powder 4A Rating]
    S4 --> R2[ABC Dry Powder 21B or Foam]
    S5 --> R3[ABC Dry Powder 21B or CO2]
    S6 --> R4[Class D Special Powder NaCl or Cu]
    S7 --> R5[Wet Chemical Potassium Acetate]
    S8 --> R6[CO2 or Clean Agent FK-5-1-12]

    R1 --> X1[Mount at 1.5 m Height]
    R2 --> X2[Travel Distance 15 m Max]
    R3 --> X2
    R4 --> X3[Special Training Required]
    R5 --> X4[6 Months Visual Check]

```

> The above three diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `A1`, `P13`, `R6`, `X5`) and **clean uppercase text labels** with no markdown formatting inside the double quotes. This guarantees successful rendering in Mermaid-compatible markdown viewers, GitHub README files, and Obsidian/VS Code previews.
