Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the WBGT (Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature) Index The WBGT index was developed by the U.S. Marine Corps in the 1950s and is the most widely used occupational heat stress screening tool in the world, mandated by **ISO 7243** and adopted by **ACGIH**. **Step 1 — Identify the three contributing thermometer readings:** - $T_{db}$ — **Dry-Bulb Temperature**: ordinary air temperature measured with a standard mercury thermometer shielded from radiation. - $T_{nwb}$ — **Natural Wet-Bulb Temperature**: measured with a thermometer whose bulb is covered by a wick kept moist with distilled water (no forced ventilation). Represents combined effect of **temperature + humidity** on evaporative cooling. - $T_g$ — **Globe Temperature**: temperature measured inside a hollow **matte-black copper sphere** (150 mm diameter). Represents combined effect of **temperature + radiant heat**. **Step 2 — Weight the contributions based on environment type:** The weighting factors are derived empirically from physiological studies showing that: - **Evaporative cooling (T_nwb)** is the dominant heat-loss pathway → gets the largest weight (**0.7**). - **Radiant/convective load (T_g)** is secondary → gets **0.3** indoors or is split with T_db outdoors. **Step 3 — Indoor / No Solar Load Formula:** $$WBGT_{indoor} = 0.7 \cdot T_{nwb} + 0.3 \cdot T_g$$ **Step 4 — Outdoor / With Solar Load Formula (additional 10% to T_db):** $$WBGT_{outdoor} = 0.7 \cdot T_{nwb} + 0.2 \cdot T_g + 0.1 \cdot T_{db}$$ **Step 5 — Interpretation:** Compare the calculated WBGT against the ACGIH TLV table for the relevant work rate and work-rest cycle. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU-Style) > **[Problem Statement]:** A construction site (outdoor, full sun) has the following readings taken at 2 PM: Dry-bulb temperature = 38 °C, Natural wet-bulb temperature = 27 °C, Globe temperature = 48 °C. Workers are performing brick-laying (moderate work, metabolic rate ≈ 250 W/m²), working 75% of the hour with 25% rest. The crew is fully acclimatized and wearing standard summer work clothing. Determine: > (a) The WBGT index. > (b) Whether the exposure exceeds the ACGIH TLV. > (c) Recommend a control measure if exceeded. #### Step-by-Step Solution: **Part (a): WBGT Calculation (Outdoor, with solar load)** $$\begin{aligned} WBGT_{outdoor} &= 0.7 \cdot T_{nwb} + 0.2 \cdot T_g + 0.1 \cdot T_{db} \\ &= 0.7 \cdot (27) + 0.2 \cdot (48) + 0.1 \cdot (38) \\ &= 18.90 + 9.60 + 3.80 \\ &= 32.30 \, °C \end{aligned}$$ **Part (b): Compare against ACGIH TLV Table** For **moderate work (250 W/m²) at 75% work / 25% rest**, the TLV ceiling is **28 °C** (acclimatized). $$\text{Measured WBGT} = 32.30 \, °C > \text{TLV Ceiling} = 28 \, °C$$ **Heat exposure EXCEEDS the safe limit by 4.3 °C.** **Part (c): Recommended Control Measures (Hierarchy of Controls)** | Control Level | Specific Recommendation | |---|---| | **Engineering** | Erect **shaded rest shelters**; provide **mechanical ventilation**; install **misting fans** at rest area | | **Administrative** | Shift work to **early morning (5 AM – 11 AM)**; enforce **mandatory hydration** (1 L/hr); reduce work cycle to **50% work / 50% rest** | | **PPE** | Provide **light-coloured, loose-weave, breathable clothing**; wide-brim hats; cooling neck towels | After applying administrative control (reducing to 50% work / 50% rest), the permissible WBGT rises to **29.4 °C**. Since 32.3 °C still exceeds 29.4 °C, **further engineering controls (active cooling, reflective barriers) are mandatory**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient The combined (mixed) convection regime transitions based on air velocity: **Step 1 — Natural Convection (V < 0.5 m/s, no forced air movement):** $$h_c = 2.38 \cdot \vert T_{sk} - T_a \vert^{0.25} \quad \text{[W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)]}$$ **Step 2 — Forced Convection (V ≥ 0.5 m/s):** $$h_c = 10.4 \cdot \sqrt{V} \quad \text{[W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)]}$$ **Step 3 — Example:** For T_sk = 35 °C, T_a = 25 °C, V = 1.5 m/s: $$\begin{aligned} \Delta T &= \vert T_{sk} - T_a \vert = 10 \, °C \\ h_c \text{ (natural)} &= 2.38 \cdot (10)^{0.25} = 2.38 \cdot 1.778 = 4.23 \, \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)} \\ h_c \text{ (forced)} &= 10.4 \cdot \sqrt{1.5} = 10.4 \cdot 1.225 = 12.74 \, \text{W/(m}^2\cdot\text{K)} \end{aligned}$$ Since 12.74 > 4.23, forced convection dominates → **use h_c = 12.74 W/(m²·K)**. **Step 4 — Convective Heat Exchange (per m² body surface):** $$C = h_c \cdot f_{cl} \cdot (T_{sk} - T_a) = 12.74 \cdot 1.1 \cdot (35 - 25) = 140.14 \, \text{W/m}^2$$ This means **140.14 W per square meter** of skin surface is being transferred to the air. --- ### 3.4 Wind Chill Index Worked Example > **[Problem]:** A cold-storage worker is exposed to T_a = −15 °C with wind speed V = 20 km/h. Calculate the Wind Chill Index. **Step 1 — Apply the Wind Chill formula:** $$WCI = T_a + 0.45 - \sqrt{V} \cdot (0.45 + 0.111 \cdot (T_a + 33)) + 0.45 \cdot \sqrt{V}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$\begin{aligned} WCI &= -15 + 0.45 - \sqrt{20} \cdot (0.45 + 0.111 \cdot (-15 + 33)) + 0.45 \cdot \sqrt{20} \\ &= -14.55 - 4.472 \cdot (0.45 + 0.111 \cdot 18) + 0.45 \cdot 4.472 \\ &= -14.55 - 4.472 \cdot (0.45 + 1.998) + 2.012 \\ &= -14.55 - 4.472 \cdot 2.448 + 2.012 \\ &= -14.55 - 10.945 + 2.012 \\ &= -23.48 \, °C \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** Although the actual air temperature is −15 °C, the **effective "feels-like" temperature is −23.48 °C**. According to the Wind Chill Hazard Table, frostbite can occur on exposed skin within **10–15 minutes** at this WCI value. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Heat Stress Indices | Index | Year | Key Inputs | Best Used For | Limitation | |---|---|---|---|---| | **WBGT** | 1957 | T_db, T_nwb, T_g | Screening & compliance | Empirical, no metabolic input | | **Effective Temperature (ET)** | 1923 | T_db, T_wb, V | General comfort | Ignores radiation | | **Heat Stress Index (HSI)** | 1959 | M, W, E_req, E_max | Strain prediction | Complex calculation | | **Required Sweat Rate (ISO 7933)** | 1989 | M, W, V, T_a, RH, I_cl | Detailed analysis | Requires full instrumentation | | **PMV-PPD (Fanger)** | 1970 | All 6 variables | Indoor comfort | Designed for steady-state comfort, not extreme work | --- ### 3.6 Body Surface Area Calculation Example For a worker of W = 70 kg, H = 1.75 m: $$A_D = 0.202 \cdot (70)^{0.425} \cdot (1.75)^{0.725}$$ $$\begin{aligned} (70)^{0.425} &\approx 6.578 \\ (1.75)^{0.725} &\approx 1.494 \\ A_D &= 0.202 \cdot 6.578 \cdot 1.494 \\ &\approx 1.985 \, \text{m}^2 \quad (\text{close to the standard } 2.0 \, \text{m}^2) \end{aligned}$$ If metabolic rate M = 250 W (absolute), then **M per m²** = 250 / 1.985 ≈ **125.94 W/m²** (Light-Moderate work category). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Human Heat Exchange with the Environment ```mermaid flowchart LR M[Metabolic Heat Production M] --> S{Heat Storage S in Body Core} W[External Work Output W] --> S S --> C[Convective Loss C to Air] S --> R[Radiative Loss R to Surfaces] S --> E[Evaporative Loss E via Sweat] S --> K[Conductive Loss K via Contact] S --> Resp[Respiratory Losses Cres and Eres] C --> Env((Thermal Environment)) R --> Env E --> Env K --> Env Resp --> Env Env --> Cloth{Clothing Insulation Icl} Cloth --> S ``` ### 4.2 Hierarchy of Controls for Thermal Stress ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify Thermal Hazard via WBGT or WCI] --> B{Hazard Confirmed?} B -- No --> Z[Document and Monitor Periodically] B -- Yes --> C[Elimination: Stop the Process] C --> D[Substitution: Automate Hot Tasks] D --> E[Engineering Controls] E --> E1[Ventilation Fans] E --> E2[Radiant Heat Shields] E --> E3[Insulation of Hot Pipes] E --> E4[Warmed Air Curtains for Cold] E --> F[Administrative Controls] F --> F1[Work Rest Cycles] F --> F2[Acclimatization Program] F --> F3[Hydration Schedule] F --> F4[Shift Rescheduling] F --> G[PPE Controls] G --> G1[Heat: Cooling Vests] G --> G2[Cold: Insulated Parkas] G --> H[Medical Surveillance] H --> I[Periodic Heat Strain Monitoring] ``` ### 4.3 Decision Tree for Heat Stress Assessment (ISO 7243 Protocol) ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Start: Workplace Survey]) --> Q1{Is WBGT
Measurable?} Q1 -- No --> Q2[Use ISO 15265
Analytical Method] Q1 -- Yes --> Q3[Calculate WBGT
at Workplace] Q3 --> Q4[Identify Metabolic
Rate Category] Q4 --> Q5[Identify Clothing
Insulation in clo] Q5 --> Q6{Look up ACGIH TLV
Table or ISO 7243} Q6 --> Q7{WBGT exceeds
TLV?} Q7 -- No --> Pass([Compliant: Document]) Q7 -- Yes --> Q8[Apply Hierarchy
of Controls] Q8 --> Q9[Reassess After
Controls] Q9 --> Q6 ``` ### 4.4 Cold Stress Triage Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Measure Ta and V] --> B[Calculate Wind Chill Index WCI] B --> C{WCI Range?} C -- WCI greater than minus 10 --> D1[Low Risk: Standard PPE] C -- WCI minus 10 to minus 28 --> D2[Moderate Risk: Insulated PPE + Buddy System] C -- WCI minus 28 to minus 45 --> D3[High Risk: Limit Exposure to 30 min + Medical Check] C -- WCI less than minus 45 --> D4[Extreme Risk: Suspend Outdoor Work] D1 --> M[Monitor Every 2 hours] D2 --> M D3 --> M D4 --> M ``` ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Thermal Stressor Control System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[INPUT SOURCES] I1[Air Temperature Sensor] I2[Humidity Sensor] I3[Anemometer for Velocity] I4[Black Globe Thermometer] I5[WBGT Calculator Module] end subgraph PROCESS[PROCESSING UNIT] P1[Microprocessor Controller] P2[Threshold Comparator] P3[Alarm Logic Block] end subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT ACTUATORS] O1[Exhaust Fan Relay] O2[Misting System Valve] O3[Work Cycle Siren] O4[Worker Wristband Vibration Alert] end I1 --> P1 I2 --> P1 I3 --> P1 I4 --> I5 I5 --> P1 P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> O1 P3 --> O2 P3 --> O3 P3 --> O4 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** The block-level architecture above represents an **IoT-enabled real-time thermal stress monitoring system** deployed in modern smart factories. Sensors feed into a central processor that compares measured WBGT against programmable TLV thresholds and triggers engineered responses (fans, misters, alarms) automatically. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define WBGT index. How is it calculated for outdoor and indoor environments? Mention its significance in occupational heat stress monitoring.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** *WBGT (Wet-Bulb Globe Temperature)* is an empirical heat stress index that combines the effects of air temperature, humidity, radiant heat, and wind into a single number representing the **thermal load** experienced by a worker. It is the **standard screening tool** mandated by ISO 7243 and ACGIH for occupational heat stress compliance. - **Outdoor (with solar load):** $WBGT_{out} = 0.7 \cdot T_{nwb} + 0.2 \cdot T_g + 0.1 \cdot T_{db}$ - **Indoor (no solar load):** $WBGT_{in} = 0.7 \cdot T_{nwb} + 0.3 \cdot T_g$ **Significance:** WBGT is measured directly with a WBGT meter, then compared against ACGIH TLV tables adjusted for metabolic rate, work-rest cycle, acclimatization, and clothing. It is the most widely adopted practical tool because it requires minimal instrumentation yet captures the dominant factors affecting human heat exchange. --- **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **Explain the hierarchy of controls for managing thermal stressors in industries. List any four engineering controls for heat stress.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The **hierarchy of controls** prioritizes intervention strategies from most effective (elimination) to least effective (PPE). For thermal stressors, the order is: 1. **Elimination:** Remove the hot/cold process entirely (e.g., substitute manual pouring with robotic pouring). 2. **Substitution:** Replace hazardous processes with safer alternatives (e.g., water-based instead of molten-metal cooling). 3. **Engineering Controls:** Modify the physical environment — four examples: - **General / Local Exhaust Ventilation** to remove hot, contaminated air. - **Radiant heat shields / reflective barriers** between furnaces and workers. - **Insulation of hot pipes, vessels, and boilers.** - **Air conditioning / heated air curtains** in cold rooms. 4. **Administrative Controls:** Work-rest cycles, hydration schedules, shift rotation, acclimatization protocols. 5. **PPE:** Cooling vests for heat; insulated parkas for cold; reflective aprons for radiant exposure. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **(a)** [7 Marks] **Explain in detail the human heat balance equation. Describe the significance of each term and how clothing insulation modifies the equation.** **(b)** [7 Marks] **An indoor foundry has the following data: T_db = 36 °C, T_nwb = 28 °C, T_g = 42 °C. Workers perform heavy work (metabolic rate = 360 W/m²) for 50% of the hour with 50% rest, wearing standard summer clothing. Check compliance against ACGIH TLV. If exceeded, recommend two engineering and two administrative controls.** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Human Heat Balance Equation [7 Marks]** The human body maintains thermal equilibrium through the **heat balance equation**, a first-principles thermodynamic model based on the First Law of Thermodynamics applied to the body as a control volume: $$S = (M - W) - E_{sk} - E_{res} - C_{res} \pm C \pm R$$ (Equivalent forms are acceptable in KTU valuation.) **Term-by-Term Explanation (1 Mark Each):** - **S — Heat Storage:** Net imbalance. If S > 0, body temperature rises (hyperthermia); if S < 0, body temperature falls (hypothermia). - **M — Metabolic Heat Production:** Energy from carbohydrate/fat oxidation. Ranges from 100 W (rest) to 1,000 W (heavy labor). - **W — External Work:** Useful mechanical work output. Typically 0 to 20% of M. - **E_sk — Evaporative Heat Loss from Skin:** Latent heat removed by sweat evaporation. Most important active cooling mechanism. - **E_res — Evaporative Respiratory Loss:** Heat lost from humidifying inspired air. - **C_res — Convective Respiratory Loss:** Heat lost warming inspired air to body temperature. - **C — Convective Heat Exchange:** Heat transfer between skin surface and ambient air via forced/natural convection. - **R — Radiative Heat Exchange:** Heat transfer between skin and surrounding surfaces via infrared radiation. **Effect of Clothing (2 Marks):** Clothing acts as a **thermal resistance layer** between the skin and the environment. The clothing area factor **f_cl** ranges from 1.0 (nude) to 1.2 (typical work clothes). The convective and radiative terms are modified as: $$C = h_c \cdot f_{cl} \cdot (T_{sk} - T_a) \quad \text{and} \quad R = h_r \cdot f_{cl} \cdot (T_{sk} - T_{mrt})$$ The evaporative term is also reduced by the clothing's **vapor permeability index (i_m)**. Clothing insulation is measured in **clo** units (1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W). Typical summer work clothes = **0.6 clo**; winter outdoor = **1.5 to 3.0 clo**; firefighting bunker gear = **> 4 clo** (extreme insulation → high heat strain risk). --- **Part (b) — Numerical Problem [7 Marks]** **Step 1 (1 Mark): Calculate WBGT (Indoor formula):** $$\begin{aligned} WBGT_{in} &= 0.7 \cdot T_{nwb} + 0.3 \cdot T_g \\ &= 0.7 \cdot (28) + 0.3 \cdot (42) \\ &= 19.6 + 12.6 \\ &= 32.2 \, °C \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 (1 Mark): Look up ACGIH TLV.** For **Heavy Work (360 W/m²) at 50% work / 50% rest (acclimatized, standard clothing)**, the TLV ceiling is **27.9 °C**. **Step 3 (1 Mark): Compliance Check:** $$WBGT_{measured} = 32.2 \, °C \quad > \quad TLV = 27.9 \, °C$$ **Heat exposure EXCEEDS the TLV by 4.3 °C → NON-COMPLIANT.** **Step 4 (2 Marks): Recommended Engineering Controls:** 1. **Install canopy hoods with Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** above furnaces to capture convective and radiant heat at source; design capture velocity ≥ 0.5 m/s. 2. **Erect reflective radiant barriers (aluminum sheets)** between furnaces and workstations to reduce Tmrt incident on workers. **Step 5 (2 Marks): Recommended Administrative Controls:** 1. **Reduce work cycle to 25% work / 75% rest** — this raises permissible WBGT to 30 °C; combined with cooling rest area, can bring exposure below threshold. 2. **Implement a 14-day acclimatization program** (gradually increasing exposure) and enforce **mandatory hydration** of 1 L/hour with electrolyte supplementation. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** [7 Marks] **Discuss cold stress in occupational settings. Explain the Wind Chill Index, its calculation, and the associated health effects (hypothermia, frostbite, trench foot).** **(b)** [7 Marks] **List and briefly explain the various heat stress indices used in occupational health (WBGT, ET, HSI, ISO 7933 SW_req). Compare their applicability.** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Cold Stress [7 Marks]** **Definition (1 Mark):** Cold stress occurs when skin temperature drops significantly due to convective, conductive, and radiative heat loss to a cold environment, overwhelming the body's metabolic heat production. **Wind Chill Index (3 Marks):** The WCI is the "feels-like" temperature that combines air temperature and wind speed. Calculated as: $$WCI = T_a + 0.45 - \sqrt{V} \cdot (0.45 + 0.111 \cdot (T_a + 33)) + 0.45 \cdot \sqrt{V}$$ (Ta in °C, V in km/h) **Health Effects of Cold Stress (3 Marks):** - **Hypothermia:** Core body temperature drops below 35 °C. Symptoms progress from shivering (mild, 32–35 °C) to confusion, loss of coordination, and cardiac arrhythmia (severe, <28 °C). It is **immediately life-threatening**. - **Frostbite:** Freezing of skin and subcutaneous tissues. Classified as: - **First-degree (Frostnip):** Numbness, white patches, reversible. - **Second-degree:** Blistering, clear fluid. - **Third-degree:** Hemorrhagic blisters, tissue damage. - **Fourth-degree:** Full-thickness damage including muscle and bone; may require amputation. - **Trench Foot (Immersion Foot):** Non-freezing cold injury from prolonged exposure to wet, cold (0–15 °C) conditions. Causes vasoconstriction, tissue ischemia, and gangrene. - **Chilblains (Pernio):** Inflammation of small blood vessels after cold exposure; itchy, red patches on fingers/toes. - **Cold Urticaria:** Allergic skin reaction to cold exposure. **PPE for Cold (included for context):** Insulated parkas, thermal gloves, balaclavas, insulated boots, hand/toe warmers. Buddy system mandatory. --- **Part (b) — Heat Stress Indices [7 Marks]** **WBGT (2 Marks):** Empirical, simple, requires 3 thermometers. Best for screening and statutory compliance (ACGIH/ISO). Limitation: no metabolic input, so requires separate work-rate assessment. **Effective Temperature (ET) (1 Mark):** Combines T_db, T_wb, and air velocity into a single comfort number from a nomograph. Now largely obsolete but historically important (1923, Houghton & Yaglou). **Heat Stress Index (HSI) (2 Marks):** Proposed by Belding & Hatch (1959). Compares required evaporative cooling (E_req) with maximum possible evaporative cooling (E_max): $$HSI = \frac{E_{req}}{E_{max}} \times 100 \, \%$$ If HSI > 100 %, body cannot dissipate metabolic heat → heat stroke inevitable. Useful for engineering design but requires detailed data. **Required Sweat Rate (SW_req) — ISO 7933 (2 Marks):** Predicts the sweat rate the body must produce to maintain thermal equilibrium: $$SW_{req} = \frac{(M - W) - E_{res} - C - R}{r}$$ Also predicts **maximum allowable exposure time (D_{lim})** and **recovery time**. It is the most comprehensive analytical tool for detailed occupational heat strain evaluation. Limitation: requires instrumentation for all six thermal variables. **Comparative Summary (KTU Exam-Ready Table):** | Index | Inputs Required | Best Use Case | |---|---|---| | WBGT | 3 temperatures | Quick statutory screening | | ET | T_db, T_wb, V | Historical/comfort | | HSI | M, V, T_a, RH | Engineering design | | SW_req | All 6 variables | Detailed strain prediction | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Forgetting to multiply by WBGT coefficients:** A common error is adding T_db + T_nwb + T_g without weighting them by 0.7/0.2/0.1. **Loss of 2 marks.** > 2. **Using the outdoor formula for indoor environments:** The indoor formula omits the 0.1·T_db term. Mixing them up is a frequent mistake. **Loss of 1 mark.** > 3. **Ignoring acclimatization:** ACGIH TLVs are for acclimatized workers. For unacclimatized workers, subtract **2.5 °C** from the TLV ceiling. **Loss of 1–2 marks.** > 4. **Confusing WBGT with Heat Index:** The Heat Index used in weather forecasts (NOAA) is different from occupational WBGT — it uses only T_db and RH, not globe temperature. **Loss of 1 mark.** > 5. **Forgetting unit conversion in clo:** Always remember **1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W**. Students often state the value without specifying the unit. **Loss of 1 mark.** > 6. **In heat balance equation, sign conventions:** Convection and radiation can be either loss or gain depending on whether skin is warmer or cooler than the environment. Always state sign explicitly. **Loss of 1 mark.** --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Thermal stressors** are environmental conditions that disrupt the body's core temperature of **37 °C**, leading to heat stress (heat gain) or cold stress (heat loss). - The **six fundamental variables** governing the thermal environment are: T_a, T_mrt, V, RH, M, I_cl. - The **human heat balance equation** is: $S = (M - W) \pm C \pm R - E$ - **WBGT (Indoor)** = $0.7 \cdot T_{nwb} + 0.3 \cdot T_g$ - **WBGT (Outdoor)** = $0.7 \cdot T_{nwb} + 0.2 \cdot T_g + 0.1 \cdot T_{db}$ - **DuBois body surface area:** $A_D = 0.202 \cdot W^{0.425} \cdot H^{0.725}$ (typical = **2.0 m²**) - **Convective coefficient (still air):** $h_c = 2.38 \cdot \vert \Delta T \vert^{0.25}$ - **Convective coefficient (forced):** $h_c = 10.4 \cdot \sqrt{V}$ - **Clothing unit:** **1 clo = 0.155 m²·K/W** - **Wind Chill Index formula:** $WCI = T_a + 0.45 - \sqrt{V} \cdot (0.45 + 0.111 \cdot (T_a + 33)) + 0.45 \cdot \sqrt{V}$ - **ACGIH TLV ceilings (acclimatized):** 30 / 26.7 / 25 °C for Light / Moderate / Heavy continuous work. - For **unacclimatized workers**, reduce TLV ceiling by **2.5 °C**. - **Hierarchy of controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE. - **Heat-related illnesses:** Heat rash → Heat cramps → Heat exhaustion → **Heat stroke (fatal)**. - **Cold-related illnesses:** Chilblains → Trench foot → Frostbite → **Hypothermia (fatal)**. - **HSI** = (E_req / E_max) × 100; if **> 100 %**, body cannot maintain equilibrium. - **ISO 7933 SW_req** predicts required sweat rate and maximum exposure duration. - **Latent heat of vaporization of sweat:** **2,430 kJ/kg**. - **Indian regulatory references:** Factories Act 1948 (Section 36), Indian Mines Rules (Regulation 35), OSHA-NIOSH thermal standards, ACGIH TLVs, ISO 7243, ISO 7933. - **Acclimatization:** A **14-day progressive exposure program** reduces heat strain and is mandatory under ACGIH guidelines for new workers in hot environments. - **Hydration rule of thumb:** Workers in hot environments should drink **~ 1 L/hr** of cool water with electrolytes.

topics.title: Ventilation Investigation and Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET832 - OCCUPATIONAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ventilation Investigation and Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ventilation Investigation and Analysis** refers to the systematic engineering evaluation of workplace air movement systems to determine whether the air quality, contaminant concentration, and thermal environment comply with occupational safety standards (such as those prescribed by ACGIH, OSHA, BIS, and the Indian Factories Act).

### Formal Definition

**Industrial Ventilation** is the engineering technique of controlling the workplace environment by either supplying fresh air or removing contaminated air using dilution, general exhaust, or *local exhaust ventilation (LEV)*. **Ventilation Investigation and Analysis** is the diagnostic procedure used to:

1. Quantify the contaminant generation rate.
2. Measure the existing airflow rates (supply, exhaust, and makeup air).
3. Analyze the efficiency of hoods, ducts, fans, and air-cleaning devices.
4. Recommend corrective redesign of the ventilation system.

The governing equation of any ventilation system is the **contaminant mass balance**:

$$G = Q \times C$$

where $G$ is the contaminant generation rate (mg/s), $Q$ is the airflow rate (m³/s), and $C$ is the contaminant concentration (mg/m³).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Kitchen Chimney"** 🍳
> Imagine your mother frying *puris* in the kitchen. The smoke spreads everywhere because there is no chimney (no exhaust). Now imagine installing an exhaust hood right above the stove — the smoke is captured *at the source* before it spreads. That is **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)**. Now imagine opening all windows in the house to let fresh air dilute the smoke — that is **Dilution/General Ventilation**. Ventilation investigation is like a "doctor" checking whether your chimney (hood) is working well enough to keep the kitchen (workplace) healthy.

### Why Investigation is Required

> [!NOTE]
> - To verify compliance with **Threshold Limit Values (TLV)** and **Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL)**.
> - To identify leaks, blockages, or improper hood placement.
> - To optimize energy consumption — a poorly designed duct may consume **40% more fan power** than necessary.
> - To validate the engineering control hierarchy (substitution → engineering → administrative → PPE).

### Key Physical Constants & Standards

- **Standard air density**: $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (at 20 °C, 101.325 kPa).
- **Standard face velocity for fume hoods**: **0.5 m/s**.
- **Minimum transport velocity** in ducts: **7.5 to 10 m/s** for dusts.
- **Recommended Air Changes per Hour (ACH)** for general industry: **6 to 15**.
- **ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual (30th Ed.)** is the KTU-recommended reference.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Ventilation Systems

| **Type** | **Mechanism** | **Best Used For** | **Effectiveness** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dilution Ventilation** | Adds clean air to lower contaminant concentration | Low-toxicity, low-generation-rate contaminants | Low (1:10 ratio) |
| **General Exhaust Ventilation** | Removes air uniformly from the room | Heat, vapors, low-toxicity gases | Medium |
| **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** | Captures contaminant at source with hood + duct + fan + cleaner | Dusts, fumes, vapors, highly toxic substances | **High (99% capture)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **hierarchy of effectiveness** in industrial hygiene controls places **LEV at the top of engineering controls**, ahead of dilution ventilation, because LEV acts *at the source* before contaminants enter the breathing zone.

### 2.2 Components of a Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) System

1. **Hood** — the entry point that captures the contaminant (enclosing, exterior, or receiving hood).
2. **Duct** — the transport pathway that conveys contaminated air.
3. **Air Cleaner** — removes particulates/gases (cyclone, baghouse, scrubber, electrostatic precipitator).
4. **Fan** — provides the energy to move air (typically a centrifugal fan for LEV).
5. **Stack/Discharge** — releases cleaned air to the atmosphere above roof level.

### 2.3 Key Engineering Parameters

- **Capture Velocity ($V_c$)** — the air velocity required at the emission point to overcome the opposing air currents and draw the contaminant into the hood. ACGIH specifies values from **0.25 to 2.5 m/s** depending on the release condition.
- **Face Velocity ($V_f$)** — air velocity entering the hood opening (typical fume hood: **0.5 m/s**).
- **Transport Velocity ($V_t$)** — minimum duct velocity to prevent settling of contaminants (typically **7.5–25 m/s**).
- **Duct Velocity Pressure ($V_p$)** — given by $V_p = \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$.
- **Static Pressure (SP)** — pressure exerted by air perpendicular to flow direction (causes friction and fitting losses).
- **Total Pressure (TP)** — sum of static and velocity pressures: $TP = SP + V_p$.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **frequently tested in KTU End Semester Examinations**. Memorize the variables, units, and application context.

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = A \times v$ | Volume flow rate = Area × velocity | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q = \frac{V \times ACH}{60}$ | ACH-based flow rate | m³/s |
| 3 | $G = Q \times C$ | Contaminant generation = flow × concentration | mg/s |
| 4 | $V_p = \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$ | Velocity pressure | Pa |
| 5 | $\Delta P_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot \rho v^2}{2D}$ | Friction loss in duct (Darcy) | Pa |
| 6 | $H_{total} = \Delta P_f + \Sigma \Delta P_{fittings} + V_{p,exit}$ | Total system pressure | Pa |
| 7 | $\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Fan Law 1 — Flow proportional to RPM | — |
| 8 | $\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Fan Law 2 — Pressure proportional to RPM² | — |
| 9 | $\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | Fan Law 3 — Power proportional to RPM³ | — |
| 10 | $V_c = \frac{Q}{10 \cdot x^2 \cdot A}$ | Capture velocity for a flanged hood at distance $x$ | m/s |
| 11 | $\eta_{hood} = \frac{V_c}{V_f}$ | Hood capture efficiency (ratio) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $C = \frac{G}{Q}$ | Resulting contaminant concentration | mg/m³ |

> Where:
> - $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless, from Moody chart).
> - $L$ = duct length (m).
> - $D$ = duct diameter/hydraulic diameter (m).
> - $\rho$ = air density = **1.2 kg/m³**.
> - $N$ = fan rotational speed (RPM).
> - $H$ = fan pressure (Pa).
> - $P$ = fan power (W).

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Foundries & Welding shops**: Use LEV to capture metal fumes (Mn, Cr, Ni) below TLV.
- **Pharma industry**: HEPA-filtered ventilation maintains ISO Class 7/8 cleanrooms.
- **Tunnels & underground mines**: Dilution ventilation + auxiliary LEV for diesel particulates (DPM) and methane.
- **Hospital OTs**: Laminar airflow ventilation maintains 20–25 ACH with HEPA filtration.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Capture Velocity for a Flanged Round Hood

Consider a flanged (with a flange) round hood of area $A$ placed at a distance $x$ from a point source. By applying **conservation of mass** to a hemispherical air envelope of radius $r$:

**Step 1 — Setup of the Control Volume**

The volumetric flow $Q$ entering the hood equals the total flow crossing any hemispherical surface centered on the source:

$$Q = A_{hemisphere} \times v(r) = 2 \pi r^2 \cdot v(r)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the Local Velocity at Radius $r$**

$$v(r) = \frac{Q}{2 \pi r^2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $r = x$ for the Capture Point**

$$v(x) = \frac{Q}{2 \pi x^2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Empirical ACGIH Correction for Flanged Hoods**

The ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual introduces a constant (typically $10$) to convert from the theoretical hemisphere to the actual hood geometry:

$$V_c = \frac{Q}{10 \cdot x^2 \cdot A}$$

$$\boxed{V_c = \frac{Q}{10 \, x^2 \, A}}$$

This is the **canonical capture velocity formula** tested in KTU examinations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Friction Loss in a Straight Duct (Darcy-Weisbach)

The pressure drop due to friction in a straight duct of length $L$, diameter $D$, with air velocity $v$ and density $\rho$:

**Step 1 — Begin with Friction Force per Unit Surface Area**

The wall shear stress $\tau_w$ produces a pressure drop over the duct length:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{4 \tau_w L}{D}$$

**Step 2 — Define the Friction Factor**

Using the Darcy friction factor $f$ (4 × Fanning factor), the wall shear stress relates to the dynamic pressure:

$$\tau_w = f \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{8}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Back**

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{4 L}{D} \cdot f \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{8}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot v^2}{2 D}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute Numerical Example**

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example:** A duct of diameter $D = 0.3 \text{ m}$, length $L = 20 \text{ m}$, carries air at velocity $v = 15 \text{ m/s}$. For $Re \approx 3 \times 10^5$, the friction factor from the Moody chart is $f = 0.018$. Take $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

Step 1 — Substitute values:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{0.018 \times 20 \times 1.2 \times (15)^2}{2 \times 0.3}$$

Step 2 — Square the velocity term:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{0.018 \times 20 \times 1.2 \times 225}{0.6}$$

Step 3 — Multiply numerator stepwise:

$$0.018 \times 20 = 0.36$$
$$0.36 \times 1.2 = 0.432$$
$$0.432 \times 225 = 97.2$$

Step 4 — Divide by $0.6$:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{97.2}{0.6} = 162 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_f = 162 \text{ Pa}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Fan Laws — From First Principles

The fan laws are derived from the principle of **dynamic similarity** (same fluid, same Reynolds regime) for a geometrically similar fan operating at different speeds.

**Step 1 — Flow rate scales linearly with impeller diameter $D$ and speed $N$:**

$$Q \propto D^3 N \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^3 \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

For geometrically similar fans ($D_1 = D_2$):

$$\boxed{\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}}$$

**Step 2 — Pressure scales with $\rho$, $D^2$, and $N^2$:**

$$H \propto \rho D^2 N^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$$

For the same fan:

$$\boxed{\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Power scales with $\rho$, $D^5$, and $N^3$:**

$$P \propto \rho D^5 N^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy insight:** Doubling the fan speed **triples the flow** but **multiplies power by 8**. This is why KTU stresses fan selection at *design point*, not oversizing.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Ventilation Investigation Procedure

| **Step** | **Activity** | **Instrument / Method** | **Outcome / Output** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Workplace Survey | Walk-through, process mapping | Identify contaminant sources |
| 2 | Sampling | Personal sampling pumps, impingers, filters | TWA / STEL exposure data |
| 3 | Airflow Measurement | Pitot tube, anemometer, velometer, smoke tube | $Q$ in m³/s |
| 4 | Pressure Mapping | Manometer, Magnehelic gauge | $\Delta P$ across hood/duct/fan |
| 5 | Velocity Profiling | Hot-wire anemometer | Velocity profile & uniformity |
| 6 | Hood Performance | Capture velocity test | Hood efficiency $\eta_{hood}$ |
| 7 | Duct Integrity | Smoke pencil, DP test | Leak detection |
| 8 | Fan Performance | Tachometer, ammeter | Fan laws verification |
| 9 | Cleaner Efficiency | Inlet-outlet concentration | Collection efficiency $\eta_c$ |
| 10 | Compliance Reporting | Compare with TLV/PEL | Pass / Fail / Redesign |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Ventilation Investigation

The diagram below illustrates how a ventilation investigator traces airflow and contaminants through the system, identifying measurement points and decision nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Hazard Source Identification"] --> B["Contaminant Characterization"]
    B --> C["Select Ventilation Type"]
    C --> D1["Local Exhaust Ventilation"]
    C --> D2["Dilution Ventilation"]
    C --> D3["General Exhaust Ventilation"]
    D1 --> E1["Measure Hood Capture Velocity"]
    D2 --> E2["Compute ACH and Air Distribution"]
    D3 --> E3["Measure Room Air Change Rate"]
    E1 --> F1["Pitot Tube and Anemometer Reading"]
    E2 --> F2["Tracer Gas Method"]
    E3 --> F3["Hot Wire Anemometer Scan"]
    F1 --> G["Compute Volume Flow Rate Q"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H["Pressure Mapping across Ducts"]
    H --> I["Fan Performance Verification"]
    I --> J["Air Cleaner Efficiency Check"]
    J --> K["Compare with TLV and PEL"]
    K --> L{Compliant?}
    L -- Yes --> M["Document and Monitor"]
    L -- No --> N["Redesign Hood or Fan"]
    N --> C
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Pressure Components Along a Duct Run

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Hood Entry TP = 250 Pa"] --> B["Straight Duct Loss 80 Pa"]
    B --> C["Elbow Loss 40 Pa"]
    C --> D["Air Cleaner 100 Pa"]
    D --> E["Fan adds 200 Pa"]
    E --> F["Discharge to Atmosphere TP = 0"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each block represents a pressure *change* in Pascals. The fan must add pressure equal to **all losses combined** to maintain the design airflow.

### 4.3 Decision Matrix — When to Choose Each Ventilation Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Contaminant Identified"] --> Q1{"Highly Toxic or Carcinogenic?"}
    Q1 -- Yes --> LEV["Use LEV with HEPA/Activated Carbon"]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Generated at Fixed Point?"}
    Q2 -- Yes --> LEV
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Heat or Low-Toxicity Vapor?"}
    Q3 -- Yes --> GV["Use General Ventilation 10 to 15 ACH"]
    Q3 -- No --> DV["Use Dilution Ventilation"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading guidance:** The decision tree above is the standard algorithm followed in **ACGIH Chapter 3.4** and is directly aligned with KTU Module 3 outcome *"Evaluate the appropriateness of ventilation strategies"*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV). List any four components of an LEV system.*

**Model Answer:**

Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) is an engineering control system that captures airborne contaminants **at or near the source** of generation before they enter the worker's breathing zone, and conveys them through ducts to an air-cleaning device and exhaust fan before discharging to atmosphere.

Four components of an LEV system:

1. **Hood** — capture point (enclosing, exterior, or receiving).
2. **Ductwork** — transport pathway.
3. **Air Cleaner** — cyclone, baghouse, scrubber, ESP, or HEPA.
4. **Fan (Blower)** — provides motive energy.
5. **Discharge stack** — releases cleaned air safely.

*(Any four → 2 marks; definition → 1 mark)*

---

**A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*State the three Fan Laws and explain their practical significance.*

**Model Answer:**

$$\text{Fan Law 1: } \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2} \quad\quad \text{Fan Law 2: } \frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2 \quad\quad \text{Fan Law 3: } \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$$

**Significance:** Doubling fan RPM **triples** airflow, **quadruples** pressure, but **multiplies power by 8**. Therefore, fan speed selection directly controls energy consumption — operating fans at design point saves **up to 60% energy** compared to throttling via dampers. *(3 marks)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice — Answer ANY ONE)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A grinding operation generates **120 mg/s** of aluminium dust. The exhaust hood is located **1.2 m** from the source and has a face area of **0.15 m²**. The ACGIH capture velocity requirement is **0.5 m/s**. Determine:

(i) The required volume flow rate $Q$.
(ii) The duct diameter for a transport velocity of **18 m/s**.
(iii) The duct friction loss over **30 m** straight duct (assume $f = 0.02$).

**(b) [7 Marks)** Describe with a neat sketch the procedure of a **ventilation investigation** in a foundry. Include at least 6 measurement points and explain how capture velocity is verified using a **smoke tube**.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Required volume flow rate:**

$$V_c = \frac{Q}{10 \cdot x^2 \cdot A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q = V_c \cdot 10 \cdot x^2 \cdot A$$

Step 1 — Substitute values:

$$Q = 0.5 \times 10 \times (1.2)^2 \times 0.15$$

Step 2 — Square the distance:

$$(1.2)^2 = 1.44$$

Step 3 — Multiply all terms:

$$Q = 0.5 \times 10 \times 1.44 \times 0.15 = 1.08 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 1.08 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**(ii) Duct diameter for transport velocity $v_t = 18$ m/s:**

$$Q = A \cdot v_t = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v_t \quad \Rightarrow \quad D = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v_t}}$$

Step 1 — Substitute:

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.08}{\pi \times 18}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.32}{56.55}}$$

Step 2 — Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{4.32}{56.55} = 0.0764$$

Step 3 — Take the square root:

$$D = \sqrt{0.0764} = 0.276 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 0.276 \text{ m or 276 mm}}$$

**(iii) Friction loss over 30 m duct:**

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{f \cdot L \cdot \rho \cdot v_t^2}{2 D}$$

Step 1 — Substitute $v_t = 18$, $L = 30$, $D = 0.276$, $f = 0.02$, $\rho = 1.2$:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{0.02 \times 30 \times 1.2 \times (18)^2}{2 \times 0.276}$$

Step 2 — Square velocity:

$$(18)^2 = 324$$

Step 3 — Multiply numerator:

$$0.02 \times 30 = 0.6$$
$$0.6 \times 1.2 = 0.72$$
$$0.72 \times 324 = 233.28$$

Step 4 — Denominator:

$$2 \times 0.276 = 0.552$$

Step 5 — Final division:

$$\Delta P_f = \frac{233.28}{0.552} = 422.6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_f \approx 422.6 \text{ Pa}}$$

#### Part (b) — Ventilation Investigation in a Foundry

**Procedure (6 measurement points):**

1. **Hood face** — measure face velocity using anemometer at 5 points and average.
2. **Duct entry** — Pitot traverse to find duct velocity pressure and compute $Q$.
3. **Duct midpoint** — static pressure tap + manometer → friction loss per meter.
4. **Before air cleaner** — measure dust loading using isokinetic sampling.
5. **After air cleaner** — measure outlet dust loading to find collection efficiency.
6. **Fan outlet** — tachometer (RPM) + ammeter (kW) to verify fan law compliance.

**Capture velocity verification using smoke tube:**

A **smoke tube** (e.g., Drager, Sensidyne) emits a neutral-density white smoke. The investigator releases the smoke at the contaminant generation point and observes whether the smoke stream is **drawn into the hood within 2–3 seconds**. If the smoke escapes or stagnates, capture velocity is inadequate and the hood must be repositioned closer, flared, or the fan speed increased.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **hierarchy of ventilation controls**. Compare the effectiveness, capital cost, and energy consumption of LEV versus dilution ventilation for a welding shop emitting **8 mg/s** of manganese fumes.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the help of a sketch, describe the working of a **centrifugal fan**. State the three fan laws and a numerical example illustrating that doubling fan RPM increases power consumption **8-fold**.

---

### Model Solution — Question B (Highlights)

#### Part (a) — Hierarchy of Ventilation Controls

Order of preference (highest first):
1. **Elimination** — stop the process.
2. **Substitution** — replace toxic material.
3. **Engineering Controls** — LEV is the most common.
4. **Administrative Controls** — shift rotation, training.
5. **PPE** — respirators (last resort).

**Comparison Table:**

| **Parameter** | **LEV** | **Dilution Ventilation** |
|---|---|---|
| Capture point | At source | Whole room |
| Effectiveness | 90–99% | 10–30% |
| Capital cost | High (₹5–15 Lakh) | Low (₹1–3 Lakh) |
| Energy | Low (focused) | Very high (large $Q$) |
| Worker protection | Direct | Indirect |

For Mn fumes (TLV-TWA = **0.5 mg/m³**), only LEV can practically achieve compliance. Dilution would require **>16 m³/s** of fresh air.

#### Part (b) — Centrifugal Fan and Fan Laws

**Centrifugal Fan Description:**
- Air enters axially through the **inlet (eye)**.
- The **impeller** (backward-curved, forward-curved, or radial blades) accelerates the air radially.
- Air is discharged into a **scroll casing** that converts velocity energy to static pressure.

**Numerical Example for Fan Law 3:**

Given: $N_1 = 1000$ RPM, $P_1 = 5$ kW. If $N_2 = 2000$ RPM:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{2000}{1000}\right)^3 = 2^3 = 8$$

$$\boxed{P_2 = 8 \times 5 = 40 \text{ kW}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common mistake 1:** Students often use the formula $V_c = \frac{Q}{2\pi x^2}$ (theoretical hemisphere) instead of the **ACGIH-corrected** $V_c = \frac{Q}{10 x^2 A}$. The constant 10 includes the flange correction — using $2\pi \approx 6.28$ gives a non-standard answer.
> 2. **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert duct diameter from mm to m before substituting into the Darcy equation. A 300 mm duct must be entered as **0.3 m**, not 300.
> 3. **Common mistake 3:** In Fan Law problems, students mix up which variable scales linearly, quadratically, or cubically. Memorize the trick: **Q ∝ N¹, H ∝ N², P ∝ N³**.
> 4. **Common mistake 4:** In ACH problems, forgetting to divide by 60 when converting between **per hour** and **per minute**.
> 5. **Common mistake 5:** Failing to include the **exit velocity pressure** term in total system pressure — this alone accounts for **15–20% of fan power** in low-loss systems.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 3 — Ventilation Investigation and Analysis):**

- **Ventilation Types:** Dilution < General Exhaust < **Local Exhaust (best)**.
- **Core Formula:** $G = Q \times C$ — generation = flow × concentration.
- **Capture Velocity Formula:** $V_c = \dfrac{Q}{10 \, x^2 \, A}$ (ACGIH standard).
- **Air Changes per Hour:** $Q = \dfrac{V \times ACH}{60}$ with $V$ in m³.
- **Friction Loss (Darcy):** $\Delta P_f = \dfrac{f L \rho v^2}{2 D}$.
- **Fan Laws:** Flow ∝ N¹, Pressure ∝ N², Power ∝ **N³**.
- **Recommended minimum transport velocity:** 7.5 m/s for vapors; **15–25 m/s** for dusts.
- **Standard air density:** 1.2 kg/m³ (use this unless specified otherwise).
- **Investigation Tools:** Pitot tube, anemometer, smoke tube, manometer, hot-wire probe.
- **Investigation Sequence:** Survey → Sampling → Flow measurement → Pressure mapping → Fan check → Compliance verification.
- **Standards to Remember:** ACGIH TLV, OSHA PEL, BIS IS 3103, Indian Factories Act Section 14.
- **Hygiene Hierarchy:** Eliminate → Substitute → **Engineering (LEV)** → Administrative → PPE.
- **Fan Power Insight:** Doubling fan speed wastes **8× the electricity** for only **3× the airflow** — always select fans at design point.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Toxicology: Material Safety Data Sheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET832 - OCCUPATIONAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Industrial Toxicology: Material Safety Data Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Toxicology — Formal Definition

**Industrial Toxicology** is the branch of toxicology that deals specifically with the study of the nature, effects, detection, and treatment of poisons arising from industrial chemicals, processes, and by-products encountered in the workplace environment. It is concerned with the adverse effects of chemical, biological, and physical agents on living organisms (humans, animals, plants) exposed in occupational settings.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> Industrial Toxicology is the systematic study of the harmful effects of workplace chemicals. The discipline integrates principles from pharmacology, biology, chemistry, occupational hygiene, and epidemiology to predict, identify, quantify, and control occupational health hazards.

### Sub-disciplines of Industrial Toxicology

- **Clinical Toxicology** — Diagnosis and treatment of poisoning cases in workers.
- **Forensic Toxicology** — Legal investigation of chemical exposures and fatalities.
- **Environmental Toxicology** — Impact of industrial pollutants on ecosystems.
- **Occupational (Industrial) Toxicology** — Workplace-specific exposure assessment and prevention.

### Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Toxic vs. Hazardous** — These terms are often confused but are legally distinct.
> - **Toxic**: Capable of causing injury or death through chemical action (depends on dose).
> - **Hazardous**: Any material posing physical, health, or environmental risk (broader category, includes flammables, reactives, etc.).

| Term | Definition | Unit / Metric |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Toxicant** | A substance that can produce harmful biological effects | — |
| **Toxicity** | Inherent capacity of a substance to cause harm | Qualitative |
| **Hazard** | Probability that a substance causes harm under specific conditions | Qualitative |
| **Risk** | Quantitative measure of hazard × exposure probability | Dimensionless ratio |
| **Dose** | Amount of substance entering the body per unit body weight | **mg/kg body weight** |
| **LD₅₀** | Lethal Dose causing death in 50% of test population | **mg/kg** |
| **LC₅₀** | Lethal Concentration killing 50% of test subjects in air/water | **ppm** or **mg/m³** |
| **TLV** | Threshold Limit Value — airborne concentration for safe exposure | **ppm** or **mg/m³** |
| **PEL** | Permissible Exposure Limit (OSHA legal standard) | **ppm** or **mg/m³** |

## 1.2 Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS / SDS) — Formal Definition

A **Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS)**, now formally referred to as a **Safety Data Sheet (SDS)** under the Globally Harmonized System (GHS), is a standardized 16-section document prepared by the chemical manufacturer or importer that provides comprehensive information regarding the properties, hazards, protective measures, and emergency procedures for a specific chemical product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GHS Mandate (KTU High-Yield)**
> Under GHS adopted by OSHA (Hazard Communication Standard, 29 CFR 1910.1200), every hazardous chemical shipped after June 2015 must have a 16-section SDS. This is a **legally mandated** document, not optional.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Chemical User Manual"**
> Imagine buying a complex power tool. The instruction manual tells you what it does, how to use it safely, what PPE to wear, what to do if it malfunctions, and how to dispose of it properly. An **MSDS/SDS is exactly this** — but for chemicals instead of tools.
>
> Just as you would not operate a chainsaw without reading the manual, an industrial worker **must not handle a chemical without consulting its SDS**. The SDS is the universal language that bridges the chemical manufacturer, the transporter, the employer, and the worker — ensuring that critical safety information is communicated clearly across the entire supply chain.

### Routes of Toxicant Entry into the Body

Think of the human body as a fortress with four possible breaches through which a chemical enemy (toxicant) can enter:

1. **Inhalation (Respiratory Route)** — Most common in industry (~90% of occupational exposures). Gases, vapours, dusts, fumes, and mists enter via the lungs. *Analogy: An unlocked front door.*

2. **Skin Absorption (Dermal Route)** — Direct contact with liquid or soluble chemicals. *Analogy: A cracked window the toxicant slips through.*

3. **Ingestion (Oral Route)** — Eating/drinking in contaminated areas, hand-to-mouth transfer. *Analogy: The back door left open by unsafe habits.*

4. **Injection (Parenteral Route)** — Sharp injuries, splinters contaminated with chemicals. *Analogy: A forced breach — the rarest but most dangerous route.*

## 1.4 Why MSDS/SDS Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU ESE (End Semester Examination), questions on Industrial Toxicology frequently test the student's ability to:
> - **Identify** the 16 sections of an SDS.
> - **Interpret** toxicological data (LD₅₀, LC₅₀, TLV).
> - **Apply** dose-response relationships in workplace safety scenarios.
> - **Recommend** appropriate PPE and first-aid measures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Dose-Response Relationship — The Foundation of Toxicology

The fundamental principle of toxicology is stated by **Paracelsus (1493–1541)**:

> *"Sola dosis facit venenum"* — *"Only the dose makes the poison."*

This means **every substance can be toxic at a high enough dose**, and even the deadliest poisons are harmless at very low doses.

### Mathematical Representation

The dose-response relationship quantifies how the magnitude of a biological response (R) varies with the administered dose (D):

$$
R = f(D)
$$

Where:
- $R$ = biological response (effect magnitude, % mortality, etc.)
- $D$ = dose administered (mg/kg body weight for oral/dermal; mg/m³ or ppm for inhalation)
- $f$ = functional relationship — typically **sigmoidal (S-shaped)** when plotted on a log-dose scale.

### Characteristic Sigmoidal Curve

For most toxicants, plotting response versus $\log_{10}(D)$ yields an **S-shaped curve** with three critical regions:

1. **No-Observed-Adverse-Effect Level (NOAEL)** — Highest dose at which no adverse effect is observed.
2. **Lowest-Observed-Adverse-Effect Level (LOAEL)** — Lowest dose at which an adverse effect becomes statistically detectable.
3. **Lethal Dose 50 (LD₅₀)** — The statistically derived single dose that causes death in 50% of the test population under defined conditions.

## 2.2 Key Toxicological Metrics — Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet** — Memorize these for direct numerical questions in Part A (3 marks).

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit | Application |
|--------|---------------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Dose** | $D = \dfrac{M}{W_b}$ | **mg/kg** | Oral / dermal exposure |
| **Inhalation Concentration (Mass)** | $C_m = \dfrac{M}{V}$ | **mg/m³** | Workplace air quality |
| **Conversion (ppm ↔ mg/m³)** | $C_{m} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$ | **mg/m³** at 25 °C, 1 atm | Gases and vapours |
| **Inverse Conversion** | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{C_{m} \times 24.45}{MW}$ | **ppm** | For TLV comparisons |
| **LD₅₀** | $LD_{50} = \log^{-1}\!\left[\dfrac{\log D_a + \log D_b}{2}\right]$ | **mg/kg** | Acute oral/dermal toxicity |
| **Probit Model** | $\text{Probit}(P) = a + b \log_{10}(D)$ | Dimensionless | Dose-mortality analysis |
| **Risk Quotient** | $HQ = \dfrac{E}{R_f}$ | Dimensionless | Hazard characterization |
| **Safety Factor (Composite)** | $SF = f_1 \cdot f_2 \cdot f_3 \cdot \cdots$ | Dimensionless | Reference Dose derivation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $M$ = mass of substance absorbed
> - $W_b$ = body weight of exposed individual (typically 70 kg for an adult)
> - $MW$ = molecular weight of the substance (g/mol)
> - $24.45$ = molar volume (L/mol) of an ideal gas at 25 °C, 1 atm
> - $E$ = exposure level; $R_f$ = reference dose/concentration
> - $D_a, D_b$ = doses bracketing the 50% mortality point

## 2.3 Classification of Toxicity Based on LD₅₀ (WHO/IPCS Classification)

This table is a **favourite KTU Part A question** (3 marks).

| Class | Oral LD₅₀ in Rats (mg/kg) | Dermal LD₅₀ in Rabbits (mg/kg) | Inhalation LC₅₀ (mg/L/4h) | Pictogram |
|-------|--------------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Class Ia — Extremely Hazardous** | $\leq 5$ | $\leq 50$ | $\leq 0.2$ | Skull & Crossbones (red) |
| **Class Ib — Highly Hazardous** | $5 - 50$ | $50 - 200$ | $0.2 - 2$ | Skull & Crossbones (red) |
| **Class II — Moderately Hazardous** | $50 - 2000$ | $200 - 1000$ | $2 - 20$ | Exclamation Mark |
| **Class III — Slightly Hazardous** | $> 2000$ | $> 1000$ | $> 20$ | No symbol |
| **Class IV — Practically Non-toxic** | $> 5000$ | $> 5000$ | — | No symbol |

## 2.4 Categories of Toxic Effects

### Based on Onset & Duration

- **Acute Toxicity** — Adverse effects occurring within **24 hours** of a single or short-term exposure (e.g., chlorine gas poisoning).
- **Sub-acute Toxicity** — Effects from repeated exposure over **1 month (28–90 days)**.
- **Chronic Toxicity** — Effects from prolonged/repeated exposure over **> 3 months**, often at low doses (e.g., asbestosis, lead poisoning).
- **Carcinogenicity** — Capacity to induce cancer (e.g., benzene → leukemia).
- **Mutagenicity** — Capacity to cause genetic mutations.
- **Teratogenicity** — Capacity to cause fetal malformations (e.g., thalidomide).

### Based on Site of Action

- **Local Toxicants** — Action at the site of contact (e.g., corrosive acids on skin).
- **Systemic Toxicants** — Require absorption and distribution to a target organ (e.g., lead → CNS, kidneys).
- **Hepatotoxicants** — Liver damage (e.g., carbon tetrachloride, vinyl chloride).
- **Nephrotoxicants** — Kidney damage (e.g., cadmium, mercury).
- **Neurotoxicants** — Nervous system damage (e.g., manganese, n-hexane).
- **Pulmonotoxicants** — Lung damage (e.g., silica, asbestos).

## 2.5 The 16 Sections of an SDS (GHS Format) — Complete Reference

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Memorize the **section numbers** and the **information content**. Past KTU papers have asked students to identify which section contains a specific piece of data.

| Section | Title | Key Content |
|---------|-------|-------------|
| **1** | Identification | Product name, manufacturer, emergency contact, recommended uses |
| **2** | Hazard(s) Identification | GHS classification, signal word (Danger/Warning), pictograms, hazard statements |
| **3** | Composition / Ingredients | Chemical name, CAS number, concentration, impurities |
| **4** | First-Aid Measures | Inhalation, skin, eye, ingestion procedures + symptoms |
| **5** | Fire-Fighting Measures | Suitable extinguishing media, specific hazards, protective equipment for firefighters |
| **6** | Accidental Release | Personal precautions, environmental precautions, containment, cleanup methods |
| **7** | Handling and Storage | Precautions for safe handling, incompatible materials, storage conditions |
| **8** | Exposure Controls / PPE | TLV/PEL/REL values, engineering controls, PPE specifications |
| **9** | Physical / Chemical Properties | Appearance, odour, pH, melting/boiling point, flash point, vapour pressure |
| **10** | Stability and Reactivity | Chemical stability, conditions to avoid, incompatible materials, hazardous decomposition products |
| **11** | Toxicological Information | LD₅₀, LC₅₀, routes of exposure, symptoms, carcinogenicity data |
| **12** | Ecological Information | Aquatic toxicity, persistence, bioaccumulation potential |
| **13** | Disposal Considerations | Waste treatment methods, contaminated packaging |
| **14** | Transport Information | UN number, transport hazard class, packing group |
| **15** | Regulatory Information | Safety/health/environmental regulations specific to the chemical |
| **16** | Other Information | Preparation date, revision history, key abbreviations |

### GHS Pictograms — Must Memorize

| Pictogram | Symbol | Hazard Class |
|-----------|--------|--------------|
| **GHS01** | Exploding bomb | Explosives |
| **GHS02** | Flame | Flammables |
| **GHS03** | Flame over circle | Oxidizers |
| **GHS04** | Gas cylinder | Gases under pressure |
| **GHS05** | Corrosion | Corrosives |
| **GHS06** | Skull & crossbones | Acute toxicity (fatal/toxic) |
| **GHS07** | Exclamation mark | Irritant / harmful |
| **GHS08** | Health hazard | Carcinogen / mutagen / sensitizer |
| **GHS09** | Environment | Aquatic toxicity |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding industrial toxicology and SDS interpretation is critical for:

- **Process Safety Engineers** — Designing inherently safer plants (As Low As Reasonably Practicable — ALARP principle).
- **Industrial Hygienists** — Conducting exposure assessments using TLV/PEL data.
- **HSE Managers** — Preparing Chemical Safety Risk Assessments (CSRA) and Hazard & Operability (HAZOP) studies.
- **Emergency Responders** — Knowing immediate first-aid and spill-control measures from SDS Section 4 & 6.
- **Procurement Engineers** — Selecting less-toxic substitutes during material substitution studies.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of ppm ↔ mg/m³ Conversion (Standard Temperature & Pressure)

This derivation is **frequently asked in KTU Part B questions** (14 marks).

### Starting Principles

The **molar volume** of an ideal gas at **25 °C (298.15 K) and 1 atm** is a physical constant:

$$
V_m = 24.45 \;\; \text{L/mol}
$$

This is derived from the ideal gas equation:

$$
PV = nRT
$$

Solving for $V/n$:

$$
\frac{V}{n} = \frac{RT}{P}
$$

### Step-by-Step Numerical Derivation

**Step 1** — Substitute values for $T$ and $P$:
$R = 0.082057 \;\; \text{L·atm/(mol·K)}$, $T = 298.15 \;\; \text{K}$, $P = 1 \;\; \text{atm}$.

$$
\frac{V}{n} = \frac{(0.082057)(298.15)}{1} = 24.45 \;\; \text{L/mol}
$$

**Step 2** — Express concentration. By definition, **1 ppm** means 1 part by volume per million parts of air:

$$
1 \;\; \text{ppm} = \frac{1 \;\; \text{mole of substance}}{10^6 \;\; \text{moles of air}}
$$

**Step 3** — Convert ppm to molar fraction. For 1 m³ (= 1000 L) of air at NTP conditions:

$$
\text{Moles of air in 1 m}^{3} = \frac{1000 \;\; \text{L}}{24.45 \;\; \text{L/mol}} = 40.90 \;\; \text{mol}
$$

**Step 4** — For a concentration of $C_{ppm}$ ppm, the moles of contaminant per m³ of air:

$$
n_{\text{contaminant}} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times 40.90}{10^{6}} \;\; \text{mol}
$$

**Step 5** — Convert moles to mass using molecular weight $MW$ (in g/mol):

$$
\text{Mass per m}^{3} = n_{\text{contaminant}} \times MW \times 10^{3} \;\; \text{mg}
$$

**Step 6** — Final conversion formula:

$$
C_{mg/m^{3}} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}
$$

The reverse conversion follows algebraically:

$$
C_{ppm} = \frac{C_{mg/m^{3}} \times 24.45}{MW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorization Tip for KTU Students:** Just remember the constant **24.45**. For air-quality calculations in Indian conditions (often cited at 25 °C, 1 atm), this is the universally accepted value.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1 — Benzene Exposure Assessment

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** The TLV-TWA for benzene is 0.5 ppm. Calculate the equivalent concentration in **mg/m³** at 25 °C and 1 atm. Given: Molecular weight of benzene (C₆H₆) = **78 g/mol**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1** — Identify the relevant formula:

$$
C_{mg/m^{3}} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute the values:

$$
C_{mg/m^{3}} = \frac{0.5 \times 78}{24.45}
$$

**Step 3** — Compute the numerator:

$$
0.5 \times 78 = 39
$$

**Step 4** — Divide by 24.45:

$$
C_{mg/m^{3}} = \frac{39}{24.45} = 1.595 \;\; \text{mg/m}^{3}
$$

**Step 5** — Final rounded result:

$$
\boxed{C_{benzene} \approx 1.60 \;\; \text{mg/m}^{3}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** If workplace benzene concentration exceeds **1.60 mg/m³ (8-hour TWA)**, engineering controls (ventilation, enclosure) and respiratory PPE must be deployed.

### Example 2 — Sulphur Dioxide Threshold Comparison

**Problem:** The OSHA PEL for SO₂ is **5 mg/m³**. Express this in **ppm**. Given: Molecular weight of SO₂ = **64 g/mol**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1** — Apply the inverse conversion formula:

$$
C_{ppm} = \frac{C_{mg/m^{3}} \times 24.45}{MW}
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute the values:

$$
C_{ppm} = \frac{5 \times 24.45}{64}
$$

**Step 3** — Numerator:

$$
5 \times 24.45 = 122.25
$$

**Step 4** — Final computation:

$$
C_{ppm} = \frac{122.25}{64} = 1.91 \;\; \text{ppm}
$$

**Step 5** — Final rounded result:

$$
\boxed{C_{SO_2} \approx 1.91 \;\; \text{ppm}}
$$

### Example 3 — LD₅₀ Calculation via Arithmetic Mean of Log-Doses

**Problem:** In an acute oral toxicity study on rats, 100% mortality was observed at 1000 mg/kg, and 0% mortality at 100 mg/kg. Calculate the LD₅₀ using the moving average method.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1** — Take base-10 logarithms of both doses:

$$
\log D_a = \log(1000) = 3.0
$$

$$
\log D_b = \log(100) = 2.0
$$

**Step 2** — Apply the formula:

$$
LD_{50} = \log^{-1}\!\left[\frac{\log D_a + \log D_b}{2}\right]
$$

**Step 3** — Average the log-doses:

$$
\frac{3.0 + 2.0}{2} = \frac{5.0}{2} = 2.5
$$

**Step 4** — Take the antilog:

$$
LD_{50} = \log^{-1}(2.5) = 10^{2.5}
$$

**Step 5** — Final numerical result:

$$
\boxed{LD_{50} \approx 316.23 \;\; \text{mg/kg}}
$$

### Example 4 — Dose Calculation for a Worker

**Problem:** A 70-kg worker is exposed to 250 mg of a solvent through accidental skin absorption. Compute the dose in **mg/kg**.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1** — Apply the dose formula:

$$
D = \frac{M}{W_b}
$$

**Step 2** — Substitute:

$$
D = \frac{250 \;\; \text{mg}}{70 \;\; \text{kg}}
$$

**Step 3** — Final result:

$$
\boxed{D \approx 3.57 \;\; \text{mg/kg}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting Appropriate Engineering Controls from SDS

| SDS Section Consulted | Engineering Control Selected | Reasoning |
|----------------------|-------------------------------|-----------|
| Section 9 — Flash point | Use explosion-proof electrical fittings | If flash point < 38 °C, classify as Class IA flammable |
| Section 8 — TLV/PEL | Install local exhaust ventilation (LEV) | When measured air concentration > TLV |
| Section 10 — Incompatibilities | Segregate storage from oxidizers | Prevent violent reactions (e.g., acids + bases) |
| Section 6 — Spill procedures | Provide spill kits with absorbents | Specified containment material in SDS |
| Section 4 — First-aid measures | Install emergency eyewash + safety shower | Within 10 seconds of hazard zone |
| Section 11 — LD₅₀/LC₅₀ | Mandate appropriate PPE class | High toxicity = supplied-air respirator |

## 3.4 Comparative Table — Types of Workplace Exposure Limits

| Standard | Agency | Legal Status | Update Frequency | Notation Used |
|----------|--------|--------------|------------------|---------------|
| **TLV-TWA** | ACGIH (USA) | Advisory | Annual | 8-hour time-weighted average |
| **PEL** | OSHA (USA) | Legal (US Federal) | Less frequent | 8-hour TWA |
| **REL** | NIOSH (USA) | Recommended | Updated periodically | 10-hour TWA (up to 40 h/week) |
| **OEL** | EU / National | Legal in member states | Variable | 8-hour TWA |
| **MAK** | DFG (Germany) | Legal in Germany | Annual | 8-hour TWA |
| **WEL** | HSE (UK) | Legal in UK | Periodic | 8-hour TWA |
| **IDLH** | NIOSH (USA) | For emergency planning | — | Immediate danger threshold |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian Context:** India follows the **Factories Act 1948** and the **Model Rules under Schedule II** which references ACGIH-USA TLVs. The **Indian Standard IS 1446** also classifies hazardous chemicals. The **Public Liability Insurance Act 1991** and the **Manufacture Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules 1989** govern industrial chemical safety in India.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define LD₅₀ and LC₅₀. State the WHO toxicity classification based on oral LD₅₀ in rats.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**LD₅₀ (Lethal Dose 50):** The statistically derived single dose of a substance that is expected to cause death in **50% of the test population** (typically rats or mice) when administered by a specific route (oral, dermal, subcutaneous) over a defined period. Expressed in **mg/kg body weight**.

**LC₅₀ (Lethal Concentration 50):** The concentration of a substance in air (or water) that is expected to cause death in **50% of the test population** during a specified exposure time (usually 4 hours). Expressed in **mg/m³** or **ppm**.

**WHO Toxicity Classification based on Oral LD₅₀ in Rats (1 mark):**

| Class | Oral LD₅₀ (mg/kg) |
|-------|-------------------|
| Class Ia — Extremely Hazardous | $\leq 5$ |
| Class Ib — Highly Hazardous | $5 - 50$ |
| Class II — Moderately Hazardous | $50 - 2000$ |
| Class III — Slightly Hazardous | $> 2000$ |

---

### Question 2: List any six sections of a Safety Data Sheet (SDS) and briefly state the content of two of them.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 16-section GHS-aligned SDS contains:

1. Identification
2. Hazard Identification
3. Composition / Ingredients
4. First-Aid Measures
5. Fire-Fighting Measures
6. Accidental Release Measures
7. Handling and Storage
8. Exposure Controls and PPE
9. Physical and Chemical Properties
10. Stability and Reactivity
11. Toxicological Information
12. Ecological Information
13. Disposal Considerations
14. Transport Information
15. Regulatory Information
16. Other Information

**Two Sections Expanded:**

- **Section 11 — Toxicological Information:** Provides LD₅₀, LC₅₀ values, routes of exposure, symptoms of exposure, carcinogenicity, mutagenicity, and reproductive toxicity data.
- **Section 8 — Exposure Controls / PPE:** Lists the Threshold Limit Values (TLV), Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL), recommended engineering controls (ventilation), and specifies Personal Protective Equipment (respirators, gloves, eye protection).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Students answer ONE full question of 14 marks from Module 3, with sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Choose **either A or B**.

---

### ⭐ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q(a):** Define Industrial Toxicology. Explain in detail the various **routes of entry** of toxic substances into the human body, with suitable industrial examples for each route.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**

Industrial Toxicology is the branch of toxicology that deals with the study of the nature, effects, detection, and control of poisons arising from industrial chemicals, raw materials, intermediates, products, and by-products that workers may be exposed to during manufacturing, processing, storage, transport, use, or disposal.

**Routes of Entry (5 marks, 1.25 marks each):**

**(i) Inhalation (Respiratory Route)** — Most common and most hazardous route. Gases, vapours, dusts, fumes, mists, and aerosols enter the respiratory system through the nose and mouth, reaching the alveolar membrane where rapid gas exchange occurs into the bloodstream.
*Examples:* Welding fumes (manganese, ozone), solvent vapours (benzene, toluene), silica dust, asbestos fibres, chlorine gas leaks.

**(ii) Skin Absorption (Dermal Route)** — Chemicals penetrate through the intact skin via the epidermis, sweat glands, or hair follicles. Lipid-soluble substances penetrate more readily than water-soluble ones.
*Examples:* Absorption of phenol through bare hands, exposure to aniline dyes in textile industry, penetration of organic solvents (DMF, carbon tetrachloride) through gloves.

**(iii) Ingestion (Oral Route)** — Chemicals enter via the mouth through contaminated hands, food, drinks, or cigarettes kept in the work area. They are absorbed through the gastrointestinal tract.
*Examples:* Lead dust ingestion in battery manufacturing, pesticide residue on food, accidental swallowing of kerosene.

**(iv) Injection (Parenteral Route)** — Direct entry through cuts, abrasions, puncture wounds, or splinters. Although rare, this route bypasses the skin barrier and delivers the toxicant directly into systemic circulation.
*Examples:* Needlestick injuries in healthcare, contaminated metal splinters in machining, hypodermic injuries with chemicals.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q(b):** Explain the **dose-response relationship** in toxicology. Derive the conversion formula between **ppm and mg/m³** at 25 °C and 1 atm, and compute the TLV of acetone (TLV = 250 ppm) in mg/m³. Given: MW of acetone = 58 g/mol.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**

**Dose-Response Relationship (3 marks):**

The dose-response relationship describes how the magnitude of a biological effect in an organism depends on the dose of a toxicant administered. As the dose increases, the response (proportion of population affected or severity of effect) increases monotonically. When plotted on a semi-log scale (response vs. $\log_{10}$ dose), the curve is **sigmoidal (S-shaped)**.

Key concepts include:
- **Threshold dose** — minimum dose producing a measurable effect.
- **NOAEL** — No-Observed-Adverse-Effect Level.
- **LOAEL** — Lowest-Observed-Adverse-Effect Level.
- **LD₅₀** — dose producing 50% mortality in the test population.

Paracelsus's principle states: *"Only the dose makes the poison"* — every chemical has a threshold below which it is harmless and above which it becomes toxic.

**Conversion Formula Derivation (2.5 marks):**

Starting from the ideal gas law, $PV = nRT$:

$$
\frac{V}{n} = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{(0.082057)(298.15)}{1} = 24.45 \;\; \text{L/mol}
$$

For 1 ppm = 1 mole of contaminant per $10^6$ moles of air, the moles of contaminant in 1 m³ of air is:

$$
n = \frac{C_{ppm}}{10^{6}} \times \frac{1000}{24.45}
$$

The mass in mg is $m = n \times MW \times 10^{3}$. Combining:

$$
\boxed{C_{mg/m^{3}} = \frac{C_{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}}
$$

**Numerical Computation (1.5 marks):**

For acetone:

$$
C_{mg/m^{3}} = \frac{250 \times 58}{24.45} = \frac{14500}{24.45} \approx 593.05 \;\; \text{mg/m}^{3}
$$

$$\boxed{C_{acetone} \approx 593 \;\; \text{mg/m}^{3}}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q(a):** What is a **Safety Data Sheet (SDS)**? Describe the GHS pictograms used for hazard communication, with examples for at least six pictograms.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**

**SDS Definition (1 mark):**

A Safety Data Sheet (SDS), formerly called Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS), is a standardized 16-section document that provides comprehensive information on the hazards, composition, handling, storage, emergency procedures, and toxicological properties of a chemical. It is mandated under the Globally Harmonized System (GHS) of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals adopted by OSHA (USA), EU (CLP), and India.

**GHS Pictograms (6 marks, 0.5 mark pictogram + 0.5 mark example each):**

| Pictogram Code | Symbol Description | Hazard Class | Example Chemical |
|----------------|--------------------|--------------|----------|
| **GHS01** | Exploding bomb | Explosives, self-reactives, organic peroxides | TNT, ammonium perchlorate |
| **GHS02** | Flame | Flammable liquids, solids, gases, aerosols | Acetone, petrol, propane |
| **GHS03** | Flame over circle | Oxidizing gases, liquids, solids | Hydrogen peroxide, potassium permanganate |
| **GHS04** | Gas cylinder | Gases under pressure (compressed, liquefied, dissolved) | Oxygen cylinders, LPG, nitrogen |
| **GHS05** | Corrosion (test tubes + surfaces) | Corrosive to metals, skin corrosion, eye damage | Sulphuric acid, sodium hydroxide |
| **GHS06** | Skull & crossbones | Acute toxicity (fatal/toxic categories 1–3) | Hydrogen cyanide, methanol |
| **GHS07** | Exclamation mark | Irritant, skin sensitizer, narcotic effects | Isopropanol, ethanol |
| **GHS08** | Health hazard (silhouette) | Carcinogen, mutagen, reproductive toxin, respiratory sensitizer | Benzene, asbestos, formaldehyde |
| **GHS09** | Environment (fish + tree) | Hazardous to aquatic environment, ozone layer | Mercury, polychlorinated biphenyls |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q(b):** Discuss the **classification of toxicants** based on (i) site of action, (ii) duration of exposure, and (iii) effect produced. Provide two examples for each category.

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Module 3]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Classification Based on Site of Action (2.5 marks):**

| Category | Definition | Example 1 | Example 2 |
|----------|------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Local Toxicants** | Action confined to the site of contact | Sulphuric acid burns skin | Chlorine gas irritates respiratory tract |
| **Systemic Toxicants** | Require absorption and distribution to remote organs | Lead → CNS & kidneys | Benzene → bone marrow |
| **Hepatotoxicants** | Damage liver | Carbon tetrachloride (CCl₄) | Vinyl chloride |
| **Nephrotoxicants** | Damage kidneys | Cadmium | Mercury |
| **Neurotoxicants** | Damage nervous system | n-Hexane | Manganese |
| **Pulmonotoxicants** | Damage lungs | Silica (silicosis) | Asbestos (asbestosis) |
| **Hematotoxicants** | Damage blood | Carbon monoxide (binds Hb) | Benzene (aplastic anemia) |

**(ii) Classification Based on Duration of Exposure (2.5 marks):**

- **Acute Toxicity** — Adverse effects appearing within 24 hours of a single or short-term exposure.
  - *Example:* Acute chlorine inhalation causes pulmonary oedema; acute arsenic poisoning causes vomiting and diarrhoea.

- **Sub-acute Toxicity** — Adverse effects from repeated doses over 28–90 days.
  - *Example:* Repeated low-dose toluene exposure causes CNS depression.

- **Chronic Toxicity** — Adverse effects from prolonged exposure (>3 months, often at low doses).
  - *Example:* Chronic benzene exposure → leukemia; chronic silica exposure → silicosis.

**(iii) Classification Based on Effect Produced (2 marks):**

- **Carcinogens** — Induce cancer. *Examples:* Benzene (leukemia), vinyl chloride (angiosarcoma).
- **Mutagens** — Cause genetic mutations. *Examples:* Ionizing radiation, formaldehyde.
- **Teratogens** — Cause fetal malformations. *Examples:* Thalidomide, methyl mercury.
- **Allergens / Sensitizers** — Cause immune-mediated hypersensitivity. *Examples:* Chromium (contact dermatitis), isocyanates (asthma).
- **Asphyxiants** — Depriving tissues of oxygen. *Examples:* Carbon monoxide, hydrogen cyanide.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Conflating TLV, PEL, and REL** — These are issued by **different agencies** (ACGIH, OSHA, NIOSH) with **different legal statuses**. Specifying the source earns full credit.
> 2. **Missing the molecular weight** in ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversions — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if $MW$ is not stated.
> 3. **Memorizing pictograms as "danger symbols"** without naming the **GHS code (GHS01–GHS09)** — both are required.
> 4. **Omitting the time basis** (8-hour TWA, STEL, ceiling) when quoting exposure limits — the 8-hr TWA must be stated.
> 5. **Not mentioning Indian regulations** (Factories Act 1948, Manufacture Storage & Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules 1989) where relevant — examiners reward context-specific answers.
> 6. **Confusing "Hazard" with "Risk"** — Hazard is intrinsic to the substance; Risk is a function of hazard × exposure.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Industrial Toxicology & MSDS**

### 📌 Definitions to Memorize
- ☐ **Toxicology** — Study of adverse effects of chemicals on living organisms.
- ☐ **Industrial Toxicology** — Toxicology applied to workplace chemicals.
- ☐ **Toxicant vs. Hazard vs. Risk** — Substance vs. intrinsic property vs. probability × consequence.
- ☐ **MSDS / SDS** — 16-section document under GHS for hazard communication.
- ☐ **Paracelsus Principle** — "Only the dose makes the poison."

### 📌 Key Metrics
- ☐ **LD₅₀** in **mg/kg body weight**
- ☐ **LC₅₀** in **mg/m³ or ppm** (usually 4-hr inhalation)
- ☐ **TLV-TWA** (ACGIH, 8-hr, advisory)
- ☐ **PEL** (OSHA, 8-hr, legal US)
- ☐ **NOAEL / LOAEL** — toxicity benchmarks

### 📌 Conversion Formula (MUST memorize)
- ☐ $C_{mg/m^{3}} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times MW}{24.45}$ at 25 °C, 1 atm
