* $S$ = Severity (1 = negligible, 10 = catastrophic without warning)
* $O$ = Occurrence (1 = very rare, 10 = very high failure rate)
* $D$ = Detection (1 = almost certain detection, 10 = cannot be detected)

**Boundary Conditions:**

$$
RPN_{min} = 1 \times 1 \times 1 = 1 \qquad ; \qquad RPN_{max} = 10 \times 10 \times 10 = 1000
$$

**Interpretation Bands:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Low Priority} &: RPN < 50 \\
\text{Moderate Priority} &: 50 \leq RPN < 200 \\
\text{High Priority} &: 200 \leq RPN < 500 \\
\text{Critical Priority} &: RPN \geq 500
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern):** A centrifugal pump in a chemical plant suffers from seal leakage.
>
> * Severity of chemical burn injury: $S = 8$
> * Occurrence of seal failure per year: $O = 6$
> * Detection difficulty (no online monitoring): $D = 7$
>
> Therefore:
>
> $$
> RPN = 8 \times 6 \times 7 = 336
> $$
>
> Since $200 \leq 336 < 500$, this is classified as **High Priority** — the engineer must immediately install a seal-pot monitoring system and a secondary containment bund.

### 3.3 Derivative Step 3 — Individual Risk per Annum (for Map-Based Land-Use Planning)

When the Map Method is used for **quantitative land-use planning** (e.g., for a hazardous chemical storage terminal near a residential colony), the *Individual Risk per Annum* is computed as:

$$
IR = f_i \cdot P_{f \mid i}
$$

Where:
* $f_i$ = frequency of incident $i$ (per year)
* $P_{f \mid i}$ = conditional probability of fatality given incident $i$

For multiple incident scenarios, total individual risk at a location is the **sum of contributions**:

$$
IR_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i \cdot P_{f \mid i}
$$

> **Regulatory Benchmarks (HSE, UK / Dutch QRA Standard):**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> IR_{limit,\,worker} &= 10^{-4} \text{ per year (1 in 10,000)} \\
> IR_{limit,\,public} &= 10^{-5} \text{ per year (1 in 100,000)} \\
> IR_{limit,\,sensitive} &= 10^{-6} \text{ per year (1 in 1,000,000)}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> Any location on the Map exceeding these contours must be excluded from the planned land use (e.g., schools, hospitals).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Map vs Tabular

| Selection Criterion | Map Method Wins | Tabular Method Wins |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Data is geographic / spatial | ✓ | ✗ |
| Need to communicate to non-technical audience | ✓ | ✗ |
| Process is highly procedural with many steps | ✗ | ✓ |
| Need to capture multi-cause / multi-consequence logic | ✗ | ✓ |
| Regulatory requirement (PSM, ISO 12100) | ✗ | ✓ |
| Emergency response planning | ✓ | ✗ |
| Quantitative FMEA / RPN ranking | ✗ | ✓ |
| Construction site walk-through audit | ✓ | ✗ |
| Complex process plant hazard review (HAZOP) | ✗ | ✓ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

All node IDs below are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `step1`, `nodeA`, `process1`), labels contain no markdown formatting, and reserved keywords are never used as standalone node names.

### 4.2 Workflow Diagram — The Map Method (6-Step Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start Risk Mapping Project]) --> step1[Step 1 Select Scaled Base Map]
    step1 --> step2[Step 2 Identify Risk Parameters to Plot]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3 Collect Spatial and Incident Data]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4 Apply Transparent Overlay Technique]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5 Apply Colour Coded Risk Legend]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6 Interpret and Communicate Findings]
    step6 --> endA([Risk Map Documented for Safety Committee])
```

### 4.3 Workflow Diagram — The Tabular Method (HAZOP/FMEA Pipeline)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start Tabular Analysis]) --> nodeA[Define Scope and System Boundary]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Decompose Process into Steps or Nodes]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Identify Deviations using Guide Words]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Determine Root Causes for Each Deviation]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Estimate Consequences and Severity]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Document Existing Safeguards]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Compute Risk Score or RPN]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Is Risk Tolerable?}
    nodeH -- No --> nodeI[Recommend Additional Controls Hierarchy]
    nodeI --> nodeH
    nodeH -- Yes --> endB([Tabular Risk Register Completed])
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Map + Tabular Hybrid (Industry Best Practice)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Site Layout Drawing] --> layer1
    input2[Incident History Database] --> layer1
    input3[Gas Detection Sensor Readings] --> layer1
    input4[Chemical Inventory Data] --> layer1
    layer1[Map Method Engine Spatial Overlay and Risk Zoning] --> output1[Coloured Risk Map]
    layer2[Process Flow Diagram P and ID] --> engine2
    engine2[Tabular Method Engine HAZOP FMEA Analysis] --> output2[Seven Column Risk Register]
    output1 --> report[Safety Committee Report]
    output2 --> report
    report --> action[Hierarchy of Controls Action Plan]
```

### 4.5 Schematic — Risk Matrix Colour Legend (Used in Map Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    low[Low Risk RS 1 to 7 GREEN ZONE Routine Controls Adequate] --> med[Medium Risk RS 8 to 14 YELLOW ZONE Engineering Controls Required]
    med --> high[High Risk RS 15 to 75 RED ZONE Immediate Action Substitution Elimination]
```

### 4.6 Schematic — Tabular Method 7-Column Logical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    col1[Column 1 Step ID] --> col2[Column 2 Element or Activity]
    col2 --> col3[Column 3 Deviation or Hazard]
    col3 --> col4[Column 4 Possible Causes]
    col4 --> col5[Column 5 Consequences]
    col5 --> col6[Column 6 Existing Safeguards]
    col6 --> col7[Column 7 Recommended Action]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Q1.a]**

> **Q1.a) Differentiate between the Map Method and the Tabular Method of risk analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Sl. No. | Aspect | Map Method | Tabular Method |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Visual Format | Geographic / spatial map with overlays | Row-column matrix / table |
| 2 | Data Type | Spatial, geographic, layout-based | Procedural, causal, descriptive |
| 3 | Best Suited For | Plant layout, escape routes, accident hot-spots | Process steps, equipment failures, HAZOP |
| 4 | Output Type | Coloured risk map | Tabulated risk register with RPN |
| 5 | Audience | Non-technical workforce, emergency responders | Engineers, safety auditors |
| 6 | Primary Strength | Pattern recognition at a glance | Logical completeness and traceability |

*(1 mark for each correct distinguishing point × 3 = 3 marks)*

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Q1.b]**

> **Q1.b) State the formula used for Risk Score calculation in the Map Method and explain its three components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
RS = P \times S \times F
$$

* **P – Probability (1–5):** Likelihood that the hazardous event will occur during the worker's exposure.
* **S – Severity (1–5):** Magnitude of harm, ranging from negligible injury to fatality.
* **F – Frequency of Exposure (1–3):** How often the worker comes into contact with the hazard (rare, occasional, continuous).

RS is then classified as Low (≤7, Green), Medium (8–14, Yellow), or High (≥15, Red).

*(1 mark for formula + 1 mark for any two components explained + 1 mark for classification = 3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module 4 Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper, Module 4, Q.7 Choice A (14 Marks)]**

**Question A: (a) Explain the Map Method of risk analysis in detail with a suitable industrial example. (7 marks)**
**(b) A chemical plant section has the following hazard parameters at three locations. Calculate the Risk Score (RS) and assign the appropriate risk zone. (7 marks)**

| Location | Probability (P) | Severity (S) | Frequency (F) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| L-1 (Solvent storage) | 4 | 5 | 3 |
| L-2 (Office block) | 1 | 2 | 1 |
| L-3 (Loading bay) | 3 | 4 | 2 |

---

### **Model Solution – Question A**

**(a) Map Method Explanation (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Map Method is a graphical risk-analysis technique that uses scaled layouts with colour-coded overlays to spatially represent hazards, accidents, or risk zones.

**Six-step Procedure (4 marks):**

1. *Select the base map:* Choose a scaled plant layout (e.g., 1:200) including roads, equipment, escape routes.
2. *Identify risk parameters:* Decide whether to plot hazard zones, incident history, gas dispersion contours, or noise isopleths.
3. *Collect spatial data:* Use incident logs, walk-through surveys, sensor logs.
4. *Apply overlay technique:* Transparent overlays (or CAD/GIS layers) – one per parameter.
5. *Apply colour legend:* Green / Yellow / Red risk zones based on Risk Score.
6. *Interpret & communicate:* Discuss in safety committee meetings; integrate with emergency preparedness plans.

**Industrial Example (2 marks):** *In an LPG bottling plant*, the Map Method was used to overlay historical cylinder-leak incidents (red dots), wind-rose dispersion contours, and fire-hydrant locations. The overlay revealed that 80 % of leaks occurred within 5 m of the filling bay — leading to the redesign of the filling bay with remote-operated valves and a 10-m exclusion zone.

---

**(b) Risk Score Calculation (7 marks):**

Using the formula:

$$
RS = P \times S \times F
$$

**Location L-1 (Solvent Storage):**

$$
RS_{L1} = 4 \times 5 \times 3 = 60
$$

Since $RS = 60 \geq 15$, classify as **High Risk → RED ZONE.** Immediate substitution with less flammable solvent and installation of flame arrestors recommended.

**Location L-2 (Office Block):**

$$
RS_{L2} = 1 \times 2 \times 1 = 2
$$

Since $RS = 2 \leq 7$, classify as **Low Risk → GREEN ZONE.** Routine safety signage adequate.

**Location L-3 (Loading Bay):**

$$
RS_{L3} = 3 \times 4 \times 2 = 24
$$

Since $RS = 24 \geq 15$, classify as **High Risk → RED ZONE.** Engineering controls such as over-fill prevention interlocks and bund walls must be installed.

**Final Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Location | RS | Risk Zone | Control Priority |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| L-1 | 60 | RED | Immediate — Substitution + Engineering |
| L-2 | 2 | GREEN | Routine — Signage only |
| L-3 | 24 | RED | Engineering + Administrative controls |

---

> **[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Paper, Module 4, Q.7 Choice B (14 Marks)]**

**Question B: (a) Describe the Tabular Method of risk analysis with its standard 7-column template. (7 marks)**
**(b) For the reactor charging step of a batch chemical process, prepare a Tabular Analysis listing at least three deviations, their causes, consequences, and recommended controls. (7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution – Question B**

**(a) Tabular Method Description (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The Tabular Method analyses a process step-by-step in a structured matrix, listing hazards, causes, consequences, and controls for each element of the system.

**Purpose and When to Use (1 mark):** Used for procedural, equipment-intensive systems where every step must be examined for deviation. Used in HAZOP, FMEA, JSA.

**Standard 7-Column Template (3 marks):**

1. *Column 1 — Step/Node ID:* Sequential identifier (S-01, S-02 …).
2. *Column 2 — Element/Activity:* Description of process step.
3. *Column 3 — Deviation/Hazard:* What can go wrong (using HAZOP guide words: NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN).
4. *Column 4 — Possible Causes:* Root causes — equipment failure, human error, external events.
5. *Column 5 — Consequences:* Worst credible outcome.
6. *Column 6 — Existing Safeguards:* Current engineering and administrative controls.
7. *Column 7 — Recommended Action:* Additional controls per Hierarchy of Controls.

**Advantages (1 mark):** Logical completeness, traceability, suitable for documentation, easy to update, supports RPN calculation.

**Limitations (1 mark):** Time-consuming for large systems; relies on team expertise; may miss spatial interactions (hence the Map Method is used as a complement).

---

**(b) Tabular Analysis — Reactor Charging Step (7 marks):**

**Scenario (1 mark):** A 5,000-litre glass-lined batch reactor is being charged with 3,000 L of toluene and 800 kg of solid catalyst from a hopper.

**Completed Tabular Risk Register (6 marks):**

| Step ID | Activity | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Existing Safeguards | Recommended Action |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| S-01 | Charge toluene from road tanker | LESS toluene charged (under-fill) | Tanker flow meter faulty; operator misreads gauge | Insufficient solvent → catalyst concentration rises → exothermic runaway | Local level gauge on reactor | Install mass-flow meter + low-level alarm |
| S-02 | Charge toluene | MORE toluene charged (over-fill) | Inlet valve stuck open; pump runs continuously | Reactor overflow → vapour cloud → fire/explosion | Bund wall (110% capacity) | Add high-level interlock + automatic shut-off valve |
| S-03 | Charge solid catalyst via hopper | NO catalyst flow (blockage) | Hopper bridging, moisture caking, screw conveyor jammed | Reaction stalls → batch rejected; or delayed initiation causes stratification on restart | Manual poking rod; hammer the hopper from outside | Install vibratory hopper + load-cell weighing + SOP for restart |
| S-04 | Charge catalyst | AS WELL AS foreign object in catalyst | Unsealed bags, metal scoop contamination | Catalyst poisoning + risk of spark in flammable vapour | Sieve at hopper inlet | X-ray screening + dedicated non-sparking tools |

**Interpretation (included in marks):** The Tabular Method reveals that all four major deviations can lead to either runaway reaction or process failure, justifying the installation of **two independent layers of protection (per IEC 61511 SIL-2 requirement)** on the toluene inlet line.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the two methods:** Writing "Map Method uses a table" or "Tabular Method uses overlays" — this *immediately* loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Risk Score formula:** Many students describe the Map Method without writing $RS = P \times S \times F$. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping the colour legend:** Examiners expect the **Green / Yellow / Red zoning scheme**. Omitting it loses the application marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning guide words:** For the Tabular Method, HAZOP guide words (NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN) are *expected terminology*. Without them, the answer is incomplete.
> 5. **Writing columns but not filling them:** Drawing the 7-column template without populating it with a worked example is considered *theoretical only* — KTU awards full marks only when both template and content are present.
> 6. **Mixing up RPN scaling:** Stating RPN maximum as 75 (Map Method scale) instead of **1000** (FMEA 1–10 scale) is a frequent mistake.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] The **Map Method** is *graphical & spatial* — based on a scaled layout with overlays and colour-coded risk zones.
- [ ] The **Tabular Method** is *logical & matrix-based* — based on step-by-step decomposition and a 7-column standard template.
- [ ] The Map Method formula is $RS = P \times S \times F$ with **P(1–5), S(1–5), F(1–3)** — maximum RS = **75**.
- [ ] Risk Zone classification: **Green (≤7), Yellow (8–14), Red (≥15)**.
- [ ] The Tabular Method is the framework for **HAZOP, FMEA, FMEA-RPN, JSA, and Checklist Analysis**.
- [ ] HAZOP guide words must be memorised: **NO, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN**.
- [ ] FMEA's **RPN = S × O × D** on a 1–10 scale, with RPN<sub>max</sub> = **1000**.
- [ ] The Hierarchy of Controls ordering: **Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → PPE** (always quote it correctly).
- [ ] Both methods are *complementary*, not alternatives — best practice is Map first, then Tabular.
- [ ] Land-use planning IR limits: workers **10⁻⁴**, public **10⁻⁵**, sensitive receptors **10⁻⁶** per year (HSE, UK).
- [ ] Regulatory frameworks that mandate these methods: **OSHA 1910.119, ISO 12100, IEC 61511, ISO 45001 Clause 6.1.2, Indian Factories Act Section 7A**.
- [ ] When asked for a *table* in KTU exams, always include **all 7 columns** and **at least 3 populated rows**.
- [ ] When asked for a *map*, always include **scale, north arrow, legend, and the three-colour risk zones**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fault Tree Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET832 - OCCUPATIONAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fault Tree Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is a systematic, top-down, deductive analytical technique used in safety and reliability engineering. It graphically represents the logical combinations of various sub-events (failures, errors, or conditions) that can lead to a single, undesired top event (system failure, accident, or hazard). It was originally developed in **1962** by **H.A. Watson** of Bell Telephone Laboratories for the U.S. Air Force's Minuteman missile launch control system, and was later formalized under the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) and adopted in standards such as **NUREG-0492** and **IEC 61025**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Fault Tree Analysis is a **deductive** (top-down) analysis technique, as opposed to Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA), which is **inductive** (bottom-up). This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question in KTU examinations.

The fundamental building blocks of a fault tree are:

- **Top Event:** The single, undesirable event at the apex of the tree (e.g., "Boiler Explosion," "Worker Fatality," "Plant Shutdown").
- **Intermediate Event:** A failure event that results from the logical combination of lower-level events.
- **Basic Event:** A leaf node representing a primary, independent failure (no further development).
- **Undeveloped Event:** A basic event with insufficient information or a deliberately omitted secondary failure.
- **Logic Gates:** Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT, XOR, INHIBIT, etc.) that connect events.

> [!NOTE]
> **Events vs. Gates — A Critical Distinction:** In a fault tree, **rectangles** denote *events* (states of the system), while the connecting logic symbols (such as AND/OR) are called **gates**. A circle is a basic event, a diamond is an undeveloped event, and a house (pentagon) is a switch/house event.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective Working Backwards" Model

Imagine a **murder mystery**. The detective (analyst) is presented with the *top event* — the crime itself ("Mr. X was found dead"). The detective then asks, "What sequence of circumstances **must have happened** for this murder to occur?" — and the **OR gate** says: *either it was a gunshot, a stabbing, a poisoning, OR an accident.* If it was a gunshot (intermediate event), the **AND gate** says: *the gun must have been loaded AND pointed at the victim AND the trigger pulled.* Each of these is a *basic event* — a primary cause that cannot be broken down further.

Similarly, a **safety engineer** starts with a disaster (e.g., "Toxic Gas Release") and works **backwards** through logical AND/OR relationships to identify the minimal combinations of component failures (root causes) that *could* produce the disaster. This is exactly what FTA does — it is **deductive reverse-engineering of failure**.

## 1.3 Key Standardized Symbols in FTA (Per IEC 61025 / NUREG-0492)

| Symbol | Name | Meaning |
|:------:|:----:|:--------|
| Rectangle | Event Event | A failure event (intermediate/top) |
| Circle | Basic Event | A primary component failure (leaf node) |
| Diamond | Undeveloped Event | Deliberately not further analyzed |
| House | House Event | A conditional/switch event |
| Trapezoid | Transfer | Logic transferred to/from another tree page |
| AND Gate | Logical AND | All inputs must occur simultaneously |
| OR Gate | Logical OR | Any one input must occur |

> [!NOTE]
> **International Standards Governing FTA:** **IEC 61025 (Fault Tree Analysis)** and **NUREG-0492** are the two most widely cited references. **MIL-HDBK-338B** is also used for military/aerospace systems. Students should be able to name at least one of these for full marks in definition questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Philosophy of FTA

FTA is not a single calculation — it is a **structured workflow**. The breakdown of its operational logic is as follows:

1. **Define the Top Event** (Boundary Definition): The first and most critical step. The analyst must precisely define the *undesirable event* in unambiguous terms (e.g., "Loss of containment of ≥100 L of benzene at the storage tank T-101"). A poorly defined top event invalidates the entire analysis.
2. **Establish the System Boundary**: Define what is *inside* the system under analysis and what is *outside* (e.g., external power grid, human operators). Boundary conditions are critical for repeatable analysis.
3. **Construct the Fault Tree (Top-Down Deduction)**: Starting from the top event, ask the question: *"What immediate conditions or sub-failures, individually or in combination, could cause this top event?"* Connect them with AND/OR logic gates. Repeat recursively for every intermediate event until only basic events remain.
4. **Solve the Tree (Boolean Algebra)**: Convert the tree into a logical Boolean expression (sum of products / product of sums) and algebraically simplify it.
5. **Identify Minimal Cut Sets (MCS)**: A **Cut Set** is a combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence produces the top event. A **Minimal Cut Set** is a cut set that cannot be reduced further (removing any one event breaks the failure path). MCS are the *root causes* of system failure.
6. **Quantify Probabilities (Quantitative Analysis)**: If failure probability data (λ) is available for each basic event, calculate the *unavailability* (Q) of the system for a given mission time.
7. **Prioritize and Recommend Mitigation**: Order the MCS by probability/likelihood and propose safety barriers, redundancies, or procedural changes to break the cut sets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — "Why FTA is Powerful":** FTA allows qualitative ranking (which cut sets dominate?) AND quantitative calculation (what is the probability of disaster?). The minimal cut sets of size 1 (single-point failures) are the most dangerous and must be eliminated first through redundancy.

## 2.2 Boolean Logic Foundations for FTA

The entire quantitative power of FTA rests on Boolean algebra. The two fundamental gates are:

### 2.2.1 The AND Gate (Logical Intersection)

The top event occurs **only if ALL** input events occur simultaneously.

$$E_{top} = A \cdot B \cdot C \cdot \ldots \cdot N$$

For independent basic events with probabilities $P_i$, the probability of the AND-gate output is:

$$P_{AND} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i = P_A \times P_B \times P_C \times \ldots \times P_N$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for AND gates:** An AND gate represents a *coincidence of independent failures* — typically used for **redundant system failures** (e.g., both the primary pump AND the backup pump fail). The probability of coincidence drops rapidly with each added component.

### 2.2.2 The OR Gate (Logical Union)

The top event occurs if **ANY ONE OR MORE** input events occur.

$$E_{top} = A + B + C + \ldots + N$$

For *small probabilities* (rare events) with independence, the rare-event approximation is:

$$P_{OR} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i = P_A + P_B + P_C + \ldots + P_N$$

The exact (non-approximated) form for two events is:

$$P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A \cap B)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for OR gates:** An OR gate represents *any one of several alternative failure paths* — typically used for **series systems** or multiple independent causes of a failure. Even though individual probabilities are small, summing many small probabilities can yield a substantial total.

### 2.2.3 Other Gates Briefly

- **NOT Gate (Inverter):** Output is the logical complement. Used for *success* events.
- **XOR (Exclusive OR):** Exactly one input must occur (not both). Used for *k-out-of-n* logic.
- **INHIBIT Gate:** Output occurs if input occurs *and* a conditional event is true.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Boolean Expression | Application / Context |
|:--------|:----------------------------|:----------------------|
| AND Gate Probability | $P_{AND} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Redundant system failure (coincidence) |
| OR Gate Probability (rare-event approx.) | $P_{OR} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Series system / alternative paths |
| OR Gate Probability (exact, two events) | $P(A \cup B) = P_A + P_B - (P_A \cdot P_B)$ | When $P_A$ and $P_B$ are not negligibly small |
| Boolean AND | $A \cdot B$ | Coincidence / redundancy |
| Boolean OR | $A + B$ | Alternative paths / series failures |
| Unavailability (constant failure rate) | $Q(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \approx \lambda t$ | Component failure probability over mission time $t$ |
| Minimal Cut Set Definition | A cut set whose removal of any single event eliminates the failure path | Used for qualitative risk ranking |
| Demorgan's Law | $\overline{A \cdot B} = \overline{A} + \overline{B}$ | Used in solving Boolean expressions in fault trees |
| Importance Measure (Birnbaum) | $I_i = \frac{\partial Q_{system}}{\partial q_i}$ | Ranks which basic event most impacts system failure |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall in KTU Exams:** Do **NOT** confuse FTA's **AND/OR logic** with **Series/Parallel reliability logic**. In reliability block diagrams, *series* = AND-like logic, but in fault trees, the mapping is exactly inverted in terms of analyst intent. Many students lose marks by mixing these two conventions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

FTA is mandated in the design and risk assessment of:

- **Nuclear Power Plants** (reactor scram system reliability, core damage frequency).
- **Aerospace Systems** (rocket launch, satellite deployment failures).
- **Chemical Process Industries** (LOPA + FTA combined for SIL determination in IEC 61511).
- **Offshore Oil & Gas Platforms** (Blowout Preventer reliability).
- **Automotive Functional Safety** (ISO 26262 — fault trees for ASIL determination).
- **Medical Device Design** (IEC 62304 / ISO 14971).

In a **KTU answer**, citing even one industry application can earn 1–2 additional marks in long-answer questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Boolean Algebra Simplification of a Fault Tree

**Problem:** A simple fault tree has the following top-level logic:

$$T = A \cdot (B + C)$$

where $A$, $B$, $C$ are basic events. Determine the **minimal cut sets** and the **probability** of the top event if $P(A) = 0.01$, $P(B) = 0.02$, and $P(C) = 0.03$, assuming all events are independent.

### Step 1: Write the Boolean Expression in Standard Form

The top event $T$ is already given in canonical **Sum-of-Products (SOP)** form:

$$T = A \cdot (B + C)$$

### Step 2: Apply the Distributive Law to Expand

The distributive law of Boolean algebra states that $X \cdot (Y + Z) = (X \cdot Y) + (X \cdot Z)$. Applying this:

$$T = A \cdot B + A \cdot C$$

### Step 3: Identify the Cut Sets

Each product term $(A \cdot B)$ and $(A \cdot C)$ represents a **cut set** — a combination of basic events that, if all occur, produces the top event.

The cut sets are: $\{A, B\}$ and $\{A, C\}$.

### Step 4: Verify Minimality

A cut set is **minimal** if removing any event makes it no longer a cut set. Here:

- Removing $A$ from $\{A, B\}$ leaves $\{B\}$ — but $B$ alone does not cause $T$ (since $T$ requires $A$).
- Removing $A$ from $\{A, C\}$ leaves $\{C\}$ — but $C$ alone does not cause $T$.
- Removing $B$ from $\{A, B\}$ leaves $\{A\}$ — but $A$ alone does not cause $T$ (since $T$ requires both $A$ AND $(B+C)$).

Therefore, both cut sets are **minimal**. The minimal cut sets are:

$$MCS_1 = \{A, B\} \quad \text{and} \quad MCS_2 = \{A, C\}$$

### Step 5: Compute the Probability of the Top Event

The exact probability (using inclusion-exclusion) is:

$$P(T) = P(A \cap B) + P(A \cap C) - P(A \cap B \cap C)$$

Since all events are independent, $P(X \cap Y) = P(X) \cdot P(Y)$. Substituting values:

$$P(T) = (0.01 \times 0.02) + (0.01 \times 0.03) - (0.01 \times 0.02 \times 0.03)$$

$$P(T) = 0.0002 + 0.0003 - 0.000000006$$

$$\boxed{P(T) = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} - 6.0 \times 10^{-9} \approx 5.0 \times 10^{-4}}$$

**Intuition:** The probability of the top event is **less than** the sum of individual AND-gate outputs because the two cut sets share the common event $A$ (so we over-counted $A \cap B \cap C$ once). For rare events, the triple intersection is negligible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rare-Event Approximation Check:** For this case, $P(T) \approx 5 \times 10^{-4}$. The exact and approximate values differ by only $6 \times 10^{-9}$ — completely negligible. This is why the **rare-event approximation** $P_{OR} \approx \sum P_i$ is widely used in KTU-level FTA problems.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Two-Stage System with AND/OR Combination

**Problem:** A critical industrial system has the following fault tree:

- **Top Event T:** "Loss of Cooling" = $G_1 + G_2$ (OR gate)
  - $G_1$ (AND): "Primary Pump Fails ($A$)" AND "Backup Pump Fails ($B$)"
  - $G_2$ (AND): "Valve Closed ($C$)" AND "Power Lost ($D$)"

Where: $P(A) = 0.01$, $P(B) = 0.01$, $P(C) = 0.05$, $P(D) = 0.02$.

### Step 1: Boolean Expansion

The complete Boolean expression is:

$$T = (A \cdot B) + (C \cdot D)$$

### Step 2: Identify Minimal Cut Sets

$$MCS_1 = \{A, B\} \quad \text{and} \quad MCS_2 = \{C, D\}$$

Both are size-2 (second-order) cut sets, which means the system requires *two* independent component failures to fail. This indicates **good redundancy** — a single component failure cannot cause the top event.

### Step 3: Compute the Probability of Each Cut Set

For $MCS_1$:

$$P(MCS_1) = P(A) \cdot P(B) = 0.01 \times 0.01 = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

For $MCS_2$:

$$P(MCS_2) = P(C) \cdot P(D) = 0.05 \times 0.02 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 4: Apply the OR-Gate Combination (Exact)

Since the cut sets are disjoint in their constituent events (they share no common basic event), the probability of their intersection is effectively zero *if* all events are independent. However, in the exact formula:

$$P(T) = P(MCS_1) + P(MCS_2) - P(MCS_1 \cap MCS_2)$$

$$P(MCS_1 \cap MCS_2) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C) \cdot P(D) = 0.01 \times 0.01 \times 0.05 \times 0.02$$

$$P(MCS_1 \cap MCS_2) = 1.0 \times 10^{-7}$$

### Step 5: Final Calculation

$$P(T) = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-3} - 1.0 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\boxed{P(T) \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

### Step 6: Engineering Interpretation

The cut set $\{C, D\}$ contributes **90.9%** of the total system failure probability:

$$\text{Contribution of } MCS_2 = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 90.9\%$$

**Actionable Insight:** The safety engineer should focus mitigation efforts on the **valve** (C) and **power supply** (D) — for example, installing a backup valve position indicator and an uninterruptible power supply (UPS) for the valve actuator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip — Show All Steps:** Even when the question asks "find the probability," always write: (1) the Boolean expression, (2) the minimal cut sets, (3) the probability per MCS, and (4) the final sum. Examiners reward **methodology** over just the final number.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Boolean Simplification Using De Morgan's Law

**Problem:** A fault tree yields the Boolean expression:

$$T = (A + B) \cdot (C + D)$$

Simplify this expression and find all minimal cut sets.

### Step 1: Apply the Distributive Law

The distributive law of Boolean algebra is identical in form to its counterpart in standard algebra:

$$T = (A \cdot C) + (A \cdot D) + (B \cdot C) + (B \cdot D)$$

### Step 2: Identify All Cut Sets

The cut sets derived from the expansion are:

$$\{A, C\}, \quad \{A, D\}, \quad \{B, C\}, \quad \{B, D\}$$

### Step 3: Verify Minimality

Each of these four cut sets consists of *two* basic events. Removing any one event from any of these makes the resulting single event insufficient to cause the top event. Hence **all four are minimal cut sets**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Recognition for KTU:** The expression $T = (A+B) \cdot (C+D)$ represents a **parallel-of-series** system, which always yields $n \times m$ minimal cut sets when expanded. The expression $T = (A \cdot B) + (C \cdot D)$ represents a **series-of-parallel** system, yielding the OR of AND-terms.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Architecture — The FTA Workflow Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of an FTA study, showing the inter-relationship of all phases from system definition to mitigation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Define Top Event and System Boundary"] --> node2["Identify Immediate Causes via Deductive Analysis"]
    node2 --> node3["Apply Boolean Logic Gates: AND OR NOT XOR"]
    node3 --> node4["Develop Each Intermediate Event Recursively"]
    node4 --> node5["Reach Basic Event Leaf Nodes"]
    node5 --> node6["Convert Tree to Boolean Expression"]
    node6 --> node7["Algebraic Simplification using Boolean Laws"]
    node7 --> node8["Identify Minimal Cut Sets MCS"]
    node8 --> node9["Qualitative Analysis: Rank MCS by Order"]
    node8 --> node10["Quantitative Analysis: Compute Probabilities"]
    node9 --> node11["Prioritize Mitigation Strategies"]
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12["Document Fault Tree with Standards IEC 61025"]
```

## 4.2 Fault Tree Structure — Visual Representation of a Worked Example

The following diagram represents the **"Loss of Cooling"** fault tree from Worked Example 2 in Section 3.2. It shows a top event connected through an OR gate to two AND gates, each of which contains two basic event circles.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeTop["TOP EVENT T: Loss of Cooling"]
    nodeOR{"OR GATE"}
    nodeA["Basic Event A: Primary Pump Fails"]
    nodeB["Basic Event B: Backup Pump Fails"]
    nodeC["Basic Event C: Valve Closed"]
    nodeD["Basic Event D: Power Lost"]
    nodeAND1{"AND GATE"}
    nodeAND2{"AND GATE"}

    nodeTop --> nodeOR
    nodeOR --> nodeAND1
    nodeOR --> nodeAND2
    nodeAND1 --> nodeA
    nodeAND1 --> nodeB
    nodeAND2 --> nodeC
    nodeAND2 --> nodeD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The top event T is realized if EITHER (Primary Pump Fails AND Backup Pump Fails) OR (Valve Closed AND Power Lost) occur. Note the two *paths through the tree* — each path is a minimal cut set of size 2.

## 4.3 Boolean Algebra Simplification Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the analytical process used in Worked Example 1, mapping how a raw tree is transformed into actionable minimal cut sets.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    rawExpr["Raw Tree Boolean Expression: T = A times B plus C"]
    distLaw["Apply Distributive Law: X times Y plus Z equals XY plus XZ"]
    expanded["Expanded SOP Form: T = AB plus AC"]
    mcsID["Identify MCS: Cut Set 1 equals A and B"]
    mcsID2["Identify MCS: Cut Set 2 equals A and C"]
    probCalc["Compute Probabilities: P_AB and P_AC"]
    rareApprox["Rare-Event Approximation or Exact Inclusion-Exclusion"]
    finalP["Final P_T"]

    rawExpr --> distLaw --> expanded --> mcsID
    expanded --> mcsID2
    mcsID --> probCalc
    mcsID2 --> probCalc
    probCalc --> rareApprox --> finalP
```

## 4.4 Event vs. Gate Symbol Legend (Reference Card)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    legendRect["RECTANGLE: Event (intermediate or top)"]
    legendCirc["CIRCLE: Basic Event (primary component failure)"]
    legendDia["DIAMOND: Undeveloped Event (not further analyzed)"]
    legendHouse["HOUSE: Conditional or Switch Event"]
    legendAnd["AND GATE: All inputs must occur"]
    legendOr["OR GATE: Any one input causes output"]
    legendTrapezoid["TRAPEZOID: Transfer symbol between pages"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Diagrams:** Fault tree diagrams carry **high visual impact** in KTU answer sheets. Even a hand-drawn tree with proper gate symbols, basic event circles, and clear labels can earn **2 to 3 extra marks** in 14-mark questions, because the examiner can instantly verify the student's structural understanding of the system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define Fault Tree Analysis. Mention any two standard symbols used in fault tree construction with their meanings.**

**Model Answer:**

Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) is a **deductive, top-down analytical technique** used in safety and reliability engineering. It begins with an undesired top event (such as an accident or system failure) and traces backwards through Boolean logic gates (AND, OR) to identify all combinations of lower-level events (basic component failures) that could cause the top event. It was originally developed at Bell Labs in **1962** for the U.S. Air Force Minuteman missile project and is standardized under **IEC 61025** and **NUREG-0492**.

**Two Standard Symbols:**

1. **Rectangle:** Represents an *event* (top event or intermediate event) that results from the logical combination of lower-level events. The top event is shown as a rectangle at the apex of the tree.
2. **Circle:** Represents a *basic event* — a primary, independent component failure or condition that is not developed further. Circles form the leaf nodes of the tree.

> [!NOTE]
> *For the third mark, mentioning the OR/AND gate symbol, the diamond (undeveloped event), or citing IEC 61025 also earns full credit.*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between a Cut Set and a Minimal Cut Set in FTA.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Cut Set | Minimal Cut Set |
|:-------|:--------|:----------------|
| Definition | A set of basic events whose **simultaneous** occurrence causes the top event. | A cut set from which **no single basic event can be removed** without losing the property of causing the top event. |
| Size | Can be of any order, including non-minimal. | Smallest possible combination (often size 1, 2, or 3). |
| Example | $\{A, B, C\}$ causing top event $T$. | $\{A, B\}$ if $A$ alone is insufficient, $B$ alone is insufficient, but $A$ AND $B$ causes $T$. |
| Engineering Use | Used as an intermediate step. | Used for **prioritizing safety improvements**. |

> [!NOTE]
> *Minimal Cut Sets are the primary deliverable of a qualitative FTA. They are sorted by size — smaller MCS (especially size-1 single-point failures) are the most critical and must be eliminated first through design redundancy.*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]

**a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction methodology of a Fault Tree Analysis. List the standard symbols used in fault trees and state the **two key Boolean rules** used in evaluating AND/OR gates. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A chemical reactor cooling system has the following fault tree:

- **Top Event T:** "Reactor Overheat" = $G_1 \cdot G_2$ (AND gate)
  - $G_1$ (OR): "Pump A fails" ($A$) OR "Pump B fails" ($B$)
  - $G_2$ (OR): "Coolant valve stuck" ($C$) OR "Heat exchanger fouled" ($D$)

Given $P(A) = 0.02$, $P(B) = 0.03$, $P(C) = 0.04$, $P(D) = 0.05$, find:
(i) The Boolean expression for $T$.
(ii) The minimal cut sets.
(iii) The probability of the top event, assuming independence and using the rare-event approximation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Construction Methodology (Steps):**

1. **Define the Top Event:** State the undesired event precisely in measurable terms (e.g., "Reactor temperature exceeds 500°C for >10 minutes"). This becomes the apex rectangle of the tree.
2. **Establish System Boundary and Assumptions:** Clearly list which components, operators, and external factors are within the scope of analysis.
3. **Identify Immediate Causes:** Working deductively, ask: *"What single events or combinations of events could directly cause the top event?"* Connect these via AND or OR gates to the top event.
4. **Develop Each Intermediate Event:** For every intermediate event, recursively repeat Step 3 until only basic events (circles) remain.
5. **Apply Boolean Logic:** The fully constructed tree is converted into a Boolean expression and algebraically simplified using laws such as $X + X = X$ (idempotency), $X \cdot (Y + Z) = X \cdot Y + X \cdot Z$ (distributive), and De Morgan's laws.
6. **Identify Minimal Cut Sets:** These are the smallest combinations of basic events causing the top event. They are obtained from the simplified Boolean expression.

**Standard Symbols (Mandatory List):**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|:------:|:--------|
| Rectangle | Event (top or intermediate) |
| Circle | Basic event (leaf) |
| Diamond | Undeveloped event |
| House | Switch/conditional event |
| AND Gate | All inputs must occur |
| OR Gate | Any one input causes output |

**Two Key Boolean Rules for AND/OR Gates:**

- **AND Gate:** $P(AND) = \prod P_i$ (output occurs if ALL inputs occur).
- **OR Gate:** $P(OR) \approx \sum P_i$ for rare events (output occurs if ANY input occurs).

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Write the Boolean Expression**

Given the structure:

$$T = G_1 \cdot G_2 = (A + B) \cdot (C + D)$$

**Step 2: Expand Using the Distributive Law**

Applying the distributive law $X \cdot (Y + Z) = X \cdot Y + X \cdot Z$ *twice* (first to $G_1$ and then expand $G_2$):

$$T = A \cdot C + A \cdot D + B \cdot C + B \cdot D$$

**Step 3: Identify the Minimal Cut Sets**

The expansion yields four size-2 cut sets, each consisting of two basic events. Since no event can be removed from any cut set without breaking the failure path, **all four are minimal**:

$$MCS_1 = \{A, C\}, \quad MCS_2 = \{A, D\}, \quad MCS_3 = \{B, C\}, \quad MCS_4 = \{B, D\}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Probability of Each MCS**

Using the AND-gate formula $P_{AND} = P_i \cdot P_j$ (independent events):

$$P(MCS_1) = 0.02 \times 0.04 = 8.0 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$P(MCS_2) = 0.02 \times 0.05 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P(MCS_3) = 0.03 \times 0.04 = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$P(MCS_4) = 0.03 \times 0.05 = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5: Compute the Top Event Probability (OR of all MCS)**

Applying the OR-gate rare-event approximation $P_{OR} \approx \sum P_i$:

$$P(T) \approx 8.0 \times 10^{-4} + 1.0 \times 10^{-3} + 1.2 \times 10^{-3} + 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{P(T) \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The cut set $\{B, D\}$ contributes the most risk ($1.5 \times 10^{-3}$, about 33% of total). Mitigation should focus on Pump B and the heat exchanger.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]

**a)** Explain the **Boolean algebra approach** used in fault tree simplification. List any **four Boolean laws** with their symbolic form. **(7 marks)**

**b)** An industrial system has the Boolean fault tree expression:

$$T = (A + B) \cdot (A + C) \cdot (B + C)$$

Simplify this expression, identify all minimal cut sets, and compute the top event probability given $P(A) = 0.1$, $P(B) = 0.2$, $P(C) = 0.3$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Boolean Algebra Approach in Fault Tree Simplification:**

Once a fault tree is fully constructed, it is converted into a **logical Boolean expression** connecting basic events via AND ($\cdot$), OR ($+$), and NOT (overbar) operators. The objective of simplification is to obtain the **Sum-of-Products (SOP) form** of the expression, from which the **Minimal Cut Sets (MCS)** can be directly read off. Simplification removes redundant terms, shortens the analysis, and reveals the *true* root-cause combinations.

**Four Boolean Laws Used in FTA:**

| Law | Symbolic Form | Application in FTA |
|:----|:--------------|:-------------------|
| **Idempotent Law** | $X + X = X$ ; $X \cdot X = X$ | Removes duplicate basic events within a cut set. |
| **Distributive Law** | $X \cdot (Y + Z) = X \cdot Y + X \cdot Z$ | Expands AND-of-OR into OR-of-AND (SOP form). |
| **Absorption Law** | $X + X \cdot Y = X$ ; $X \cdot (X + Y) = X$ | Removes cut sets that are supersets of smaller ones. |
| **De Morgan's Law** | $\overline{X \cdot Y} = \overline{X} + \overline{Y}$ ; $\overline{X + Y} = \overline{X} \cdot \overline{Y}$ | Converts AND to OR (and vice versa) when complements are involved. |

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Step 1: Expand the Expression Using the Distributive Law**

The given expression is:

$$T = (A + B) \cdot (A + C) \cdot (B + C)$$

First, multiply the first two brackets:

$$(A + B) \cdot (A + C) = A \cdot A + A \cdot C + B \cdot A + B \cdot C$$

Applying the idempotent law $A \cdot A = A$:

$$= A + A \cdot C + A \cdot B + B \cdot C$$

Now factor $A$ from the first three terms:

$$= A \cdot (1 + C + B) + B \cdot C$$

Since $1 + X = 1$ in Boolean algebra (the OR with TRUE gives TRUE):

$$= A \cdot 1 + B \cdot C = A + B \cdot C$$

**Step 2: Multiply the Simplified Expression by the Third Bracket**

$$T = (A + B \cdot C) \cdot (B + C)$$

Expanding:

$$T = A \cdot B + A \cdot C + B \cdot C \cdot B + B \cdot C \cdot C$$

Applying the idempotent law $B \cdot B = B$ and $C \cdot C = C$:

$$T = A \cdot B + A \cdot C + B \cdot C + B \cdot C$$

Since $X + X = X$, the term $B \cdot C$ appears twice — they collapse into one:

$$\boxed{T = A \cdot B + A \cdot C + B \cdot C}$$

**Step 3: Identify the Minimal Cut Sets**

Each product term is a minimal cut set:

$$MCS_1 = \{A, B\}, \quad MCS_2 = \{A, C\}, \quad MCS_3 = \{B, C\}$$

All are size-2 cut sets. Note that no size-1 cut set exists — meaning there is no single-point failure that causes the top event. This indicates **built-in redundancy**.

**Step 4: Compute the Probability of Each MCS**

Using $P_{AND} = P_i \cdot P_j$ for independent events:

$$P(MCS_1) = 0.1 \times 0.2 = 0.02$$

$$P(MCS_2) = 0.1 \times 0.3 = 0.03$$

$$P(MCS_3) = 0.2 \times 0.3 = 0.06$$

**Step 5: Compute the Top Event Probability Using Inclusion-Exclusion**

For three cut sets, the exact formula is:

$$P(T) = \sum P(MCS_i) - \sum P(MCS_i \cap MCS_j) + P(MCS_1 \cap MCS_2 \cap MCS_3)$$

Since all basic events are independent:

$$P(MCS_1 \cap MCS_2) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C) = 0.1 \times 0.2 \times 0.3 = 0.006$$

By symmetry, all pairwise intersections equal $0.006$.

$$P(MCS_1 \cap MCS_2 \cap MCS_3) = 0.006 \text{ (same as above, since the three-way intersection equals the pairwise product for the same three events)}$$

Substituting:

$$P(T) = (0.02 + 0.03 + 0.06) - (0.006 + 0.006 + 0.006) + 0.006$$

$$P(T) = 0.11 - 0.018 + 0.006 = 0.098$$

$$\boxed{P(T) = 0.098}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification using Boolean Algebra Complement Method:** The complement of the top event is $P(\overline{T}) = P(\overline{A \cdot B + A \cdot C + B \cdot C})$. Using De Morgan's law and simplifying, this gives the probability that *none* of the MCS occur. For the values above, $\overline{T}$ has probability $0.902$, so $P(T) = 1 - 0.902 = 0.098$. ✓ (Confirms the inclusion-exclusion result.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks in FTA Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing AND/OR in Fault Trees with Series/Parallel in Reliability Block Diagrams.** The mapping is inverted in terms of *interpretation*: a series reliability connection = AND in the fault tree; a parallel connection = OR. Drawing them wrongly costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the Independence Assumption.** If the question says "events are independent" but the student uses a correlated formula, the entire 7-mark calculation is invalidated.
> 3. **Skipping the MCS Identification Step.** Many students jump straight to probability calculation without listing the minimal cut sets explicitly. The MCS table carries **2–3 marks** in a 7-mark part.
> 4. **Applying Rare-Event Approximation Inappropriately.** When $P_i > 0.1$ (as in Question B above), the rare-event approximation $P_{OR} \approx \sum P_i$ is invalid. Students must use the **exact inclusion-exclusion formula**.
> 5. **Omitting Units or Numerical Magnitude Check.** A probability > 1.0 indicates a calculation error. Always verify $0 \le P \le 1$.
> 6. **Not Drawing the Fault Tree Diagram.** Even a schematic sketch with rectangles, circles, and gates can earn 2 easy marks. KTU examiners explicitly reward "neat sketch" sub-parts.
> 7. **Ignoring Standards Reference.** Mentioning **IEC 61025**, **NUREG-0492**, or **MIL-HDBK-338B** in a definition question instantly elevates the answer to a 3-mark full score.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FTA = Deductive, Top-Down Failure Analysis.** It works *backwards* from a top event to basic root causes. This is the opposite of FMEA (inductive, bottom-up).
- **Originator:** H.A. Watson, Bell Labs, **1962** (Minuteman missile). Standards: **IEC 61025** and **NUREG-0492**.
- **Top Event** is the apex rectangle — must be precisely defined in measurable terms.
- **Basic Events** (circles) are leaf nodes — primary component failures.
- **AND Gate** = all inputs must occur (coincidence / redundancy). Probability: $P_{AND} = \prod P_i$.
- **OR Gate** = any input causes output (alternative paths / series). Probability: $P_{OR} \approx \sum P_i$ (rare events) or exact inclusion-exclusion.
- **Minimal Cut Set (MCS)** is the smallest combination of basic events causing the top event. **Size-1 MCS = single-point failures = highest priority for elimination.**
- **Boolean Simplification** uses distributive, absorption, idempotent, and De Morgan's laws to obtain the Sum-of-Products (SOP) form.
- **Inclusion-Exclusion Formula** for multiple MCS: $P(T) = \sum P(MCS_i) - \sum P(MCS_i \cap MCS_j) + P(MCS_1 \cap \ldots \cap MCS_n)$.
- **Rare-Event Approximation** is valid only when $P_i \ll 1$ (typically $P_i < 0.01$). For larger $P_i$, use the exact formula.
- **Applications:** Nuclear, aerospace, chemical process, automotive (ISO 26262), medical devices, oil & gas.
- **Always draw the tree** for partial-credit recovery, label gates clearly, and state the **assumptions of independence and mission time**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEPET832 - OCCUPATIONAL SAFETY AND HEALTH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HAZOP Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# HAZOP Analysis — Module 4 | KTU 2024 Scheme

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hazard and Operability (HAZOP) Study** is a structured, systematic, and rigorous qualitative risk assessment technique used predominantly in **process industries** (chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, pharmaceutical, nuclear) to identify potential hazards, operability problems, and deviations from design intent that may lead to unsafe operations, equipment damage, environmental release, or loss of production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> *"HAZOP is a creative, systematic, and structured examination of a defined process or operation, conducted by a multidisciplinary team, which uses a set of guide words in combination with process parameters to discover how deviations from the design intent of the system can occur, and to determine whether these deviations can lead to hazards or operability problems."*

The technique was originally developed by the **Process Safety Group of Imperial Chemical Industries (ICI), United Kingdom, in the early 1970s**, and is now formalized under international standards:
- **IEC 61882** – HAZOP Studies – Application Guide
- **ISO 31000** – Risk Management (parent standard)
- **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (Process Safety Management) — mandates HAZOP for highly hazardous chemicals in the USA.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective on a Pipeline"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy (Plain English):**
> Imagine a long, complex water pipeline in your house. You suspect something *might* go wrong — a blockage, a leak, sudden pressure surge — but you don't know *where* or *how*. Instead of waiting for a burst, you hire a **team of experts** (a plumber, a pressure specialist, a chemical analyst). They walk through the pipeline section by section, asking structured questions like:
> *"What if there is **NO** flow here?"*
> *"What if there is **MORE** pressure than designed?"*
> *"What if the water contains **AS WELL AS** sand particles?"*
>
> Each question is a **HAZOP guide word + a process parameter**. By systematically asking these questions at every critical **node** (junction, valve, pump, tank), they uncover every possible way things can go wrong — **before** a real accident happens.
>
> **That is exactly what HAZOP does for an industrial plant.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Emphasis)

- **Standard Guide Words**: **7 guide words** (per IEC 61882) — *No/None, More, Less, As Well As, Part Of, Reverse, Other Than*.
- **Standard Process Parameters**: typically **8–10 parameters** — *Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, Time, Relief, Instrument Air, Utility*.
- **Team Size**: typically **5–8 members** (multi-disciplinary).
- **Node Definition**: a discrete section of the plant (e.g., one pump, one heat exchanger, one line section between two flanges).
- **Documentation Unit**: HAZOP Worksheet (one row per deviation-cause-consequence-safeguard-action).

### 1.4 Why HAZOP is Critical in Occupational Safety

HAZOP forms the **backbone of Process Safety Management (PSM)** in the **chemical, petrochemical, refinery, pharmaceutical, and fertilizer industries** — all of which employ significant numbers of KTU B.Tech Chemical Engineering graduates. It is a **mandatory regulatory requirement** in most developed nations and is increasingly being adopted in Indian PSM audits under the **Factories Act 1948 (Amended)**, the **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989)**, and the **PESO / OISD** guidelines.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** HAZOP questions almost always test **(i) guide words + meanings**, **(ii) team composition**, **(iii) step-by-step procedure**, and **(iv) a sample HAZOP worksheet row**. Memorize the 7 guide words in order with their meanings — this is the single highest-yield item in Module 4.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Pillars of HAZOP — Guide Words and Parameters

HAZOP is built on the **orthogonal combination** of two columns:

1. **Guide Words** — generic, abstract terms that describe *how* a deviation from design intent can occur.
2. **Process Parameters** — physical/chemical quantities that *what* is being deviated.

When a **Guide Word is applied to a Process Parameter**, it generates a meaningful **Deviation**. For example:
- Guide Word = `MORE` + Parameter = `PRESSURE` → Deviation = **"MORE PRESSURE"** (overpressure).
- Guide Word = `NO` + Parameter = `FLOW` → Deviation = **"NO FLOW"** (blockage / no delivery).

### 2.2 The Seven Standard Guide Words (Per IEC 61882)

| Guide Word | Meaning | Applied to "Flow" — Example Deviation |
|------------|---------|----------------------------------------|
| **NO / NONE** | Complete negation of the design intent | No flow → pump failure, blocked line |
| **MORE** | Quantitative increase | More flow → pump oversized, valve stuck open |
| **LESS** | Quantitative decrease | Less flow → partial blockage, leak |
| **AS WELL AS** | Additional activity / extra component present | Flow of liquid **as well as** vapour → two-phase flow, flashing |
| **PART OF** | Only a portion of the design intent achieved | Flow contains **part of** the required composition → contamination |
| **REVERSE** | Logical opposite of intent occurs | Reverse flow → check valve failure, backflow |
| **OTHER THAN** | Complete substitution / something else entirely | Other than water → wrong material, corrosion product |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Variant — Inerting / Start-up Guide Words:**
> Some organizations add extra guide words such as `SOONER THAN`, `LATER THAN`, `ELSEWHERE`, `BEFORE`, `AFTER`. These are **NOT part of the IEC 61882 standard** but are common in batch / procedural HAZOP. KTU students should know the **7 standard ones** by default.

### 2.3 Standard Process Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Causes of Deviation | Why it Matters |
|-----------|----------------------------|----------------|
| **Flow** | Pump failure, valve closure, line break | Loss of feed / product, overheating |
| **Pressure** | Thermal expansion, blocked vent, over-pumping | Rupture, leak, BLEVE |
| **Temperature** | Loss of cooling, exothermic runaway, fire | Decomposition, runaway reaction |
| **Level** | Loss of inflow, overflow, instrument failure | Pump cavitation, overflow, gas breakthrough |
| **Composition** | Wrong feed, contamination, side reaction | Off-spec product, toxic release |
| **Phase** | Vaporization of liquid, solidification | Pump damage, line blockage |
| **Time / Sequence** | Step done too early/late, missed step | Batch error, intermediate accumulation |
| **Instrument Air / Utility** | Air failure, power failure, cooling water off | Control valve fail, loss of agitation |
| **Mixing / Agitation** | Agitator off, poor mixing | Local hot spots, stratification |
| **Reaction / Conversion** | Catalyst loss, residence time low | Unreacted feedstock, runaway |

### 2.4 HAZOP Study Team — Composition & Roles

A HAZOP team is **multi-disciplinary and independent** of the design team. The standard composition (per IEC 61882):

| Role | Responsibility | Typical Background |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Team Leader (Facilitator)** | Guides the study, enforces methodology, ensures completeness, resolves disputes | Senior Process Safety Engineer, 5+ yrs HAZOP experience |
| **Scribe / Recorder** | Captures every discussion on the HAZOP worksheet in real time | Junior process / safety engineer |
| **Process Engineer** | Explains design intent, P&ID interpretation, process chemistry | Chemical engineer (often the *designer* of the plant) |
| **Operations / Plant Manager** | Real-world operating experience, start-up/shutdown issues | Plant operations shift manager |
| **Instrumentation / Control Engineer** | Identifies trips, interlocks, alarm logic | Instrumentation & Control engineer |
| **Mechanical / Rotating Equipment Engineer** | Pump, compressor, vessel integrity | Mechanical engineer |
| **Safety / HSE Specialist** | Identifies toxic/explosive consequences, applies standards | Safety officer |
| **Specialist (ad-hoc)** | Joined as needed — corrosion, structural, electrical, civil | Domain expert |
| **Maintenance Engineer** (optional) | Maintenance-related hazards, access, isolation | Maintenance supervisor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule:** The **Design Team must NOT lead the HAZOP**. An independent leader avoids design-bias. The design team *participates* as a member, but leadership remains with an independent HAZOP facilitator.

### 2.5 When is HAZOP Performed?

HAZOP is applied at multiple lifecycle stages, each with a different focus:

| Stage | Input Document | Focus |
|-------|----------------|-------|
| **Conceptual / Feasibility** | Block Flow Diagram, early PFD | Identify major hazards at high level |
| **Detailed Design (Front-End Engineering Design – FEED)** | P&IDs, PFDs, Equipment specs, Cause-&-Effect | Detailed deviation analysis, identify protective layers |
| **Construction / Pre-Start-Up** | As-built P&IDs, commissioning procedures | Validation against actual installation |
| **Operational (Periodic Re-validation)** | Updated P&IDs, MOC records, incident log | Review of changes since last HAZOP |
| **Decommissioning** | De-isolation procedures | Hazards of dismantling, residual chemicals |

### 2.6 Inputs (Information Needed) and Outputs (Deliverables)

**Inputs (HAZOP needs these documents):**
- Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (**P&IDs**) — *the master document*
- Process Flow Diagrams (**PFDs**)
- Material & Energy Balances
- Equipment datasheets, Plot plans
- Cause & Effect diagrams, Alarm & Trip schedules
- Operating procedures, Start-up/Shutdown logic
- Hazardous area classification drawings
- Previous incident logs, near-miss database

**Outputs (HAZOP produces these):**
- HAZOP Worksheet (the primary record)
- List of **Recommendations / Actions**
- Updated P&ID (with new safeguards, alarms, interlocks)
- Risk ranking (often via LOPA follow-up)
- Executive summary report

### 2.7 HAZOP vs. Other Risk Assessment Techniques

| Technique | Approach | Best Suited For | Difference from HAZOP |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|-----------------------|
| **HAZOP** | Guide words + Parameters at nodes | Continuous process plants (chemical, refinery) | Most detailed; team-based |
| **FMEA** (Failure Mode & Effects Analysis) | Bottom-up: failure of each component | Mechanical / equipment reliability | Component-level, not process deviation |
| **What-If** | Open-ended brainstorming | Early design, simple systems | Less structured than HAZOP |
| **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Top-down: Boolean logic of causes | Specific high-consequence events | Quantitative, single-event focused |
| **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** | Forward branching from initiating event | Consequence modelling | Frequency + consequence focused |
| **Checklist Analysis** | Yes/No against known items | Mature, well-understood plants | Not creative; misses unknowns |
| **Bow-Tie** | Threats → Top event → Consequences | Communication of major accident hazards | Shows barriers, not deviation finding |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** *"Distinguish between HAZOP and FMEA."* Memorize the row above — it is asked almost every year.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility of HAZOP

- **Mandatory** under OSHA PSM (USA), COMAH (UK), and is a strong **best-practice** requirement in Indian PSM audits.
- **Has prevented countless accidents.** Example: The **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984, Union Carbide India, Methyl Isocyanate)** could not have occurred if a HAZOP + LOPA + adequate safeguards had been performed — this is the **single most-cited case study** in Indian process safety textbooks.
- **Cost of HAZOP** is typically **<0.1% of plant capital cost**, but it saves **multiple times its cost** by avoiding downtime, incidents, and re-engineering.
- **Used in 90%+ of new process plant designs** worldwide in oil & gas, chemicals, pharma.

## 3. Step-by-Step HAZOP Procedure & Analytical Worksheet Walk-Through

### 3.1 The 10-Step HAZOP Methodology (Exhaustive, Board-Exam Ready)

This is the **canonical procedure** as per IEC 61882. KTU questions on "Explain the HAZOP procedure step-by-step" carry 7–14 marks, so memorize the order.

**Step 1 — Define the Scope and Objectives of the Study**
The Team Leader meets with the project sponsor and defines:
- The boundaries of the study (e.g., "from feed tank TK-101 to reactor outlet").
- The objectives (e.g., "identify safety, health, environmental, and operability issues").
- The deliverable format.
- The schedule, resources, and reporting structure.

**Step 2 — Assemble the HAZOP Team**
A multi-disciplinary team is formed (5–8 members). Independence of the team leader from the design team is ensured. Roles are assigned (Leader, Scribe, Members).

**Step 3 — Gather All Relevant Information (Information Pack)**
Collect P&IDs, PFDs, equipment datasheets, operating procedures, cause-&-effect diagrams, alarm/trip schedules, hazardous area classification, MSDS of all chemicals, previous incident logs, MOC (Management of Change) records, and relevant codes/standards. **No HAZOP session can begin without a complete Information Pack.**

**Step 4 — Divide the Plant into Nodes (Sectioning / Node Definition)**
The P&ID is divided into discrete **study nodes**. A node is a small section of the plant (a pump + its suction and discharge piping, or a heat exchanger, or a line section between two block valves). Each node should have a **single design intent**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Intent** = the *purpose* of the node (e.g., "Pump P-101 transfers 50 m³/h of toluene from Tank T-101 to Reactor R-201 at 4 bar g").

**Step 5 — Select the First Node and State its Design Intent**
The team picks a node and the Process Engineer verbally states the design intent clearly. The Scribe records it on the worksheet.

**Step 6 — Apply Guide Words to Process Parameters — Generate Deviations**
For each parameter (flow, pressure, temperature, level, etc.) in the node, the team applies the 7 guide words and asks:
- *"Can the flow be `NO`?"*
- *"Can the pressure be `MORE`?"*
- *"Can the temperature be `LESS`?"*
- … and so on.

If a deviation is **NOT credible** at this node, it is **dropped with a brief justification** (e.g., "MORE reverse flow" — not credible, one-way pump). If it **IS credible**, the team proceeds.

**Step 7 — Identify Causes for Each Credible Deviation**
For each credible deviation, the team brainstorms ALL possible causes. Examples for "NO FLOW from Pump P-101":
- Pump mechanical failure (impeller worn, shaft broken, seal leak).
- Suction line blocked / strainer clogged.
- Suction tank empty (NO LEVEL upstream).
- Discharge valve mistakenly closed.
- Power failure to motor.
- Vapor lock / cavitation (hot feed).
- Instrument air failure to control valve (FCV closes).
- Suction line air-locked.

**Step 8 — Determine Consequences (Safety, Health, Environmental, Operability)**
For each cause, the team identifies the consequences:
- *Safety*: Loss of containment, fire, explosion, toxic exposure, fatality.
- *Health*: Long-term exposure to hazardous substance.
- *Environmental*: Spill, atmospheric release, soil/water contamination.
- *Operability*: Production loss, equipment damage, off-spec product.
- *Reputation / Regulatory*: Penalty, shutdown order, public outcry.

**Step 9 — Identify Existing Safeguards**
The team lists ALL existing protective layers that prevent the cause or mitigate the consequence:
- Basic Process Control System (BPCS) — alarms, controllers.
- Safety Instrumented System (SIS) — interlocks, trips (e.g., ESD-1).
- Mechanical relief devices — PSV, rupture disc, vent.
- Operator intervention (procedural).
- Containment (bund, dyke, double-walled tank).
- Detection (gas detector, flame detector, level switch).
- Design margin (e.g., design pressure 1.1 × MAWP).
- Maintenance / inspection (e.g., scheduled pump rebuild).

**Step 10 — Recommend Actions and Record**
If the **risk is intolerable** after accounting for safeguards, the team issues **Recommendations / Actions**. These are typically:
- Add a new alarm (e.g., "Low flow alarm on P-101 discharge").
- Add an interlock (e.g., "Trip pump on high bearing temperature").
- Modify the design (e.g., "Add a check valve in the discharge line").
- Update the operating procedure (e.g., "Pre-start checklist for valve lineup").
- Add a relief device (e.g., "Install a PSV on the heat exchanger shell side").
- Specify a higher integrity component (e.g., "SIL-2 rated level transmitter").

Each **Recommendation has an Owner, a Target Date, and a Priority** (Action Tracking).

**Step 11 — Move to the Next Node / Parameter / Guide Word**
The team repeats Steps 5–10 for every node in the P&ID. The session typically covers **3–5 nodes per day** in a real workshop.

**Step 12 — Action Follow-Up & Report**
After the study, the Scribe compiles the **HAZOP Report** (executive summary + worksheets + action register). The actions are assigned to responsible persons, with deadlines. The team reconvenes to **close out** the actions.

### 3.2 Worked Example — HAZOP Worksheet Walk-Through (KTU Standard)

**Process:** A centrifugal pump **P-101** pumps toluene from atmospheric storage tank **T-101** to a reactor **R-201** through a control valve **FCV-101** and a flow indicator **FI-101**. Pump has a discharge pressure indicator **PI-101** and a local pressure gauge. There is a check valve **CV-101** downstream of the pump.

**Design Intent (Node 1: Pump P-101 and Discharge Line):**
*"Pump P-101 transfers 50 m³/h of toluene from T-101 to R-201 at a discharge pressure of 4.0 bar g. The line has a check valve to prevent reverse flow."*

Below is a **fully filled sample HAZOP worksheet** for this node (KTU examiners love this format):

| # | Deviation | Causes | Consequences | Existing Safeguards | Recommendations / Actions | Action By |
|---|-----------|--------|--------------|---------------------|----------------------------|-----------|
| 1 | **No Flow** from P-101 | (a) Pump failure (mechanical) (b) Suction strainer blocked (c) Air/vapor lock (hot toluene) (d) Discharge block valve closed (e) Loss of power to motor | Pump cavitation → damage. Loss of feed to R-201 → reactor upset, possible runaway if R-201 is exothermic | (a) Flow indicator FI-101 with low-low alarm at 30 m³/h (BPCS) (b) Pump suction has a foot valve (c) ESD-1 trips pump on power failure | (a) Install **low-flow trip** (SIS, SIL-2) at 20 m³/h to auto-stop pump (b) Add **temperature interlock** to prevent starting pump with hot toluene (c) Review PM schedule of strainer cleaning | Process Engr. (a) Inst. Engr. (b) |
| 2 | **Less Flow** from P-101 | (a) Partial strainer blockage (b) Pump wear (c) Cavitation due to low T-101 level (d) FCV-101 partially closed (controller tuning issue) | Reduced feed → R-201 conversion drops → unreacted toluene may accumulate → potential exotherm | (a) FI-101 with low alarm at 40 m³/h (b) Level transmitter LT-101 on T-101 with low alarm (c) Operator rounds every 2 hrs | (a) Install **cavitation detection** or **NPSH margin alarm** (b) Add **cascaded level control** of T-101 with pump speed | Process + Inst. Engr. |
| 3 | **More Flow** from P-101 | (a) Pump oversized (b) FCV-101 stuck open (controller failure) (c) Suction head too high (T-101 overfilled) | Excess toluene → R-201 flooded → runaway exotherm. Pipe vibration. | (a) FI-101 high alarm at 60 m³/h (b) Operator rounds | (a) Install **high-flow interlock** at 65 m³/h to close FCV-101 (b) Add **reactor level interlock** | Inst. Engr. |
| 4 | **No / Less Pressure** (Pump Discharge) | (a) Suction loss (b) Pump impeller worn (c) Line rupture downstream (leak) | Detection of leak by pressure drop. Potential environmental release of toluene. | (a) PI-101 low alarm at 2 bar g (b) Local gauge (c) Operator rounds (d) Dike around line | (a) Add **low-low pressure trip (SIL-2)** at 1.5 bar g to trip pump (b) **Gas detector** in pump house (LFL) | Inst. + Safety Engr. |
| 5 | **More Pressure** (Pump Discharge) | (a) Downstream block valve closed while pump running (b) Thermal expansion of trapped toluene (line blocked) (c) Pump dead-head | Pipe rupture, flange leak, possible fire | (a) PI-101 high alarm at 5 bar g (b) Pipe design pressure 10 bar g (c) Operator rounds | (a) Install **PSV (pressure safety valve)** set at 7 bar g on discharge line (b) Add **high-high pressure trip (SIL-2)** at 6 bar g | Process Engr. |
| 6 | **Reverse Flow** | (a) Check valve CV-101 fails (stuck open) (b) Pump stops while reactor pressure is higher | Toluene backflow → T-101 may overflow → flammable vapor release, fire risk. R-201 may be starved. | (a) Check valve CV-101 (single barrier) | (a) Install **second check valve in series** (defense-in-depth) OR (b) **Reverse rotation interlock** on pump motor | Process Engr. |
| 7 | **Other Than** (Wrong Material in Line) | (a) Maintenance error — wrong pipe spool installed (b) Cross-connection to another service | Toluene contaminated with other chemical → R-201 side reaction, potential runaway | (a) Line labeling (b) Material test certificates | (a) **Positive material identification (PMI)** at all spool joints (b) Lock-out-tag-out (LOTO) before maintenance | Mech. Engr. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The worksheet above is the **gold-standard answer** for a HAZOP question. In the exam, you only need to fill **2–3 rows** with causes, consequences, safeguards, and recommendations — but the **format and logic** must be exactly as shown.

### 3.3 Risk Ranking (Optional Add-On — LOPA Linkage)

After HAZOP identifies deviations, **Layers of Protection Analysis (LOPA)** is often performed to semi-quantitatively rank the risk. Each Independent Protection Layer (IPL) reduces the frequency of the unwanted consequence by an order of magnitude.

**Risk = Frequency of Cause × Probability of Failure of Safeguards × Consequence Severity**

While the math is not required for KTU 2024, the **concept of IPL hierarchy** is:

| Layer | Description | Example | PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand) |
|-------|-------------|---------|----------------------------------------|
| 1 | **Process Design** | Inherent safer design | Built-in |
| 2 | **BPCS** (Basic Process Control System) | Flow controller | ~10⁻¹ |
| 3 | **Alarm + Operator Action** | Operator responds to alarm | ~10⁻¹ |
| 4 | **SIS** (Safety Instrumented System) | SIL-1 / SIL-2 / SIL-3 trip | 10⁻¹ to 10⁻³ |
| 5 | **Mechanical Device** | PSV, Rupture disc | ~10⁻¹ to 10⁻² |
| 6 | **Physical Containment** | Bund, dyke, double wall | ~10⁻¹ to 10⁻² |
| 7 | **Emergency Response** | Fire brigade, evacuation | ~10⁻¹ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expectation:** You do NOT need to do LOPA math in the exam. You need to know the **concept** and be able to **list the IPL hierarchy**.

### 3.4 Limitations of HAZOP

1. **Highly resource-intensive** — requires a multi-day workshop with 5–8 senior engineers.
2. **Time-consuming** — typically 2–4 weeks for a medium plant.
3. **Quality depends on the team** — a weak or biased team produces weak output.
4. **Focuses on single deviations** — does not easily capture **multiple simultaneous failures** (use Bow-Tie or FTA for that).
5. **Not ideal for early conceptual stage** — needs P&IDs to be effective.
6. **Does not address ergonomic / human factors directly** — needs a complementary Human HAZOP or Human Factors analysis.
7. **Cannot identify hazards that are *not* expressible as a guide-word deviation** (e.g., a forklift accident on the site road). HAZOP is for **process** hazards.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 HAZOP Procedure — Top-Level Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Define Scope and Objectives] --> B[Step 2: Assemble HAZOP Team]
    B --> C[Step 3: Gather Information Pack - P and IDs, PFDs, Procedures]
    C --> D[Step 4: Divide Plant into Study Nodes]
    D --> E[Step 5: Select Node and State Design Intent]
    E --> F[Step 6: Apply Guide Words to Parameters]
    F --> G{Deviation Credible?}
    G -- No --> H[Drop with Justification] --> F
    G -- Yes --> I[Step 7: Identify Causes]
    I --> J[Step 8: Determine Consequences - Safety, Health, Env, Operability]
    J --> K[Step 9: Identify Existing Safeguards - BPCS, SIS, PSV, Containment]
    K --> L{Risk Tolerable?}
    L -- Yes --> M[Document in Worksheet - No Action Needed]
    L -- No --> N[Step 10: Recommend Actions - New Alarm, Interlock, PSV, Procedure]
    M --> O{All Parameters Covered for this Node?}
    N --> O
    O -- No --> F
    O -- Yes --> P{More Nodes to Study?}
    P -- Yes --> E
    P -- No --> Q[Step 11: Compile HAZOP Report and Action Register]
    Q --> R[Step 12: Action Follow-up and Close-out]
```

### 4.2 HAZOP Team Structure — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Sponsor / Project Manager] --> TL[HAZOP Team Leader - Independent Facilitator]
    TL --> SC[Scribe - Records on Worksheet]
    TL --> PE[Process Engineer - Design Intent]
    TL --> OP[Operations / Plant Manager - Field Experience]
    TL --> IE[Instrumentation and Control Engineer]
    TL --> ME[Mechanical / Rotating Equipment Engineer]
    TL --> SE[Safety / HSE Specialist]
    TL --> SP2[Specialist - Corrosion / Electrical / Civil - Ad Hoc]
    TL --> MN[Maintenance Engineer - Optional]
```

### 4.3 HAZOP Output Flow — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Information Pack - P and IDs, PFDs, Procedures, MSDS] --> HZ[HAZOP Workshop Sessions]
    HZ --> WS[HAZOP Worksheets - Per Node]
    WS --> AR[Action Register - Recommendations with Owners and Deadlines]
    AR --> EX[HAZOP Executive Report]
    EX --> MGMT[Management Review and Approval]
    MGMT --> IMPL[Implementation - Design Change, New Alarm, New Interlock]
    IMPL --> CLOSE[Action Close-out - Verification by Team Leader]
    CLOSE --> LOPA[Optional: LOPA Study - Semi-Quantitative Risk Ranking]
    LOPA --> SRS[Safety Requirement Specification for SIS]
```

```mermaid
graph LR

```

### 4.5 HAZOP vs FMEA — Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HAZOP_Block [HAZOP]
        H1[Top-Down / Process-Deviation Based] --> H2[Uses Guide Words plus Parameters]
        H2 --> H3[Team-Based - 5 to 8 Members]
        H3 --> H4[Best for Continuous Process Plants]
    end
    subgraph FMEA_Block [FMEA]
        F1[Bottom-Up / Component-Failure Based] --> F2[Identifies Failure Modes of Each Component]
```
